topics.title: Electrical Conductivity: Classical Free Electron Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrical Conductivity: Classical Free Electron Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classical Free Electron Theory (Drude–Lorentz Model, 1900):** A phenomenological model proposed by Paul Drude (1900) and extended by Hendrik Lorentz that treats the valence electrons in a metal as a **classical ideal gas** of free particles (Drude's "electron gas") confined within a fixed lattice of positive ions. The electrons move randomly, collide with the stationary ion cores, and respond to externally applied electric (E) and magnetic (B) fields between collisions, thereby explaining **electrical conduction, thermal conduction, the Hall effect, and the Wiedemann–Franz law** in metals.

The theory rests on three postulates (KTU high-yield):

1. **Free Electron Approximation:** Between collisions, the interaction between a given free electron and other electrons, and between an electron and the ion cores, is **neglected**. The ions are treated as a rigid, uniform, positively charged background.
2. **Independent Electron Approximation (Mean Free Time τ):** Each electron has a well-defined, statistically-independent **relaxation time** $\tau$ — the average time between two successive collisions. The probability of a collision in an infinitesimal interval $dt$ is $dt/\tau$.
3. **Maxwell–Boltzmann Statistics:** After every collision, the electron emerges with a velocity that is **randomly directed** and whose magnitude follows the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution appropriate to the local temperature (thermal equilibrium assumption).

> [!NOTE]
> **Drude applied the kinetic theory of gases (Maxwell–Boltzmann) to electrons in a metal**, treating the conduction electrons exactly like molecules of a classical gas bouncing inside a container — the "container" being the metal block.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **billiard hall full of tiny, super-fast moving balls (electrons) bouncing off each other and off massive, immovable pillars (positive ion cores)**. Normally, the balls whiz around in every direction equally — there is no net motion in any direction, so no current flows.

Now turn on a **gust of wind (the applied electric field E)** that gently pushes every ball in the same direction. The balls still bounce randomly, but each "free flight" between bounces gets a tiny extra push from the wind. Over many collisions, the random velocities average out, leaving a **small, slow, steady drift in the wind's direction** — this drift *is* the electric current. The faster the wind (higher E), the larger the drift, which is exactly **Ohm's Law**.

The "average time between bounces" is the **relaxation time $\tau$**, and the "average distance an electron travels between bounces" is the **mean free path $\lambda$**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bold, KTU-Standard Values)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Free electron rest mass | **$9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg** |
| $e$ | Elementary charge magnitude | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $n$ | Free electron number density (typical metal, e.g. Cu) | $\sim 10^{28}$–$10^{29}$ m$^{-3}$ |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J K$^{-1}$** |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro number | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$** |
| $v_{th}$ | Thermal (RMS) speed of electron at 300 K | $\sim 10^{5}$ m s$^{-1}$ |
| $\tau$ | Mean free / relaxation time | $\sim 10^{-14}$–$10^{-15}$ s |
| $\lambda$ | Mean free path | $\sim 10^{-9}$–$10^{-8}$ m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Postulates Decoded — The "Why" and the "How"

**Postulate 1 — Free electrons in a uniform positive background (the Jelly Model):**
The Coulomb interaction between any two free electrons is **completely ignored**. This is the most drastic — and ultimately *wrong* — simplification. Physically, however, it lets us treat each electron as an independent projectile moving in straight lines between collisions, like molecules in an ideal gas.
- *Why it is useful:* Allows us to write Newton's second law in its simplest form, $m\vec{a} = \vec{F}_{ext}$, between collisions.
- *Engineering utility:* Provides a closed-form expression for **DC and AC electrical conductivity**, essential for designing resistors, interconnects, and transmission lines in microelectronics.

**Postulate 2 — Existence of a mean collision time $\tau$:**
Each electron is assumed to undergo an instantaneous, randomizing collision after an *average* time $\tau$. The probability of *surviving* a collision in time $t$ is $P(t) = e^{-t/\tau}$.
- *Why it is useful:* Provides a single, experimentally measurable parameter that summarises the entire (complicated) electron–phonon and electron–impurity scattering physics of the real metal.
- *Engineering utility:* $\tau$ directly sets the **frequency response** of a conductor (skin effect, AC resistance).

**Postulate 3 — Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics after each collision:**
The electron "forgets" its pre-collision velocity. The post-collision velocity distribution is the **Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution** at the local lattice temperature $T$. This is the source of *thermal* energy in the electron gas.
- *Why it is useful:* Connects electrical conduction to thermal conduction via the *same* electrons carrying both charge and heat — leading naturally to the **Wiedemann–Franz law**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential** and appear in 90% of the Numerical / Derivation questions of GAPHT121 Module 1.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Drift velocity magnitude | $v_d = \dfrac{eE\tau}{m}$ | m s$^{-1}$ | $v_d \ll v_{th}$ in normal conductors |
| 2 | Current density | $J = nev_d = ne^2\tau E / m$ | A m$^{-2}$ | Vector: $\vec{J} = ne^2\tau\vec{E}/m$ |
| 3 | **DC Electrical conductivity** | $\sigma = \dfrac{ne^2\tau}{m} = ne\mu$ | S m$^{-1}$ (or $\Omega^{-1}$ m$^{-1}$) | The central result of Drude |
| 4 | **Resistivity** | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} = \dfrac{m}{ne^2\tau}$ | $\Omega$ m | $\rho \propto 1/\tau$ — scattering-controlled |
| 5 | Electron mobility | $\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E} = \dfrac{e\tau}{m}$ | m$^2$ V$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ | $\sigma = ne\mu$ |
| 6 | Mean free path | $\lambda = v_{th}\,\tau$ | m | $v_{th} = \sqrt{3k_BT/m}$ |
| 7 | Thermal (RMS) speed | $v_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_BT}{m}}$ | m s$^{-1}$ | From 1D M–B distribution: $\langle v^2 \rangle = 3k_BT/m$ |
| 8 | Specific heat per electron (classical) | $C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}k_B$ | J K$^{-1}$ | From equipartition, *fails* experimentally (Sommerfeld fix) |
| 9 | **Thermal conductivity** (Drude) | $K = \dfrac{1}{3}n\,v_{th}^{\,2}\,\tau\,C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}\dfrac{nk_B^{\,2}T\tau}{m}$ | W m$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ | Uses kinetic theory of gases |
| 10 | **Wiedemann–Franz ratio** | $L = \dfrac{K}{\sigma T} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2$ | W $\Omega$ K$^{-2}$ | $\approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K$^{-2}$ |
| 11 | Lorentz number (classical) | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2$ | W $\Omega$ K$^{-2}$ | Numerical: $1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K$^{-2}$ |
| 12 | AC conductivity (Drude) | $\sigma(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}$ | S m$^{-1}$ | $\sigma_0 = ne^2\tau/m$, high-$\omega$ roll-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall coefficient sign:** For free electrons (negative charge carriers), Drude correctly predicts $R_H = -1/(ne)$, *negative* — a great early success.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor & Microelectronics:** The relation $\sigma = ne\mu$ is the *starting point* for doping calculations in Si, Ge, GaAs, and for designing the channel conductance in MOSFETs.
- **Materials Selection:** $\tau$ (and hence $\lambda$) determines whether a metal is a *good* conductor (long $\tau$, e.g. Cu, Ag, Au) or a *poor* one (short $\tau$, e.g. nichrome for heaters).
- **Thermal Management:** The Wiedemann–Franz law lets engineers *estimate* thermal conductivity from a simple electrical resistivity measurement, used in heat-sink and PCB design.
- **High-Frequency / RF Design:** The AC Drude formula $\sigma(\omega)$ is the foundation of the **Drude model for skin depth** in microwave circuits, antenna design, and EMI shielding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout:** $\vec{v}_{thermal}$ denotes the random velocity, $\vec{v}_{drift}$ the small field-induced bias, and $\vec{v} = \vec{v}_{thermal} + \vec{v}_{drift}$. Over a thermal ensemble, $\langle \vec{v}_{thermal} \rangle = 0$, so $\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \vec{v}_d$.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Drift Velocity, Current Density, and DC Conductivity (14-mark standard derivation)

**Step 1 — Equation of motion of a free electron between collisions.**
Consider a free electron of mass $m$ and charge $-e$ (we take $e > 0$) moving in an applied DC electric field $\vec{E}$. The only force on it is the electric force:

$$\vec{F} = -e\vec{E} = m\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$$

Therefore the acceleration between collisions is:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -\dfrac{e\vec{E}}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the mean free time $\tau$.**
After a collision, the electron's velocity is completely random. The next collision occurs on average after time $\tau$. Starting from $\vec{v}(0) = \vec{v}_{random}$ (random, averages to zero in ensemble), the velocity at the instant *just before* the next collision is:

$$\vec{v}(\tau) = \vec{v}_{random} - \dfrac{e\vec{E}}{m}\,\tau$$

**Step 3 — Average over the electron ensemble.**
Since the average random velocity over the entire electron gas is zero ($\langle \vec{v}_{random} \rangle = 0$), the **average drift velocity** is:

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \vec{v}_d = -\dfrac{e\tau}{m}\vec{E}$$

The magnitude of the drift velocity is therefore:

$$v_d = \dfrac{eE\tau}{m}$$

The negative sign indicates that the electron drifts **opposite** to $\vec{E}$, as expected for a negative charge carrier.

**Step 4 — Current density $J$.**
Current density is the charge crossing unit area per unit time. With $n$ electrons per unit volume, each carrying charge $-e$ and moving with average velocity $\vec{v}_d$:

$$\vec{J} = n(-e)\vec{v}_d = (-e)\,n\,\left(-\dfrac{e\tau}{m}\vec{E}\right) = \dfrac{ne^2\tau}{m}\,\vec{E}$$

The magnitude is:

$$J = \dfrac{ne^2\tau}{m}\,E$$

**Step 5 — Identify the conductivity $\sigma$.**
By definition, Ohm's law in local form is $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$. Comparing with Step 4:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \dfrac{ne^2\tau}{m} = ne\mu\;,\quad \text{where}\quad \mu = \dfrac{e\tau}{m}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation — $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m$ — is the **crowning achievement** of the Drude model. It cleanly separates the *electronic* ($n$, $e$, $m$) and *scattering* ($\tau$) contributions, and is the starting point of nearly every transport problem in solid-state physics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Free Path from the Maxwell–Boltzmann Speed

**Step 1 — Thermal (RMS) speed of an electron.**
For a 3D classical ideal gas, the mean-square speed is obtained from equipartition:

$$\dfrac{1}{2}m\langle v^2 \rangle = \dfrac{3}{2}k_B T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v_{th} \equiv \sqrt{\langle v^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_B T}{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Mean free path.**
The mean free path is the average distance travelled between two successive collisions:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda = v_{th}\,\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_B T}{m}}\,\tau\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Conductivity of the Free-Electron Gas

We use the kinetic-theory-of-gases result for a gas of particles of number density $n$, RMS speed $v_{th}$, mean free time $\tau$, and specific heat per particle $C_v$. The thermal conductivity is:

$$K = \dfrac{1}{3}\,n\,v_{th}^{\,2}\,\tau\,C_v$$

**Step 1 — Insert the classical specific heat.**
For a 3D monatomic classical ideal gas, equipartition gives the molar heat capacity at constant volume as $C_v = (3/2)k_B$ *per particle*:

$$C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}k_B$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $C_v$ and simplify.**

$$K = \dfrac{1}{3}\,n\,v_{th}^{\,2}\,\tau \cdot \dfrac{3}{2}k_B = \dfrac{1}{2}\,n\,k_B\,v_{th}^{\,2}\,\tau$$

**Step 3 — Insert $v_{th}^{\,2} = 3k_BT/m$.**

$$K = \dfrac{1}{2}\,n\,k_B\,\left(\dfrac{3k_B T}{m}\right)\,\tau = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{n k_B^{\,2} T \tau}{m}$$

$$\boxed{\;K = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{n k_B^{\,2} T \tau}{m}\;}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Wiedemann–Franz Law

The Wiedemann–Franz ratio compares how well a metal conducts heat to how well it conducts electricity, at the same temperature.

**Step 1 — Write $L = K/(\sigma T)$.**

$$L = \dfrac{K}{\sigma T} = \dfrac{\dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{n k_B^{\,2} T \tau}{m}}{\dfrac{n e^2 \tau}{m}\cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common factors.**
Numerator and denominator share $\dfrac{n \tau}{m T}$. Therefore:

$$L = \dfrac{(3/2)\,k_B^{\,2}}{e^2} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{K}{\sigma T} = \mathcal{L} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2 \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W}\Omega\,\text{K}^{-2}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** $K/\sigma T$ is a *universal constant* — independent of the metal. The same electrons that carry charge also carry heat, and the ratio of "thermal carrying capacity" to "electrical carrying capacity" is set by the constants $k_B$ and $e$ alone. The experimental confirmation of this law was a *great* triumph of Drude theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantum correction (Sommerfeld, 1928):** Using Fermi–Dirac statistics, the Lorentz number is corrected to $\mathcal{L}_{quantum} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2 \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K$^{-2}$, which agrees with low-temperature experiments to better than 10%.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: AC Conductivity (Drude Response to a Sinusoidal Field)

Let the applied field be $\vec{E}(t) = \vec{E}_0 e^{-i\omega t}$. The equation of motion of a free electron with phenomenological damping rate $\gamma = 1/\tau$ is:

$$m\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} + m\gamma\,\vec{v} = -e\vec{E}(t)$$

**Step 1 — Assume a steady-state solution** $\vec{v}(t) = \vec{v}_0 e^{-i\omega t}$. Substituting:

$$-i\omega m\,\vec{v}_0 + m\gamma\,\vec{v}_0 = -e\vec{E}_0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \vec{v}_0 = \dfrac{-e/m}{\gamma - i\omega}\,\vec{E}_0 = \dfrac{-e\tau/m}{1 - i\omega\tau}\,\vec{E}_0$$

**Step 2 — Current density and AC conductivity.**

$$\vec{J}_0 = (-e)n\,\vec{v}_0 = \dfrac{ne^2\tau/m}{1 - i\omega\tau}\,\vec{E}_0 \equiv \sigma(\omega)\,\vec{E}_0$$

Therefore the **complex Drude conductivity** is:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{1 - i\omega\tau}\;,\qquad \sigma_0 \equiv \dfrac{ne^2\tau}{m}\;}$$

Separating real (in-phase) and imaginary (out-of-phase, capacitive) parts by multiplying numerator and denominator by $1 + i\omega\tau$:

$$\sigma(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_0(1 + i\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\sigma_0}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}}_{\text{Re}\,\sigma} + i\,\underbrace{\dfrac{\sigma_0\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}}_{\text{Im}\,\sigma}$$

- **DC limit** ($\omega\tau \ll 1$): $\sigma \to \sigma_0$ (recovers DC result).
- **High-frequency limit** ($\omega\tau \gg 1$): $\sigma \to \sigma_0/(\omega^2\tau^2) \to 0$ — the electrons cannot follow the rapidly oscillating field, conduction dies.

---

### 3.6 Successes and Failures of the Classical Free Electron Theory — Quick Audit Table

| Phenomenon | Drude Prediction | Experiment | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ohm's law $V = IR$ | ✓ | ✓ | **Success** |
| $\sigma$ values (Cu $\sim 6\times10^7$ S/m) | Correct order of magnitude | ✓ | **Success** |
| Hall coefficient sign (electrons) | Negative | Negative | **Success** |
| Wiedemann–Franz ratio $L$ | $1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ W$\Omega$/K² | $\sim 2.2$–$2.4 \times 10^{-8}$ | **Off by factor 2** (Sommerfeld fixes) |
| Electronic specific heat $C_v$ | $(3/2)n k_B$ (huge) | $\sim 10^{-4}$ of this (low $T$) | **Fails badly** |
| $\sigma \propto 1/T$ in pure metals | $\tau \propto 1/T$ (phonon scattering) | ✓ at high $T$ | **Success** |
| Magnetoresistance | Zero (open orbits) | Usually nonzero | **Fails** |
| Temperature dependence of $\sigma$ at low $T$ | Constant | $\sigma \propto T^5$ | **Fails** |

> [!WARNING]
> The **specific-heat failure** is the most damning. A classical electron gas with $n \sim 10^{28}$ m$^{-3}$ would have $C_v \sim 10^{4}$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$ — but experiment shows the *electronic* contribution is $\sim 10^{-3}$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$ at room temperature, a factor of $\sim 10^{4}$ too small. The resolution (Sommerfeld, 1928) is that electrons obey **Fermi–Dirac**, not Maxwell–Boltzmann, statistics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** flows. The first traces the chain of postulates → formulae → experimental tests. The second is a block diagram of an electron's life-cycle inside a metal under an applied field.

### 4.1 Drude Theory — Conceptual Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Metal with ions + free electrons] --> B[Postulate 1: Electrons free, ions rigid background]
    B --> C[Postulate 2: Random collisions with mean free time tau]
    C --> D[Postulate 3: Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution after collision]
    D --> E[Apply external electric field E]
    E --> F[Newton's 2nd Law between collisions: m dv/dt = -eE]
    F --> G[Average over ensemble: random part vanishes]
    G --> H[Drift velocity: v_d = eE tau / m]
    H --> I[Current density: J = nev_d = ne squared tau E / m]
    I --> J[DC Conductivity: sigma = ne squared tau / m]
    J --> K[Thermal conductivity: K = 3 n k_B squared T tau / 2m]
    K --> L[Wiedemann-Franz: L = K / sigma T = 1.11e-8 W Ohm K^-2]
    L --> M[Experimental Verification on Cu, Ag, Au at T greater than theta_D]
    M --> N{Verdict}
    N --> N1[Success: Ohm law, Hall sign, sigma, W-F law at high T]
    N --> N2[Failure: specific heat, low-T sigma, magnetoresistance]
    N2 --> O[Resolution: Sommerfeld Free Electron Theory with Fermi-Dirac]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Electron Motion Under an Applied E Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t = 0: Random collision, v = v_random] --> B[Free flight segment 1: Acceleration a = -eE/m]
    B --> C[At time t = tau: Just before next collision, v = v_random + v_d]
    C --> D[Random collision: v_random resampled from Maxwell-Boltzmann]
    D --> E[Free flight segment 2: same physics]
    E --> F[Repeat N times]
    F --> G[Ensemble average: random velocities cancel, drift remains]
    G --> H[Net result: Constant drift velocity v_d = eE tau / m]
    H --> I[Output: Steady current density J = ne squared tau E / m]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Electrical Conductivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sigma[sigma = ne squared tau / m] --> N[Carrier density n]
    Sigma --> E[Electron charge e = 1.6e-19 C]
    Sigma --> M[Electron mass m = 9.11e-31 kg]
    Sigma --> Tau[Mean free time tau]
    N --> N1[Valence electrons: 1 per atom for alkali, 2-3 for transition metals]
    E --> E1[Fixed constant of nature]
    M --> M1[Fixed constant of nature]
    Tau --> TauA[Tau decreases with T phonon scattering high T]
    Tau --> TauB[Tau decreases with impurity concentration]
    Tau --> TauC[Tau decreases with lattice defects]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern** for the course GAPHT121 — Physics for Information Science. Mark distribution: **Part A (3 marks each, ~2 questions)**, **Part B (14 marks, internal choice between two questions, each split 7 + 7)**.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State and explain the postulates of the classical free electron theory (Drude–Lorentz model).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Drude–Lorentz classical free electron theory is based on the following postulates:

1. **Free electron approximation:** In a metal, the valence electrons are free to move throughout the volume of the metal, while the positive ion cores are treated as a stationary, uniformly distributed background. The mutual interaction between free electrons is ignored.
2. **Existence of a mean free time $\tau$:** Each free electron undergoes instantaneous, random collisions with the ion cores. The *average* time between two successive collisions is called the **relaxation time** or **mean free time** $\tau$.
3. **Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics:** Immediately after each collision, the electron's velocity is randomised and its magnitude follows the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution at the local temperature of the metal.

> These three postulates together lead to the central result: $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m$.

*(Award 1 mark per postulate, full credit for stating the resultant conductivity formula.)*

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define (i) relaxation time, (ii) mean free path, and (iii) mobility of a free electron. State their SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Relaxation time $\tau$:** The average time interval between two successive collisions experienced by a free electron in a metal. **SI unit: second (s).**
- **(ii) Mean free path $\lambda$:** The average distance travelled by a free electron between two successive collisions; $\lambda = v_{th}\tau$, where $v_{th}$ is the thermal (RMS) speed. **SI unit: metre (m).**
- **(iii) Mobility $\mu$:** The drift velocity acquired per unit applied electric field; $\mu = v_d/E = e\tau/m$. **SI unit: m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹.**

*(1 mark per definition; ½ mark for the SI unit of each.)*

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two full questions. Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **electrical conductivity** of a metal on the basis of the classical free electron theory. Hence obtain the **Drude formula** for resistivity and comment on its temperature dependence.

**(b)** Using the kinetic theory of gases, derive an expression for the **thermal conductivity** of the free-electron gas. Hence obtain the **Wiedemann–Franz law** and compute the **classical Lorentz number** using standard constants.

##### Model Solution A

**(a) Derivation of $\sigma$:** (7 marks — see full Derivation 3.1 in Section 3 above. Step-wise award: 1 mark for postulate setup, 2 marks for equation of motion + integration over $\tau$, 2 marks for ensemble averaging and $v_d$, 2 marks for current density $J$ and final $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m$.)

**Comment on temperature dependence (built into the 7-mark budget, last 1–2 marks):**
The free electron number density $n$ is essentially **temperature independent** (set by the valence of the metal). The temperature dependence of $\sigma$ therefore comes entirely from $\tau$:
- At **high temperatures** ($T > \theta_D$, the Debye temperature), electron–phonon scattering dominates and $\tau \propto 1/T$. Hence $\sigma \propto 1/T$, i.e. resistivity $\rho \propto T$. (Verified for Cu, Ag, Au above 100 K.)
- At **low temperatures** ($T \ll \theta_D$), phonon scattering freezes out; $\tau$ saturates to a value set by **impurity and defect scattering**, which is temperature independent (Matthiessen's rule).

**(b) Derivation of $K$ and the Wiedemann–Franz law:** (7 marks — see Derivation 3.3 and 3.4 in Section 3 above. Step-wise award: 2 marks for kinetic theory expression with classical $C_v = (3/2)k_B$, 2 marks for $K = (3/2)nk_B^2T\tau/m$, 2 marks for forming the ratio $K/(\sigma T)$ and cancellation, 1 mark for the final boxed result and the numerical Lorentz number.)

**Numerical value of the classical Lorentz number:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2 = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}\right)^2$$

Computing the inner fraction: $\dfrac{k_B}{e} = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.614 \times 10^{-5}$ V K$^{-1}$ (this is $k_B/e$ in SI, related to $V_T = k_B T/e$).

$$\left(\dfrac{k_B}{e}\right)^2 = (8.614 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 7.42 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}^2 \text{K}^{-2}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 7.42 \times 10^{-9} = 11.13 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W}\Omega\,\text{K}^{-2}$$

> **Examiner's comment:** Many students forget to convert the $(k_B/e)^2$ units properly. Note: $(k_B/e)^2$ has units of V² K⁻² = W² A⁻² K⁻²; combined with the missing $A^2$ from conductivity (S = $\Omega^{-1}$ = A V⁻¹) and the proper $T$ division, the final units are correctly W $\Omega$ K⁻². **State the units explicitly in the answer.**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define the **mean free path** of an electron. Derive its expression in terms of the thermal speed and relaxation time. Estimate the mean free path of an electron in copper at 300 K, given $n_{Cu} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ m⁻³, $\rho_{Cu} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-8}$ $\Omega$ m, and $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

**(b)** Discuss the **successes and failures** of the classical free electron theory. How does the **Sommerfeld free electron theory** resolve the specific-heat discrepancy?

##### Model Solution B

**(a) Definition + expression (3 marks):**
The mean free path $\lambda$ is the *average distance* a free electron travels between two successive collisions in a metal. From the Drude model:

$$\lambda = v_{th}\,\tau,\quad v_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_B T}{m}}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_B T}{m}}\,\tau$$

**Numerical estimation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute thermal speed at 300 K:**

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.242 \times 10^{-20}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{1.363 \times 10^{10}}\ \text{m}^2\text{s}^{-2}$$

$$v_{th} = 1.17 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute relaxation time from conductivity:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{m}{ne^2\rho} = \dfrac{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 \times 1.7 \times 10^{-8}}$$

Denominator:

$$8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 2.566 \times 10^{-38} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-8} = 8.5 \times 2.566 \times 1.7 \times 10^{28 - 38 - 8}$$

$$= 37.08 \times 10^{-18} = 3.708 \times 10^{-17}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = \dfrac{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}{3.708 \times 10^{-17}} = 2.46 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Mean free path:**

$$\lambda = v_{th}\tau = 1.17 \times 10^{5} \times 2.46 \times 10^{-14} = 2.88 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \approx 2.9\ \text{nm}$$

> **Physical insight:** This is roughly **10 lattice spacings** of copper — short enough that the classical picture is on the verge of breaking down, which is one reason quantum (Sommerfeld) corrections are needed.

**(b) Successes and failures + Sommerfeld resolution (7 marks):**

**Successes (3 marks):**
1. Correctly predicts **Ohm's law** in local form, $\vec{J} = \sigma\vec{E}$.
2. Order-of-magnitude correct values of $\sigma$ for Cu, Ag, Au using $n \sim 10^{28}$ m⁻³ and $\tau \sim 10^{-14}$ s.
3. Correctly gives the **sign of the Hall coefficient** (negative for free-electron metals).
4. Reproduces the **Wiedemann–Franz law** $L = K/(\sigma T) = $ const; the predicted constant is within a factor of 2 of experiment (the factor $\pi^2/3$ difference is due to classical vs quantum statistics).
5. Correctly predicts $\rho \propto T$ at high $T$ from electron–phonon scattering.

**Failures (2 marks):**
1. **Electronic specific heat:** Predicts $C_v = (3/2)nk_B$, which is $\sim 10^{4}$ times too large at low $T$ (experiment shows $C_v^{el} = \gamma T$ with $\gamma \sim 10^{-3}$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻²).
2. **Temperature dependence of $\sigma$ at low $T$:** Predicts saturation at a constant residual value but not the correct $T^5$ Bloch–Grüneisen form.
3. **Magnetoresistance:** Predicts zero magnetoresistance for a free-electron gas; real metals show nonzero values.
4. **Thermionic emission / mean free path in Cu:** Underestimates the *effective* number of electrons carrying current.

**Sommerfeld resolution (2 marks):**
Arnold Sommerfeld (1928) replaced **Maxwell–Boltzmann** with **Fermi–Dirac** statistics and introduced a **Fermi energy** $E_F$ (a few eV for typical metals) and a **Fermi speed** $v_F = \sqrt{2E_F/m} \sim 10^6$ m s⁻¹. Only electrons within $\sim k_B T$ of the Fermi surface participate in transport and thermal excitation. This:
- Drastically reduces the *effective* number of thermally excited electrons, fixing the **specific-heat catastrophe** ($C_v^{el} = (\pi^2/2)(nk_B)(T/T_F) \propto T$).
- Corrects the **Wiedemann–Franz Lorentz number** to the proper quantum value $\mathcal{L} = (\pi^2/3)(k_B/e)^2 \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K⁻².
- Predicts the correct **temperature dependence** of the resistivity at low $T$ via the Bloch–Grüneisen formula.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in Classical Free Electron Theory questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the equation of motion ($F = -eE$, not $+eE$). This flips the direction of $v_d$ and hence the sign of $J$. Mark deduction: ½ to 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $v_{th} = \sqrt{2k_BT/m}$ (1D average) instead of $\sqrt{3k_BT/m}$ (3D RMS).** The 3D Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution has $\langle v^2 \rangle = 3k_BT/m$. The factor of $3$ propagates to the thermal conductivity and the Lorentz number. Wrong factor → wrong $L$ → 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Stating $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m$ without deriving the drift velocity $v_d$ first.** KTU examiners *require* the full chain: postulates → equation of motion → ensemble average → drift velocity → current density → conductivity. Skipping the middle two steps costs 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_v$ per mole vs per particle.** Equipartition gives $C_v = (3/2)k_B$ per particle, or $(3/2)R$ per mole. Storing the wrong one in $K$ corrupts the Wiedemann–Franz derivation.
> 5. **Failing to comment on temperature dependence of $\sigma$** when the question asks. Even a 1-line comment ("$\sigma \propto 1/T$ at high $T$ due to phonon scattering") can earn the last 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Numerical errors in the Lorentz number.** Always show the substitution of $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C explicitly; the final answer is $1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K⁻², **not** $1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ J K⁻² (units matter for full credit).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Must-Know Items for the KTU Board Exam on Classical Free Electron Theory:**

- ☐ **Drude's three postulates**: free electrons, mean free time $\tau$, Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics after each collision.
- ☐ **Master equation**: $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m = ne\mu$, with $\mu = e\tau/m$.
- ☐ **Drift velocity**: $v_d = eE\tau/m$, direction **opposite** to $\vec{E}$ (electron is negative).
- ☐ **Mean free time** $\tau$: related to resistivity by $\tau = m/(ne^2\rho)$.
- ☐ **Thermal speed**: $v_{th} = \sqrt{3k_B T/m}$ (3D, RMS). Do *not* confuse with mean speed $\sqrt{8k_BT/(\pi m)}$ or most probable speed $\sqrt{2k_BT/m}$.
- ☐ **Mean free path**: $\lambda = v_{th}\,\tau$. Typical metal at 300 K: $\lambda \sim 1$–$10$ nm.
- ☐ **Specific heat per electron** (classical): $C_v = (3/2)k_B$. This is the root cause of the *specific-heat catastrophe*.
- ☐ **Thermal conductivity** (Drude): $K = (3/2) n k_B^{\,2} T \tau / m$.
- ☐ **Wiedemann–Franz law**: $K/(\sigma T) = (3/2)(k_B/e)^2 = \mathcal{L} \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K⁻² (classical) or $\pi^2/3 \cdot (k_B/e)^2 \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W $\Omega$ K⁻² (quantum, Sommerfeld).
- ☐ **AC / frequency-dependent conductivity**: $\sigma(\omega) = \sigma_0/(1 + \omega^2\tau^2)$ (real part) or complex form $\sigma_0/(1 - i\omega\tau)$.
- ☐ **Hall coefficient sign**: $R_H = -1/(ne)$ (negative for electron-conducting metals). Drude got this *right*.
- ☐ **Failures to remember**: (i) electronic specific heat too large by $\sim 10^4$, (ii) low-$T$ $\sigma(T)$ wrong, (iii) zero magnetoresistance predicted, (iv) $\mathcal{L}$ off by factor $\pi^2/3 \div 3/2 \approx 2.2$. **Sommerfeld free electron theory (Fermi–Dirac statistics, $E_F$, $v_F$) fixes these.**

> **Final tip:** In a 14-mark derivation question, the examiner's *mental checklist* is: postulate → equation of motion → drift velocity → current density → conductivity → comment on temperature dependence. Hit all five checkpoints, in order, with clear statements, and a full-mark answer is yours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Conductivity in Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrical Conductivity in Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Conductivity (σ)** in a metal is defined as the intrinsic property of a material that quantifies its ability to allow the flow of **electric current** under the influence of an applied **electric field**. It is the reciprocal of **resistivity (ρ)** and has SI units of **Siemens per meter (S·m⁻¹)** or equivalently **Ω⁻¹·m⁻¹**.

In the context of the **Classical Free Electron Theory (Drude–Lorentz Model)**, the conduction electrons in a metal are treated as a **gas of free electrons** (also called the *Fermi gas*) that move randomly in a crystalline lattice of positive ions. When an external electric field is applied, these electrons acquire a small net drift superimposed on their random thermal motion, producing a macroscopic current.

Mathematically, the conductivity is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{J}{E}$$

where:
- $J$ = current density (A/m²)
- $E$ = applied electric field (V/m)
- $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme expects students to derive conductivity using Drude's classical model, calculate drift velocity and mobility, and explain the **failure of classical theory** to predict experimental values (this leads naturally to the quantum treatment covered later in Module 2).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic" Model 🚗

Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where cars (electrons) are constantly moving in every direction at random speeds (thermal motion at ~10⁶ m/s). The highway itself represents the metallic crystal lattice.

- **Without a traffic signal (no E-field):** Cars move randomly, the net flow across any imaginary line on the road is **zero** — no current.
- **With a green light (applied E-field):** All cars acquire a tiny **forward bias** in addition to their random motion. This small, uniform forward motion of millions of cars is the **drift velocity** (v_d), which is surprisingly slow (~mm/s) compared to thermal speeds.

Just as the highway has **friction** (collisions with other cars, road imperfections), electrons suffer **collisions with lattice ions, impurities, and defects**. This friction is what we call **electrical resistance**. A smoother highway (purer metal, lower temperature) means **higher conductivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Conductivity depends on **(a) the number density of free electrons (n)**, **(b) how often they collide (relaxation time τ)**, and **(c) the charge of the carrier (e)** — not on how fast they thermally zip around.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Cu) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free electron density | $n$ | **8.5 × 10²⁸** | m⁻³ |
| Electron charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e$ | **9.109 × 10⁻³¹** | kg |
| Relaxation time (τ) | $\tau$ | **~10⁻¹⁴ to 10⁻¹⁵** | s |
| Drift velocity | $v_d$ | **~10⁻⁴ m/s** at 1 A/mm² | m/s |
| Conductivity of Copper | $\sigma$ | **5.96 × 10⁷** | S/m |
| Resistivity of Copper | $\rho$ | **1.68 × 10⁻⁸** | Ω·m |

---

## 1.4 Why Study This? — Engineering Relevance

- **Semiconductor industry:** Doping controls n, the carrier density.
- **Integrated circuits:** Copper interconnects replaced aluminum because of higher σ.
- **Superconductors:** σ → ∞ below a critical temperature (T_c).
- **Sensor design:** Thin-film resistors rely on precise resistivity control.
- **PCB design:** Signal integrity depends on conductor losses quantified by σ.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Postulates of the Drude–Lorentz Classical Free Electron Model

The classical theory rests on **five key postulates**, which the student must commit to memory:

1. **Free Electron Approximation:** Between collisions, valence electrons move freely in the metal — the influence of ions and other electrons (except during collisions) is **completely ignored**.
2. **Independent Electron Approximation:** Electron–electron interactions are neglected; each electron moves independently in the mean field of the lattice.
3. **Binary Collisions:** Electrons collide **only with static ions** (not with other electrons), modeled as instantaneous, elastic scattering events.
4. **Maxwell–Boltzmann Statistics:** Electrons obey classical MB distribution at thermal equilibrium. *(This is the principal flaw — corrected by Fermi–Dirac statistics in the Sommerfeld model.)*
5. **Mean Free Path:** The probability that an electron suffers a collision in time $dt$ is $dt/\tau$, where $\tau$ is the **relaxation time** (mean time between collisions).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Current Flow

- **Step 1 — Random thermal motion:** At temperature T, free electrons move chaotically with average speed $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8k_BT/(\pi m_e)}$ (from MB distribution). Net current = **0**.
- **Step 2 — Apply field E:** Each electron experiences force $\vec{F} = -e\vec{E}$, gaining acceleration $\vec{a} = -e\vec{E}/m_e$.
- **Step 3 — Collision interrupts acceleration:** After an average time $\tau$, the electron collides and **loses its drift memory**.
- **Step 4 — Average drift velocity emerges:** The steady-state average velocity (drift velocity) is $\vec{v}_d = -e\vec{E}\tau/m_e$.
- **Step 5 — Current density:** $J = -n e v_d = ne^2\tau E/m_e$.
- **Step 6 — Ohm's law emerges naturally:** Comparing with $J = \sigma E$ gives $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m_e$.

---

## 2.3 Definitions of Key Derived Quantities

- **Drift Velocity ($v_d$):** The average velocity acquired by charge carriers due to an applied electric field. It is **vectorially opposite** to E for electrons.
- **Mobility ($\mu$):** The drift velocity per unit electric field, $\mu = v_d/E = e\tau/m_e$. Unit: **m²·V⁻¹·s⁻¹**.
- **Relaxation Time ($\tau$):** Average time between two successive collisions of an electron. It depends on **temperature, impurities, and lattice defects**.
- **Mean Free Path ($\lambda$):** Average distance traveled between collisions, $\lambda = v_{th}\tau$, where $v_{th}$ is the thermal (Fermi) velocity.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Current density | $J = n e v_d$ | A/m² | Charge per unit area per second |
| 2 | Drift velocity | $v_d = e E \tau / m_e$ | m/s | Net directed speed of electrons |
| 3 | Conductivity | $\sigma = n e^2 \tau / m_e$ | S/m | Reciprocal of resistivity |
| 4 | Resistivity | $\rho = m_e / (n e^2 \tau)$ | Ω·m | Opposition to current flow |
| 5 | Mobility | $\mu = e \tau / m_e$ | m²/(V·s) | v_d per unit field |
| 6 | Conductivity (mobility form) | $\sigma = n e \mu$ | S/m | Alternative expression |
| 7 | Mean free path | $\lambda = v_{th}\,\tau$ | m | Distance between collisions |
| 8 | Thermal velocity (classical) | $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8k_BT/(\pi m_e)}$ | m/s | MB average speed |
| 9 | Fermi velocity (quantum) | $v_F = \sqrt{2E_F/m_e}$ | m/s | Speed at top of Fermi sea |
| 10 | Relaxation time vs T | $\tau \propto 1/T$ (above Debye T) | s | Phonon scattering dominates |
| 11 | Matthiessen's rule | $\rho = \rho_{ph} + \rho_{imp}$ | Ω·m | Total resistivity = phonon + impurity |
| 12 | Wiedemann–Franz ratio | $L = \kappa/(\sigma T)$ | W·Ω/K² | Lorenz number ~ $2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| 13 | Electrical conductivity in terms of λ | $\sigma = n e^2 \lambda / (m_e v_{th})$ | S/m | Connects microscopic λ to macro σ |
| 14 | Temperature coefficient of resistance | $\alpha = (1/\rho)(d\rho/dT)$ | K⁻¹ | Linear for metals at high T |

> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute value and conditioning bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown rendering. In handwritten KTU scripts, students should use the standard $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation.

---

## 2.5 Failure of Classical Theory (KTU Favourite Question)

The Drude model successfully predicts **Ohm's law** and the **Wiedemann–Franz law**, but **fails spectacularly** in several respects:

| Experimental Observation | Classical Prediction | Actual Result | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat capacity of electrons | $C_v = \frac{3}{2} n k_B$ (Dulong–Petit-like) | $\sim 10^{-2} \times$ predicted | **Off by ~100×** |
| Mean free path $\lambda$ | Å scale (atomic spacing) | ~ 10–100 nm at room T | **Off by ~100×** |
| Temperature dependence of $\sigma$ | $\sigma \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ | $\sigma \propto 1/T$ | **Wrong power law** |
| Sign of Hall coefficient | Wrong sign for some metals | Correct with quantum | **Hall anomaly** |
| Paramagnetic/Pauli spin susceptibility | Curie-like paramagnetic | Pauli paramagnetic | **Wrong type** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution:** These failures are corrected by the **Sommerfeld Free Electron Model** (quantum mechanical treatment using Fermi–Dirac statistics), which is covered in detail in **Module 2**. The classical model is retained because it gives the **correct functional form of Ohm's law** and is sufficient for an introductory analysis expected at the KTU B.Tech level.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Electrical wiring:** Cu (σ = 5.96×10⁷ S/m) and Al (σ = 3.5×10⁷ S/m) chosen for high σ and cost.
- **Resistance thermometers:** Pt has a near-linear R vs T, used in RTDs.
- **Heating elements:** Nichrome (high ρ) used to convert $I^2R$ loss into heat (toasters, heaters).
- **Inductive heating & eddy currents:** Used in induction cooktops, metal forging.
- **Electromagnetic shielding:** High σ materials (Cu, Al) block EM waves via skin effect.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Drift Velocity of an Electron

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the average drift velocity of conduction electrons in a metal subjected to a uniform electric field $\vec{E}$, using Drude's relaxation time approximation.

### Starting Assumptions
- Consider an electron that has just suffered a collision at time $t = 0$.
- Its initial velocity after the collision is $\vec{v}_0$ (random, from thermal motion).
- A constant electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied along the negative x-axis (convention: field points from + to −, force on electron is opposite).
- The electron experiences force $\vec{F} = -e\vec{E}$ and acceleration $\vec{a} = -e\vec{E}/m_e$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The equation of motion between collisions is Newton's second law:

$$m_e \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -e\vec{E}$$

Integrate from $t = 0$ to time $t$ (until the next collision):

$$\int_{\vec{v}_0}^{\vec{v}(t)} d\vec{v} = -\frac{e\vec{E}}{m_e} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

The velocity at time $t$ is therefore:

$$\vec{v}(t) = \vec{v}_0 - \frac{e\vec{E}}{m_e}\, t$$

Now, to find the **average** drift velocity, we average over the time between collisions. Using the **relaxation time approximation**, the probability that an electron survives without collision for time $t$ and collides in the next $dt$ is $e^{-t/\tau}/\tau$. The average value of $t$ is:

$$\langle t \rangle = \int_0^{\infty} t \,\frac{e^{-t/\tau}}{\tau}\, dt = \tau$$

By definition, the **drift velocity** is the average velocity acquired **due to the field** (the random part $\vec{v}_0$ averages to zero):

$$\vec{v}_d = \langle \vec{v}(t) \rangle = \langle \vec{v}_0 \rangle - \frac{e\vec{E}}{m_e}\langle t \rangle$$

Since $\langle \vec{v}_0 \rangle = 0$ (random isotropic motion) and $\langle t \rangle = \tau$:

$$\boxed{\vec{v}_d = -\frac{e\vec{E}\,\tau}{m_e}}$$

The negative sign indicates that electrons drift **opposite** to the applied field (since they carry negative charge). For an electron, we typically write the magnitude:

$$v_d = \frac{e E \tau}{m_e}$$

> **Transition Logic:** The drift velocity is proportional to the field (consistent with Ohm's law) and inversely proportional to the mass (heavier particles drift slower). The proportionality constant is the **mobility**, $\mu = e\tau/m_e$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Electrical Conductivity $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m_e$

**Statement:** Starting from the drift velocity expression, derive the macroscopic conductivity of a metal.

### Step 1 — Define Current Density

Consider a conductor of cross-sectional area $A$. The number of free electrons per unit volume is $n$. In time $\Delta t$, the number of electrons crossing a cross-section is the number contained in a length $v_d \Delta t$:

$$N = n \, A \, v_d \, \Delta t$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Current

The total charge crossing in time $\Delta t$ is $Q = N \cdot e = n e A v_d \Delta t$. By definition, current $I = Q/\Delta t$:

$$I = n e A v_d$$

### Step 3 — Express Current Density

$$J = \frac{I}{A} = n e v_d$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Drift Velocity

Using $v_d = eE\tau/m_e$:

$$J = n e \cdot \frac{e E \tau}{m_e} = \frac{n e^2 \tau}{m_e}\, E$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Ohm's Law

Ohm's law in microscopic form states $J = \sigma E$. Comparing:

$$\sigma E = \frac{n e^2 \tau}{m_e} E$$

Dividing both sides by $E$ (non-zero):

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{n e^2 \tau}{m_e}}$$

### Step 6 — Resistivity Form

Taking the reciprocal:

$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{m_e}{n e^2 \tau}}$$

> **Transition Logic:** Conductivity scales linearly with carrier density $n$ (more carriers = more charge transport) and linearly with relaxation time $\tau$ (fewer collisions = longer mean free path = higher conductivity). This is why pure, defect-free, cold metals conduct best.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Mean Free Path and Temperature Dependence

**Statement:** Show that the relaxation time $\tau$ in a metal is inversely proportional to absolute temperature (above the Debye temperature), and hence $\sigma \propto 1/T$.

### Assumption
At high temperatures (T > Debye temperature $\Theta_D$), lattice vibrations (phonons) are the dominant scattering mechanism. The mean square displacement of ions $\langle u^2 \rangle \propto T$.

### Logic Steps
- The cross-sectional area of an ion "seen" by a colliding electron is proportional to $\langle u^2 \rangle$.
- This is called the **scattering cross-section**: $\Sigma \propto \langle u^2 \rangle \propto T$.
- The mean free path is $\lambda = 1/(n_{ions}\,\Sigma) \propto 1/T$.
- The mean free time $\tau = \lambda/v_{th}$. Since $v_{th} = \sqrt{3k_BT/m_e} \propto \sqrt{T}$ (from classical equipartition):

$$\tau = \frac{\lambda}{v_{th}} \propto \frac{1/T}{\sqrt{T}} = \frac{1}{T^{3/2}}$$

Substituting into $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m_e$:

$$\sigma \propto \frac{1}{T^{3/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Alert:** The classical theory predicts $\sigma \propto T^{-3/2}$, but experiment shows $\sigma \propto T^{-1}$ at high T. This is **another failure** of the classical model, resolved by the quantum (Sommerfeld) treatment where the velocity of electrons at the Fermi surface is essentially **temperature-independent** ($v_F$ ≈ const), giving $\tau \propto 1/T$ directly.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Wiedemann–Franz Law

**Statement:** Derive the Wiedemann–Franz law using classical free electron theory, predicting a Lorenz number $L$.

### Thermal Conductivity from Kinetic Theory

For an electron gas, the thermal conductivity (analogous to gas kinetic theory) is:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{3} n v_{th}^2 \tau C_v / m_e$$

For a classical ideal gas, the specific heat per unit volume is $C_v = \frac{3}{2} n k_B$ (Dulong–Petit for electrons, classically). Substituting:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{3} n v_{th}^2 \tau \cdot \frac{3 k_B n}{2 m_e / n} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{n v_{th}^2 \tau k_B}{m_e}$$

Using $v_{th}^2 = 3k_BT/m_e$ (from equipartition, $\frac{1}{2}m v_{th}^2 = \frac{3}{2}k_BT$):

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{2} \frac{n \cdot 3k_BT/m_e \cdot \tau k_B}{m_e} = \frac{3}{2} \frac{n k_B^2 T \tau}{m_e^2}$$

### Form the Ratio $\kappa/(\sigma T)$

Recall $\sigma = n e^2 \tau / m_e$:

$$\frac{\kappa}{\sigma T} = \frac{\frac{3}{2} n k_B^2 T \tau / m_e^2}{(n e^2 \tau / m_e) \cdot T} = \frac{3 k_B^2}{2 e^2 m_e} \cdot \frac{m_e}{1} = \frac{3 k_B^2}{2 e^2}$$

Define the **Lorenz number** $L$:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\kappa}{\sigma T} = \frac{3 k_B^2}{2 e^2} \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W}\,\Omega\,\text{K}^{-2}}$$

> **Transition Logic:** The law states that the ratio of thermal to electrical conductivity, divided by temperature, is **a universal constant** for all metals. Experimentally, $L \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W·Ω·K⁻² — the factor of ~2 discrepancy is again due to classical assumptions. The quantum correction (Sommerfeld) gives the correct numerical prefactor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔗 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electron Motion and Collision Process in a Metal

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **microscopic picture** of an electron in a metal lattice, including random thermal motion, applied field, and the collision process that gives rise to drift velocity and resistivity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Conduction electron in metal lattice]) --> B{Is electric field E applied?}
    B -- No --> C[Random thermal motion at v_th ~ 10^6 m/s]
    C --> D[Net current = 0; isotropic velocity distribution]
    B -- Yes --> E[Electron experiences force F = -eE]
    E --> F[Gains acceleration a = -eE per m_e]
    F --> G[Superimposed drift on random motion]
    G --> H{Time elapsed since last collision}
    H -- t less than tau --> I[Electron accelerates opposite to E]
    H -- t equals tau --> J[Collision with lattice ion or impurity]
    J --> K[Velocity randomized; drift component lost]
    K --> L[New random direction; new clock starts]
    L --> H
    I --> M[Drift velocity v_d = eEtau per m_e]
    M --> N[Macroscopic current density J = nev_d]
    N --> O[Ohm law emerges: J equals sigma times E]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classical Free Electron Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Electric field E]
        P2[Temperature T]

        P4[Relaxation time tau]
    end

    subgraph CORE[CLASSICAL DRUDE LORENTZ MODEL]
        Q1[Apply Newton 2nd law: m dv per dt equals -eE]
        Q2[Integrate over time: v equals v0 minus eEt per m_e]
        Q3[Average over collision times: t averages to tau]
        Q4[Drift velocity: v_d equals eEtau per m_e]
        Q5[Current density: J equals nev_d]
        Q6[Ohm law: J equals sigma E]
    end

    subgraph OUT1[OUTPUT QUANTITIES]
        R1[Conductivity sigma equals ne squared tau per m_e]
        R2[Resistivity rho equals 1 per sigma]
        R3[Mobility mu equals etau per m_e]

    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q3
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 --> Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> R1

    Q4 --> R3
    Q3 --> R4
    Q5 --> R5
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Classical vs Quantum Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        C1[Electrons obey Maxwell Boltzmann statistics]
        C2[Thermal velocity v_th proportional to sqrt T]

        C4[Heat capacity CV equals 3 NB kB per 2]
        C5[FAILURES: wrong heat capacity, wrong sigma T dependence]
    end

        S4[Heat capacity CV proportional to T]
        S5[SUCCESSES: matches experiment, Pauli paramagnetism]
    end
```
