# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part A carries 3 marks each. Answers should be **2–3 sentences** with a clear formula. Avoid lengthy derivations here.

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define electrical conductivity of a metal. Write its SI unit and the expression relating it to current density and electric field.**

**Model Answer:**

Electrical conductivity $\sigma$ of a metal is defined as the **ability of the material to conduct electric current**, quantitatively equal to the ratio of current density $J$ to the applied electric field $E$.

$$\sigma = \frac{J}{E} \;[\text{S·m}^{-1}]$$

The SI unit is **Siemens per metre (S·m⁻¹)** or equivalently **Ω⁻¹·m⁻¹**. The reciprocal of conductivity is resistivity, $\rho = 1/\sigma$, with units Ω·m.

*(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, unit & reciprocal 1)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is drift velocity? Why is it much smaller than the thermal velocity of electrons in a metal?**

**Model Answer:**

Drift velocity $v_d$ is the **small net average velocity** acquired by conduction electrons in a metal when an external electric field $E$ is applied. It is given by:

$$v_d = \frac{e E \tau}{m_e}$$

Thermal velocity ($v_{th} \sim 10^6$ m/s) arises from **random motion** of electrons at temperature T. The drift velocity is superimposed on this random motion and is typically of the order of **mm/s** for a current density of 1 A/mm². Hence $v_d \ll v_{th}$ because the directional component is averaged out by frequent collisions (every ~$10^{-14}$ s) which randomize the velocity.

*(3 marks: definition + formula 1, thermal vs drift values 1, collision reason 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. A student must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Sub-parts test both derivation skill and conceptual understanding.

---

### **📘 QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the electrical conductivity of a metal on the basis of Drude's classical free electron theory, clearly stating the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the above result, explain the temperature dependence of conductivity in metals. Why does the classical model fail to predict the correct temperature dependence?

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Assumptions of Drude's Theory:**
1. A metal contains free electrons that move in a uniform positive ion background.
2. Between collisions, electrons are free of all forces.
3. Electrons collide only with **static ions** (not with each other); collisions are instantaneous.
4. After each collision, the electron emerges with a velocity that is **randomly distributed**, independent of its pre-collision velocity.
5. The probability of a collision in time $dt$ is $dt/\tau$, where $\tau$ is the **mean relaxation time**.

**Derivation:**

When an electric field $E$ is applied along the x-axis, each electron experiences:

$$F = -eE \quad \text{and} \quad a = -\frac{eE}{m_e}$$

Between collisions, velocity at time $t$ is $v(t) = v_0 - (eE/m_e)t$. Averaging over all electrons with collision time distribution $\propto e^{-t/\tau}$:

$$v_d = \langle v(t) \rangle = 0 - \frac{eE}{m_e} \langle t \rangle = -\frac{eE\tau}{m_e}$$

Current density $J = -ne v_d$:

$$J = -ne \left(-\frac{eE\tau}{m_e}\right) = \frac{ne^2\tau}{m_e} E$$

By Ohm's law $J = \sigma E$:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{ne^2\tau}{m_e}}$$

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Temperature dependence (classical):**
The mean free path is $\lambda = 1/(n_{ion}\Sigma)$ where $\Sigma$ is the scattering cross-section. The cross-section $\Sigma \propto \langle u^2 \rangle$ where $\langle u^2 \rangle$ is the mean-square displacement of lattice ions, $\propto T$ at high $T$ (above Debye temperature $\Theta_D$). Therefore:

$$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{T}$$

The thermal velocity $v_{th} \propto \sqrt{T}$, so $\tau = \lambda/v_{th} \propto T^{-3/2}$. Substituting into $\sigma$:

$$\sigma \propto \tau \propto T^{-3/2}$$

Hence $\rho \propto T^{3/2}$.

**Why classical fails:**
Experimentally observed dependence is $\sigma \propto 1/T$ (i.e. $\rho \propto T$), **not** $T^{-3/2}$. The discrepancy arises because:
- The classical theory uses $v_{th} \propto \sqrt{T}$ as the relevant velocity, but only electrons **near the Fermi surface** participate in conduction (Sommerfeld model).
- The Fermi velocity $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s is **independent of T**.
- Therefore $\tau = \lambda/v_F \propto 1/T$ directly, giving $\sigma \propto 1/T$, matching experiment.

---

### **📗 QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **mobility** and **relaxation time** of charge carriers in a metal. Derive the relationship between electrical conductivity, carrier concentration, and mobility.

**(b) [7 marks]** State and explain the **Wiedemann–Franz law**. Show that the Lorenz number is a universal constant according to the classical free electron theory.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Mobility ($\mu$):** The drift velocity per unit electric field, $\mu = v_d/E$. Unit: m²·V⁻¹·s⁻¹.

**Relaxation time ($\tau$):** The average time interval between two successive collisions of a conduction electron. It characterises how quickly an electron's directed motion decays when the field is switched off. Unit: seconds (s).

**Relationship:**

From $v_d = eE\tau/m_e$, the mobility is:

$$\mu = \frac{v_d}{E} = \frac{e\tau}{m_e}$$

Substituting into $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m_e$:

$$\sigma = n e \cdot \frac{e\tau}{m_e} = n e \mu$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = n e \mu}$$

This form is highly useful in **semiconductor physics**, where $n$ varies with doping and temperature while $\mu$ is determined by scattering mechanisms.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Statement:** The Wiedemann–Franz law states that for metals, the ratio of thermal conductivity ($\kappa$) to electrical conductivity ($\sigma$) is directly proportional to absolute temperature T:

$$\frac{\kappa}{\sigma} = L T$$

where $L$ is the **Lorenz number**, a near-universal constant for all metals at a given temperature.

**Derivation of L (classical):**

From kinetic theory, thermal conductivity of an electron gas:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{3} n v_{th}^2 \tau \cdot \frac{C_v}{m_e}$$

For classical ideal gas, $C_v = \frac{3}{2} n k_B$ (per unit volume). Using $v_{th}^2 = 3k_BT/m_e$:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{3} \cdot n \cdot \frac{3k_BT}{m_e} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{3 n k_B}{2 m_e} = \frac{3 n^2 k_B^2 T \tau}{2 m_e^2}$$

Forming the ratio:

$$\frac{\kappa}{\sigma T} = \frac{3 n^2 k_B^2 T \tau / (2 m_e^2)}{(n e^2 \tau / m_e) \cdot T} = \frac{3 k_B^2}{2 e^2}$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{3 k_B^2}{2 e^2} \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W·Ω·K}^{-2}}$$

The experimental value is $L \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W·Ω·K⁻² (a factor of ~2.22 larger). The Sommerfeld model gives the correct numerical prefactor of $\pi^2/3$ instead of $3/2$, yielding $L = \pi^2 k_B^2 / (3 e^2)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention error in drift velocity** (loses 1 mark): Forgetting that the electron's drift is **opposite** to the applied field. State the direction explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up classical vs quantum velocity** (loses 2 marks): Always clarify whether you are using $v_{th}$ (classical, $\propto \sqrt{T}$) or $v_F$ (Fermi, constant) when discussing $\tau = \lambda/v$.
> 3. **Missing units in final answer** (loses 0.5 mark): Always append SI units — especially for $\sigma$ (S/m), $\mu$ (m²/V·s), $\tau$ (s).
> 4. **Stating assumptions incorrectly** (loses 2 marks): The five Drude postulates must be **listed explicitly**, not just vaguely referenced. Examiners check this.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the limitation of classical theory** (loses 1 mark): KTU 2024 explicitly tests this — always end a classical-theory answer with a brief note on the **failure** and pointer to quantum resolution.
> 6. **In Part B (a), writing J = nev_d without explaining n** (loses 1 mark): Define n as the free electron density per unit volume.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Drude's classical model** treats conduction electrons as a classical ideal gas in a metal.
- ✅ **Conductivity** $\sigma = ne^2\tau/m_e$ — the **most important formula** of this module.
- ✅ **Drift velocity** $v_d = eE\tau/m_e$ is **tiny** (~mm/s) compared to thermal velocity (~10⁶ m/s).
- ✅ **Mobility** $\mu = e\tau/m_e$ gives $\sigma = ne\mu$ — semiconductor-friendly form.
- ✅ **Mean free path** $\lambda = v_{th}\tau$ — classically ~Å, actually ~10–100 nm.
- ✅ **Wiedemann–Franz law**: $\kappa/(\sigma T) = L$ is a near-universal constant for metals; classical L ≈ 1.11×10⁻⁸, quantum L ≈ 2.44×10⁻⁸ W·Ω·K⁻².
- ✅ **Matthiessen's rule**: $\rho_{total} = \rho_{phonon} + \rho_{impurity}$ — resistances add.
- ✅ **Failures of classical theory**: (i) Heat capacity off by ~100×, (ii) wrong temperature dependence of $\sigma$ (T⁻³/² vs T⁻¹), (iii) wrong mean free path, (iv) wrong sign of Hall coefficient in some metals.
- ✅ **Resolution**: Sommerfeld free electron model with **Fermi–Dirac statistics** (Module 2).
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: Cu, Al wiring; Pt RTDs; nichrome heating elements; EM shielding; PCB design.
- ✅ **Physical constants to memorize**: $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ✅ **Units to memorize**: $\sigma$ in S/m, $\rho$ in Ω·m, $\mu$ in m²/(V·s), $\tau$ in s, $\lambda$ in m.
- ✅ **Examination tip**: Always state the **assumptions** before any derivation, and end with the **limitation** of the classical picture — examiners reward this structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermi-Dirac Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fermi-Dirac Distribution ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Fermi-Dirac Distribution Function** is a probability function that describes the statistical distribution of identical and indistinguishable particles (called **fermions**) over the available energy states of a system in thermal equilibrium at a given absolute temperature $T$. Fermions obey the **Pauli Exclusion Principle**, meaning no two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously. The Fermi-Dirac distribution function gives the **probability** $f(E)$ that an available energy state at energy $E$ will be occupied by a fermion (such as an electron) at absolute temperature $T$: $$f(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T} + 1}$$ where: - $E$ = energy of the quantum state - $E_F$ = **Fermi energy** (a characteristic energy of the system) - $k_B$ = **Boltzmann constant** = $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K}$ - $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Stadium Seat Booking" Model:** > Imagine a stadium with individual numbered seats, each seat being a *unique energy state*. Concert tickets (electrons/fermions) are being distributed. The crucial rule: **one person per seat — no two ticket holders can sit in the same seat** (Pauli Exclusion Principle). > - At **zero temperature (T = 0 K)**, all the lowest-numbered seats are filled from seat #1 upwards until all ticket holders are seated. The highest occupied seat's "ticket price" is the **Fermi Energy** ($E_F$). > - As the temperature **rises**, a few energetic ticket holders in the back rows (near $E_F$) can be "promoted" to slightly higher empty seats using thermal energy, leaving a few empty seats behind. This is the thermal smearing of the distribution. > - The **Fermi-Dirac distribution** $f(E)$ tells you, for any seat number (energy $E$), the probability that it is currently occupied. This distribution governs all the electronic properties of **metals**, **semiconductors**, and forms the foundation of modern **solid-state electronics**, including transistors, diodes, lasers, and integrated circuits — making it the most pivotal equation in Information Science. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why It Matters for Information Science:** > The operation of every semiconductor device — from the simplest LED to complex microprocessors — depends critically on the occupancy of energy states described by the Fermi-Dirac distribution. Without it, no $p$-$n$ junction, no CMOS transistor, no logic gate, and no digital memory would function. ### 1.3 Salient Features of Fermi-Dirac Statistics | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Particles governed** | Fermions (electrons, protons, neutrons, quarks) — particles with half-integer spin | | **Spin quantum number** | $s = 1/2, 3/2, 5/2, \ldots$ | | **Particles per state** | Maximum **one** (Pauli Exclusion Principle) | | **Distinguishability** | Indistinguishable particles | | **Limiting case at high $T$** | Reduces to classical **Maxwell-Boltzmann** distribution | | **Symmetry of wavefunction** | Antisymmetric under particle exchange | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Distribution Function **Step 1 — Understanding $E_F$ (Fermi Energy / Fermi Level)** $E_F$ is the energy at which the probability of occupation is exactly $\mathbf{50\%}$ at any temperature $T > 0 \ \text{K}$. It is **not** a fixed energy level tied to a particular state; it is a reference energy that characterizes the entire electron population of the system. **Step 2 — Behaviour at T = 0 K (Absolute Zero)** At $T = 0 \ \text{K}$: $$f(E) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{for} \ E < E_F \\ \frac{1}{2} & \text{for} \ E = E_F \\ 0 & \text{for} \ E > E_F \end{cases}$$ This is a **step function**: every state below $E_F$ is completely filled, every state above is completely empty. The sharp cutoff at $E = E_F$ is the **Fermi surface**. **Step 3 — Behaviour at T > 0 K (Finite Temperature)** The denominator $e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T}$ becomes significant near $E \approx E_F$. The function transitions smoothly from 1 (deep below $E_F$) through $\mathbf{0.5}$ (at $E = E_F$) to 0 (well above $E_F$). The width of this transition region is approximately $\pm k_B T$ around $E_F$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" of Smearing:** Thermal energy $k_B T$ allows a small fraction of electrons near the top of the filled Fermi sea (within $\sim k_B T$ of $E_F$) to be excited to higher empty states. Below $E_F - k_B T$, the distribution remains essentially unity; above $E_F + k_B T$, essentially zero. **Step 4 — High-Temperature / High-Energy Limit (Classical Limit)** When $E - E_F \gg k_B T$, the $+1$ in the denominator becomes negligible: $$f(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/k_B T} = e^{E_F/k_B T} \cdot e^{-E/k_B T}$$ This is the **Maxwell-Boltzmann (classical) distribution**, showing that Fermi-Dirac statistics correctly reduce to classical statistics when particles are far from the Fermi level. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Fermi-Dirac Distribution** | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T} + 1}$ | Dimensionless probability ($0 \le f \le 1$) | | 2 | **Density of States (3D)** | $g(E) = \dfrac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2}$ | States per unit energy per unit volume | | 3 | **Fermi Energy (3D free electron gas)** | $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\left(3\pi^2 n\right)^{2/3}$ | Joules; $n$ = electron number density (m$^{-3}$) | | 4 | **Fermi Temperature** | $T_F = \dfrac{E_F}{k_B}$ | Kelvin (typically $\sim 10^4$ K for metals) | | 5 | **Fermi Velocity** | $v_F = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_F}{m}}$ | m/s (typically $\sim 10^6$ m/s) | | 6 | **Fermi Wave Vector** | $k_F = (3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}$ | m$^{-1}$ | | 7 | **Average Energy at T = 0 K** | $\langle E \rangle_0 = \dfrac{3}{5} N E_F$ | Joules (per system of $N$ electrons) | | 8 | **Total Energy at T = 0 K** | $U_0 = \dfrac{3}{5} N E_F$ | Joules | | 9 | **Pressure of Degenerate Gas** | $P = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{U_0}{V} = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{5m}(3\pi^2)^{2/3} n^{5/3}$ | Pascals | | 10 | **Carrier Concentration (n-type)** | $n = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T}$ | m$^{-3}$ | | 11 | **Carrier Concentration (p-type)** | $p = N_v \, e^{-(E_F - E_v)/k_B T}$ | m$^{-3}$ | | 12 | **Intrinsic Carrier Conc.** | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/k_B T}$ | m$^{-6}$ | | 13 | **Electron Occupation at $E_F$** | $f(E_F) = 0.5$ (for any $T > 0$) | Always exactly 0.5 at $E = E_F$ | | 14 | **Pauli Exclusion Limit** | $f(E) \le 1$ for all $E$ and $T$ | Direct consequence of indistinguishability | > [!NOTE] > **Constants to memorize for KTU exams:** > - Planck's reduced constant: $\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ > - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K} = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{eV/K}$ > - Free electron mass: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \ \text{kg}$ > - 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | How Fermi-Dirac Statistics is Used | |---|---| | **Semiconductor Devices** | Determines carrier concentrations in $p$-type, $n$-type, and intrinsic semiconductors — the basis of all diodes, transistors, and ICs | | **Metal Conduction** | Explains why electrons in metals have huge zero-point kinetic energy ($\sim$ eV) even at $T = 0$ K | | **White Dwarf Stars** | Electron degeneracy pressure prevents gravitational collapse | | **Laser Diodes** | Population inversion ($f > 0.5$ at higher energy than at lower) requires careful Fermi-Dirac engineering | | **Quantum Computing** | Qubit energy levels and Pauli spin blockade governed by fermion statistics | | **Solar Cells** | Photocurrent depends on quasi-Fermi level splitting under illumination | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Density of States $g(E)$ for a 3D Free Electron Gas **Starting Setup:** Consider an electron of mass $m$ confined in a three-dimensional cubical box of side $L$ with volume $V = L^3$. Apply periodic boundary conditions (Born-von Karman). **Step 1 — Allowed wave vectors in $k$-space** The allowed values of the wave vector components are: $$k_x = \frac{2\pi n_x}{L}, \quad k_y = \frac{2\pi n_y}{L}, \quad k_z = \frac{2\pi n_z}{L}$$ where $n_x, n_y, n_z$ are integers ($0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$). The volume occupied by **one** allowed state in $k$-space is: $$\Delta k = \left(\frac{2\pi}{L}\right)^3 = \frac{8\pi^3}{V}$$ **Step 2 — Number of states within a sphere of radius $k$ in $k$-space** The number of allowed states inside a sphere of radius $k$ (counting spin degeneracy of 2) is: $$N(k) = 2 \times \frac{\text{Volume of sphere in } k\text{-space}}{\text{Volume per state}} = 2 \times \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi k^3}{\frac{8\pi^3}{V}}$$ Simplifying: $$N(k) = \frac{V k^3}{3\pi^2}$$ **Step 3 — Convert from $k$-space to energy $E$** For a free electron, the energy-wavevector relation is: $$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m} \implies k^2 = \frac{2mE}{\hbar^2} \implies k = \frac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar}$$ Cubing both sides: $$k^3 = \frac{(2mE)^{3/2}}{\hbar^3}$$ Substitute into the expression for $N(k)$: $$N(E) = \frac{V}{3\pi^2} \cdot \frac{(2mE)^{3/2}}{\hbar^3}$$ **Step 4 — Take derivative to obtain the density of states $g(E)$** The density of states is defined as the number of states per unit energy range: $$g(E) = \frac{dN(E)}{dE} = \frac{V}{3\pi^2} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{(2m)^{3/2}}{\hbar^3} E^{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{g(E) = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The density of states increases as $\sqrt{E}$ — higher energy levels are more densely packed because they correspond to a thin shell of large radius in $k$-space enclosing a large volume. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Fermi Energy $E_F$ for a Free Electron Gas at $T = 0$ K **Starting Point:** The total number of electrons $N$ in the system is given by integrating the density of states times the occupation probability at $T = 0$ K: $$N = \int_0^{E_F} g(E) \cdot f(E) \, dE$$ At $T = 0$ K, $f(E) = 1$ for $E \le E_F$ and $f(E) = 0$ for $E > E_F$. So: $$N = \int_0^{E_F} g(E) \, dE = \int_0^{E_F} \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2} \, dE$$ **Step 1 — Evaluate the integral** $$N = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \int_0^{E_F} E^{1/2} \, dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} E_F^{3/2}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $E_F$** Express in terms of electron number density $n = N/V$: $$n = \frac{1}{3\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E_F^{3/2}$$ Isolate $E_F^{3/2}$: $$E_F^{3/2} = 3\pi^2 n \left(\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\right)^{3/2}$$ Raise both sides to the power $2/3$: $$\boxed{E_F = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\left(3\pi^2 n\right)^{2/3}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Estimate for a Typical Metal (e.g., Copper)** For copper: $n \approx 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ electrons/m$^3$ $$E_F = \frac{(1.0546 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} \left(3\pi^2 \times 8.5 \times 10^{28}\right)^{2/3}$$ $$E_F \approx 6.11 \times 10^{-39} \times (2.51 \times 10^{30})^{2/3} \approx 6.11 \times 10^{-39} \times 1.84 \times 10^{20}$$ $$E_F \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-18} \ \text{J} \approx 7.0 \ \text{eV}$$ This is consistent with known values for copper ($E_F \approx 7.0$ eV). The corresponding Fermi temperature is: $$T_F = \frac{E_F}{k_B} = \frac{1.12 \times 10^{-18}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 8.1 \times 10^4 \ \text{K}$$ This shows why classical Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics utterly fails for electrons in metals at room temperature — thermal energy $k_B T \approx 0.025$ eV is tiny compared to $E_F \approx 7$ eV. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Average Energy at $T = 0$ K **Step 1 — Total energy integral** $$U_0 = \int_0^{E_F} E \cdot g(E) \, dE = \int_0^{E_F} E \cdot \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2} \, dE$$ $$U_0 = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \int_0^{E_F} E^{3/2} \, dE$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the integral** $$U_0 = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{2}{5} E_F^{5/2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $E_F$ expression and simplify using $n = N/V$** We know from section 3.2 that: $$\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} = \frac{3\pi^2 n}{E_F^{3/2}}$$ So: $$U_0 = \frac{V}{2\pi^2} \cdot \frac{3\pi^2 n}{E_F^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{2}{5} E_F^{5/2} = \frac{3}{5} V n \cdot E_F = \frac{3}{5} N E_F$$ $$\boxed{\langle E \rangle = \frac{U_0}{N} = \frac{3}{5} E_F}$$ **Physical Insight:** The *average* energy of an electron in a degenerate Fermi gas at absolute zero is only $\frac{3}{5}$ of the Fermi energy. This is because most electrons occupy the lower part of the energy spectrum (where $g(E)$ is small), pulling down the mean. --- ### 3.4 Behaviour of $f(E)$ at Special Points **Case 1: When $E = E_F$ (any $T > 0$)** $$f(E_F) = \frac{1}{e^{0} + 1} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = 0.5$$ **Case 2: When $T \to 0$ K, $E < E_F$** $$(E - E_F)/k_B T \to -\infty \implies e^{-\infty} \to 0 \implies f(E) \to \frac{1}{0 + 1} = 1$$ **Case 3: When $T \to 0$ K, $E > E_F$** $$(E - E_F)/k_B T \to +\infty \implies e^{+\infty} \to \infty \implies f(E) \to \frac{1}{\infty + 1} = 0$$ **Case 4: When $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ (Boltzmann Limit)** $$f(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/k_B T} = e^{E_F/k_B T} \cdot e^{-E/k_B T}$$ --- ### 3.5 Derivation of Carrier Concentration in an n-type Semiconductor **Step 1 — Number of electrons in conduction band** $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g(E) f(E) \, dE$$ **Step 2 — Approximation in the Boltzmann tail region** For $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ (which holds for $E \ge E_c$ in a non-degenerate semiconductor): $$f(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/k_B T}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and integrate** For the conduction band, using $E = E_c + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m_e^*}$: $$n = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T}$$ where the **effective density of states** in the conduction band is: $$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 4 — Analogy for p-type** Similarly for holes (which are *bosonic* in nature and follow modified statistics), the hole concentration in the valence band is: $$p = N_v \, e^{-(E_F - E_v)/k_B T}$$ where: $$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 5 — Law of Mass Action** Multiplying $n$ and $p$ (note that $E_F$ cancels out!): $$\boxed{n \cdot p = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/k_B T} = n_i^2}$$ This **fundamental result** is independent of doping — it depends only on the band gap $E_g = E_c - E_v$ and temperature. At $T = 300$ K for silicon, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Fermi-Dirac Statistics Governs Information Devices ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Quantum Mechanical System
Free Electrons in Solid] --> B[Energy Levels Discretized
Box Quantization] B --> C[Pauli Exclusion Principle
Maximum 2 Electrons per State] C --> D[Fermi-Dirac Distribution
f of E equals 1 over exp E minus EF over kT plus 1] D --> E[State Occupancy Probabilities
f of E between 0 and 1] E --> F1[Metal Conduction
Partially Filled Bands] E --> F2[Semiconductor Doping
Donor and Acceptor Levels] E --> F3[Optical Transitions
LEDs and Lasers] E --> F4[Thermoelectric Effects
Seebeck and Peltier] F1 --> G1[Transistors CMOS BJTs] F2 --> G2[Diodes and Junctions] F3 --> G3[Photodetectors and Solar Cells] F4 --> G4[Sensors and Power Devices] G1 --> H[Information Science Hardware] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H H --> I[Modern Computing
Communication and Storage] ``` ### 4.2 Fermi-Dirac Distribution Plot — Behaviour at Different Temperatures ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "T equals 0 K Step Function" S1[Occupancy equals 1
All states below EF filled] --- S2[Sharp cutoff
at E equals EF] --- S3[Occupancy equals 0
Above EF completely empty] end subgraph "Low Temperature T less than TF" L1[Occupancy near 1
Well below EF] --- L2[Smooth transition
Width about kT around EF] --- L3[Occupancy near 0
Well above EF] end subgraph "High Temperature T approaches TF" H1[Occupancy high but less than 1
Below EF] --- H2[Broad smearing
Width many kT] --- H3[Occupancy low but non-zero
Above EF] end subgraph "Classical Limit T much greater than TF" C1[Occupancy small
Exponential decay] --- C2[Approximately Maxwell-Boltzmann
f of E proportional to exp minus E over kT] --- C3[No sharp Fermi edge visible] end ``` ### 4.3 Energy Band Diagram — Position of Fermi Level in Different Materials ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph METAL[Metal Conductor] M1[Conduction Band] --- M2[Fermi Level EF inside conduction band] --- M3[Partially filled states above EF] end subgraph INTRINSIC[Intrinsic Semiconductor] I1[Conduction Band EC] --- I2[Band Gap Eg about 1 eV] --- I3[Valence Band EV] --- I4[Fermi Level EF near middle of gap] end subgraph NTYPE[n-type Semiconductor] N1[Conduction Band EC] --- N2[Donor Level ED] --- N3[Band Gap Eg] --- N4[Valence Band EV] --- N5[Fermi Level EF close to EC] end subgraph PTYPE[p-type Semiconductor] P1[Conduction Band EC] --- P2[Band Gap Eg] --- P3[Acceptor Level EA] --- P4[Valence Band EV] --- P5[Fermi Level EF close to EV] end ``` ### 4.4 Information Flow Chart — From Statistics to Device Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fermi-Dirac Statistics] --> B[State Occupancy f of E] B --> C[Carrier Density n and p] C --> D[Electrical Conductivity sigma] D --> E[Device Current Voltage Characteristics] E --> F[Logic Gates and Amplifiers] F --> G[Microprocessors and Memory] G --> H[All Modern Information Technology] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Fermi-Dirac distribution function. Discuss its behaviour at T = 0 K.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** The Fermi-Dirac distribution function gives the probability of occupation of an energy state $E$ by a fermion in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$: > $$f(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T} + 1}$$ > It applies to identical, indistinguishable particles with half-integer spin (e.g., electrons) that obey the Pauli Exclusion Principle. > [!TIP] > **Behaviour at T = 0 K (2 marks):** > - For $E < E_F$: the exponent $(E - E_F)/k_B T \to -\infty$, so $e^{-\infty} = 0$, giving $f(E) = 1$. All states below $E_F$ are completely filled. > - For $E > E_F$: the exponent $\to +\infty$, so $e^{\infty} = \infty$, giving $f(E) = 0$. All states above $E_F$ are completely empty. > - The function becomes a **step function** with a sharp discontinuity at $E = E_F$, the highest filled energy known as the **Fermi energy**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **What is Fermi energy? Why is it important in semiconductors?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Fermi energy** ($E_F$) is the energy level at which the probability of occupation by an electron is exactly **50%** at any temperature $T > 0$ K. Mathematically, $f(E_F) = 0.5$ for all $T > 0$. It can also be defined as the energy of the highest occupied state at absolute zero. > [!TIP] > **Importance in semiconductors (1.5 marks):** > 1. **Carrier concentration:** The position of $E_F$ relative to the conduction band edge $E_c$ determines electron density: $n = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T}$. A higher $E_F$ (closer to $E_c$) means higher $n$ — n-type doping. > 2. **Doping type identification:** $E_F$ near the valence band indicates p-type; near the conduction band indicates n-type; mid-gap indicates intrinsic. > 3. **p-n junction built-in potential:** The difference in Fermi levels on the two sides of a junction gives the contact potential $V_0 = (E_{F,n} - E_{F,p})/q$, fundamental to all semiconductor devices. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the Fermi energy of a free electron gas in three dimensions at absolute zero. Starting from the density of states, show all the steps of the derivation clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Fermi-Dirac distribution, obtain expressions for the electron and hole concentrations in an n-type semiconductor. Hence derive the law of mass action $np = n_i^2$. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to (a):** **Step 1 — Density of states (recall from Section 3.1):** The density of available quantum states per unit energy for a free electron in 3D is: $$g(E) = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2}$$ **Step 2 — Total number of electrons at T = 0 K:** At $T = 0$ K, the Fermi-Dirac distribution is a step function: $f(E) = 1$ for $E \le E_F$ and $f(E) = 0$ for $E > E_F$. The total number of electrons in the system is: $$N = \int_0^{E_F} g(E) \cdot 1 \, dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \int_0^{E_F} E^{1/2} \, dE$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$N = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} E_F^{3/2} = \frac{V}{3\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m E_F}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $E_F$ in terms of electron density $n = N/V$:** $$n = \frac{1}{3\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m E_F}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ $$3\pi^2 n = \left(\frac{2m E_F}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ Raising both sides to the power $2/3$: $$(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3} = \frac{2m E_F}{\hbar^2}$$ $$\boxed{E_F = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m}(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical value for typical metal (Cu, $n = 8.5 \times 10^{28}$/m$^3$):** $$E_F = \frac{(1.0546 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2(9.11 \times 10^{-31})}(3\pi^2 \times 8.5 \times 10^{28})^{2/3}$$ $$E_F \approx 7.0 \ \text{eV} \quad \text{and} \quad T_F = E_F/k_B \approx 8.1 \times 10^4 \ \text{K}$$ --- **Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Electron concentration in conduction band:** The number of electrons per unit volume occupying states in the conduction band (above $E_c$) is: $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g(E) f(E) \, dE$$ For a non-degenerate semiconductor, $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ in the conduction band, so the Boltzmann approximation applies: $$f(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/k_B T}$$ Substituting the density of states and integrating yields: $$\boxed{n = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T}}$$ where $N_c = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ is the effective density of states. **Step 2 — Hole concentration in valence band:** The probability that a state at energy $E$ in the valence band is **empty** is $1 - f(E)$. In the valence band, $E_F - E \gg k_B T$, so: $$1 - f(E) = 1 - \frac{1}{e^{(E-E_F)/k_B T}+1} = \frac{e^{(E-E_F)/k_B T}}{e^{(E-E_F)/k_B T}+1} \approx e^{(E-E_F)/k_B T}$$ The hole concentration is: $$\boxed{p = N_v \, e^{-(E_F - E_v)/k_B T}}$$ where $N_v = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$. **Step 3 — Derivation of law of mass action:** Multiply the two concentrations: $$np = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T} \cdot e^{-(E_F - E_v)/k_B T}$$ $$np = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_v)/k_B T} = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/k_B T}$$ The Fermi level cancels out completely! Defining the **intrinsic carrier concentration** $n_i$ as the value of $n$ (or $p$) in an undoped semiconductor, we obtain: $$\boxed{n \cdot p = n_i^2 = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/k_B T}}$$ This **law of mass action** is temperature-dependent (through $T^{3}$ in $N_c, N_v$ and the exponential) but is independent of the Fermi level position. It is a fundamental result of semiconductor physics. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the Fermi-Dirac distribution function using statistical mechanics. Discuss its limiting cases at $T = 0$ K and at high temperatures. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with neat sketches how the Fermi-Dirac distribution function $f(E)$ varies with temperature for a free electron gas. Compute the Fermi energy, Fermi temperature, and Fermi velocity for copper, given that the conduction electron density is $8.5 \times 10^{28}$/m$^3$. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to (a):** **Step 1 — Grand canonical ensemble approach** Consider a system of $N$ identical fermions in thermal equilibrium with a heat bath at temperature $T$. The probability of finding the system in a state with energy $E_s$ and particle number $N_s$ is: $$P_s = \frac{1}{Z_G} e^{-\beta(E_s - \mu N_s)}$$ where $\beta = 1/k_B T$, $\mu$ is the chemical potential, and $Z_G$ is the grand partition function. **Step 2 — Single-particle state occupation** For a single quantum state of energy $E_i$, the possible occupation numbers are $n_i = 0$ (empty) or $n_i = 1$ (occupied, by Pauli exclusion). The grand partition function for a single state is: $$\mathcal{Z}_i = \sum_{n_i=0,1} e^{-\beta(E_i - \mu)n_i} = 1 + e^{-\beta(E_i - \mu)}$$ **Step 3 — Average occupation** The average occupation of state $i$ is: $$\langle n_i \rangle = \frac{1 \cdot e^{-\beta(E_i-\mu)} \cdot 1}{1 + e^{-\beta(E_i-\mu)}} = \frac{1}{e^{\beta(E_i-\mu)} + 1}$$ Identifying $\mu$ with the Fermi energy $E_F$ in the context of electrons in a solid, we get the Fermi-Dirac distribution: $$\boxed{f(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T} + 1}}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting cases** > [!TIP] > **Case (i) — $T = 0$ K:** > - For $E < E_F$: $(E - E_F)/k_B T \to -\infty$, $f(E) \to 1$ (state filled). > - For $E > E_F$: $(E - E_F)/k_B T \to +\infty$, $f(E) \to 0$ (state empty). > - Result: Perfect step function — the **Fermi sea** is fully filled up to $E_F$. > [!TIP] > **Case (ii) — High temperature, $E - E_F \gg k_B T$:** > The exponential dominates the $+1$ in the denominator: > $$f(E) \approx e^{-(E-E_F)/k_B T} = A \cdot e^{-E/k_B T}$$ > This is the **Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution** with normalization constant $A = e^{E_F/k_B T}$. The Fermi-Dirac statistics reduce smoothly to classical statistics when particles are far above the Fermi level (e.g., electrons in the conduction band of an intrinsic semiconductor). --- **Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Sketch description (refer to Section 4.2)** The Fermi-Dirac distribution $f(E)$ versus $E$ exhibits: - At $T = 0$ K: a perfect **step function** dropping from 1 to 0 at $E = E_F$. - At finite $T$: a **smooth sigmoid** transitioning through $f = 0.5$ at $E = E_F$ over a width of approximately $\pm k_B T$. - The total number of electrons is conserved, so the area under the curve remains constant — electrons are transferred from just below $E_F$ to just above $E_F$ as temperature rises. - As $T \to T_F$ (Fermi temperature), smearing becomes significant; for $T \gg T_F$, the function becomes a broad exponential decay (classical limit). **Step 2 — Numerical computations for copper** Given: $n = 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ electrons/m$^3$, $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **Fermi Energy:** $$E_F = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m}(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3}$$ $$3\pi^2 n = 3 \times 9.87 \times 8.5 \times 10^{28} = 2.517 \times 10^{30} \ \text{m}^{-3}$$ $$(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3} = (2.517 \times 10^{30})^{2/3} = 1.84 \times 10^{20} \ \text{m}^{-2}$$ $$E_F = \frac{(1.0546 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} \times 1.84 \times 10^{20}$$ $$E_F = \frac{1.112 \times 10^{-68}}{1.822 \times 10^{-30}} \times 1.84 \times 10^{20} = 6.10 \times 10^{-39} \times 1.84 \times 10^{20}$$ $$\boxed{E_F \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-18} \ \text{J} \approx 7.0 \ \text{eV}}$$ **Fermi Temperature:** $$T_F = \frac{E_F}{k_B} = \frac{1.12 \times 10^{-18}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 8.12 \times 10^4 \ \text{K}$$ $$\boxed{T_F \approx 8.1 \times 10^4 \ \text{K}}$$ **Fermi Velocity:** $$v_F = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_F}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.12 \times 10^{-18}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$ $$v_F = \sqrt{2.46 \times 10^{12}} = 1.57 \times 10^6 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$\boxed{v_F \approx 1.57 \times 10^6 \ \text{m/s}}$$ This is approximately **0.5% of the speed of light** — electrons in copper are zipping around at relativistic-adjacent speeds even at $T = 0$ K, a purely quantum-mechanical effect with no classical analogue. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Marks Lost Here!):** > 1. **Forgetting the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in the density of states derivation — costs ~2 marks. Always multiply by 2 because each $k$-state holds spin-up and spin-down electrons. > 2. **Mixing up electron and hole concentration formulas** — remember, $n$ depends on $(E_c - E_F)$ and $p$ on $(E_F - E_v)$, not the reverse. The exponent must be positive. > 3. **Forgetting that $E_F$ cancels out** in the law of mass action — the final result $np = n_i^2$ is independent of Fermi level position, a fact examiners love to test. > 4. **Numerical calculation errors** in $E_F$ for copper — keep at least 3 significant figures and report the answer in both Joules and eV. The expected value is ~7.0 eV. > 5. **Not stating the Pauli Exclusion Principle explicitly** when discussing Fermi-Dirac statistics — it is the physical reason for the $+1$ in the denominator and must be mentioned. > 6. **Confusing the chemical potential $\mu$ with the Fermi energy $E_F$** — they are equal only at $T = 0$ K exactly, but the KTU convention is to use $E_F$ throughout. State this assumption clearly in derivations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fermi-Dirac Distribution (Module 1)** - **Core Equation:** $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{e^{(E - E_F)/k_B T} + 1}$ — the probability of finding a fermion in a state of energy $E$. - **Key Identity:** $f(E_F) = 0.5$ at *any* temperature $T > 0$ — Fermi level is the 50% occupancy point. - **T = 0 K Limiting Behaviour:** $f(E) = 1$ for $E < E_F$ (filled), $f(E) = 0$ for $E > E_F$ (empty) — perfect step function. - **Classical Limit:** When $E - E_F \gg k_B T$, $f(E) \to e^{E_F/k_B T} \cdot e^{-E/k_B T}$ — reduces to Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. - **Density of States (3D):** $g(E) = \dfrac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} E^{1/2}$ — grows as $\sqrt{E}$. - **Fermi Energy Formula:** $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3}$ — depends on electron density $n$, *not* on temperature. - **Average Energy at T = 0 K:** $\langle E \rangle = \dfrac{3}{5} E_F$ per electron. - **Carrier Concentrations in Semiconductors:** - Electrons: $n = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T}$ - Holes: $p = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/k_B T}$ - **Law of Mass Action:** $n \cdot p = n_i^2 = N_c N_v e^{-E_g/k_B T}$ — **independent of doping and Fermi level**. - **Constants to Memorize:** - $\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K - $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg - 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J - **Typical Magnitudes for Metals (Cu):** $E_F \approx 7$ eV, $T_F \approx 8 \times 10^4$ K, $v_F \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ m/s. - **Physical Insight:** Because $T_F \gg T_{\text{room}}$, metals are highly **degenerate** — their electrons are in a quantum regime that classical physics cannot describe. - **Application Domains:** All semiconductor devices (transistors, diodes, solar cells, lasers, LEDs), metal conductivity, white dwarf stars, quantum computers, thermoelectrics. - **Pauli Exclusion Principle:** Maximum **one fermion per quantum state** (with spin) — the fundamental reason the distribution function never exceeds 1.

topics.title: Variation of Fermi Function with Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variation of Fermi Function with Temperature

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fermi-Dirac Distribution Function** (commonly called the **Fermi Function**) gives the probability that a quantum state with energy $E$ will be occupied by a fermion (e.g., an electron) in a system of identical fermions in thermal equilibrium at absolute temperature $T$. The formal KTU 2024 Scheme expression is:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) + 1}$$

where:
- $E_F$ is the **Fermi Energy** (the energy of the highest occupied quantum state at absolute zero, $T = 0\,\text{K}$).
- $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ is the **Boltzmann Constant**.
- $T$ is the **Absolute Temperature** in Kelvin.
- $E$ is the energy of the quantum state under consideration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Fermi function governs the occupancy statistics of electrons in solids, the working of semiconductor devices, the origin of electrical conduction, and the thermal behavior of metals and insulators. It is the single most important equation in solid-state electronics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Theatre Seats Model

Imagine a huge theatre with thousands of seats, where:
- Each **seat** = one quantum energy state.
- Each **audience member** = one electron.
- The **audience** is governed by an unbreakable rule: **one person, one seat** (Pauli's Exclusion Principle).
- The **ticket price** for each seat = the energy $E$ of that state.
- The audience members fill the cheapest seats first, and only when all lower-energy seats are full do they start filling higher-energy ones.

At $T = 0\,\text{K}$ (a cold, empty theatre with no one moving): the audience fills every seat from the cheapest (lowest energy) up to a certain cut-off row — the **Fermi level**. Above this row, the theatre is **completely empty**.

As $T$ increases (the theatre warms up and people start moving around): some audience members near the cut-off row become thermally excited and **shift to slightly higher-priced seats** (higher energy), leaving a few seats empty below $E_F$ and occupying a few seats above $E_F$. The originally sharp boundary becomes a **soft, blurred transition zone** of width roughly $\pm k_B T$ centered on $E_F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The temperature does not affect states deep inside the energy band — only those within about $\pm 3 k_B T$ of $E_F$ see any meaningful change in occupation probability. This is why solids are "stable" and the electronic structure does not collapse on heating.

## 1.3 Behaviour at the Two Extreme Limits

**Case 1: $T \to 0\,\text{K}$**

For $E < E_F$: the exponent $(E - E_F)/k_B T \to -\infty$, so $\exp(-\infty) \to 0$, giving $f(E) = 1$ (state fully occupied).
For $E > E_F$: the exponent $\to +\infty$, so $f(\infty) \to 0$, giving $f(E) = 0$ (state completely empty).
The function reduces to a **sharp step function** at $E = E_F$.

**Case 2: $T \to \infty$ (or $E \gg E_F$)**

For energies well above $E_F$, the "+1" in the denominator becomes negligible and $f(E) \approx \exp[-(E - E_F)/k_B T]$, which is the **classical Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution**. This confirms that classical statistics is a limiting case of Fermi-Dirac statistics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fermi Temperature** $T_F = E_F / k_B$ quantifies how "hot" the electron sea is. For typical metals, $T_F \sim 10^4$–$10^5\,\text{K}$. Since room temperature ($T \approx 300\,\text{K}$) is far smaller than $T_F$, the smearing $\sim k_B T$ is only a tiny fraction of the total Fermi energy.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposed Mathematical Form

The Fermi function is a product of physical reasoning. Let us examine the **exponent** term by term:

$$\alpha(E, T) = \frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}$$

- **Numerator $(E - E_F)$:** Measures how far the state energy $E$ is from the Fermi level, in joules.
- **Denominator $(k_B T)$:** Is the characteristic thermal energy scale at temperature $T$.
- **Ratio $\alpha$:** A dimensionless quantity that compares the **"energy gap from the Fermi level"** to the **"available thermal energy"**.

If $\alpha \gg 1$ (i.e., $E \gg E_F$ or $T$ is very low): the state is essentially unoccupied.
If $\alpha \ll 0$ (i.e., $E \ll E_F$): the state is essentially fully occupied.
If $\alpha \approx 0$ (i.e., $E \approx E_F$): the occupation is $\sim 50\%$ and the function is most sensitive to temperature.

## 2.2 Logical Steps Behind the Variation

1. **Start with $T = 0\,\text{K}$:** A perfect step at $E_F$. All states below are filled, all above are empty.
2. **Increase $T$ to a small but finite value:** The step does not stay sharp. Electrons with energy just below $E_F$ can absorb thermal energy $k_B T$ and jump to states just above $E_F$.
3. **The transition region broadens:** The width of the "smear" is set by thermal energy — approximately $\pm k_B T$ around $E_F$.
4. **Conservation of particle number:** The total number of electrons is constant. Whatever is lost from just below $E_F$ appears just above $E_F$. The curve remains antisymmetric about the point $(E_F, 1/2)$.
5. **At very high $T$ (rare in solids):** The transition widens, but states far from $E_F$ are still essentially unaltered.

## 2.3 The "5$k_B T$ Rule" (KTU High-Yield)

In a finite-temperature Fermi function, the **thermal smearing** is conventionally taken as $\pm 3 k_B T$ (or sometimes $\pm 2.5 k_B T$) on either side of $E_F$. Numerical landmark values:

| Energy offset from $E_F$ | Approximate $f(E)$ value |
|---|---|
| $-5\,k_B T$ | $\approx 0.9933$ (essentially 1) |
| $-3\,k_B T$ | $\approx 0.9526$ |
| $-k_B T$ | $\approx 0.7311$ |
| $0$ (at $E_F$) | $0.5000$ exactly |
| $+k_B T$ | $\approx 0.2689$ |
| $+3\,k_B T$ | $\approx 0.0474$ |
| $+5\,k_B T$ | $\approx 0.0067$ (essentially 0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Only $\sim 1\%$ of the conduction electrons participate in thermal excitation at room temperature. Yet these few electrons are responsible for all temperature-dependent electronic properties — conductivity, Seebeck effect, specific heat of metals (the famous $C_V \propto T$ law).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) + 1}$ | Master Fermi-Dirac equation |
| 2 | $f(E_F) = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | At the Fermi level, occupation is exactly 50% |
| 3 | $f(E) + f(-E + 2E_F) = 1$ | Particle-hole symmetry about $E_F$ |
| 4 | $1 - f(E) = \dfrac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_F - E}{k_B T}\right) + 1}$ | Probability the state is empty |
| 5 | $T_F = \dfrac{E_F}{k_B}$ | Fermi temperature (very high for metals) |
| 6 | $k_B T_F = E_F$ | Equipartition at the Fermi level |
| 7 | $\Delta E_{\text{smear}} \approx \pm 3 k_B T$ | Width of thermal smearing |
| 8 | $f(E) \to \exp[-(E - E_F)/k_B T]$ for $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ | Classical Maxwell-Boltzmann limit |
| 9 | $f(E) \to 1$ for $E < E_F$ at $T = 0$ | Zero-temperature filled state |
| 10 | $f(E) \to 0$ for $E > E_F$ at $T = 0$ | Zero-temperature empty state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars (`|`) were used in any cell above to preserve markdown table integrity.** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ in LaTeX whenever an absolute value is needed in a table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Physics:** The Fermi function determines the carrier concentration in p-n junctions, BJTs, MOSFETs. Position of $E_F$ inside the band gap controls whether a material is n-type, p-type, or intrinsic.
- **Thermionic Emission (Richardson's Law):** Electrons emitted from a hot filament follow the high-energy tail of the Fermi function.
- **Specific Heat of Metals:** Only electrons within $\sim k_B T$ of $E_F$ contribute, giving the famous linear $C_V = \gamma T$ law, which classical theory catastrophically failed to predict.
- **Tunnel Diodes and STM:** Tunneling current depends exponentially on the overlap of filled and empty states, which is set by $f(E)$.
- **Solar Cells and Photodiodes:** Photo-excited carriers modify the quasi-Fermi level splitting that drives the open-circuit voltage.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fermi Function from Statistical Mechanics

We want to find the most probable distribution of $N$ **indistinguishable fermions** (e.g., electrons) among $g$ energy states, such that the total number and total energy are conserved.

### Step 1 — Set up the combinatorial count

The number of microstates $W$ for distributing $N$ particles into levels $i$ (with energy $E_i$, degeneracy $g_i$, and occupation $n_i$) is, considering Pauli exclusion ($n_i \le g_i$):

$$W = \prod_i \binom{g_i}{n_i} = \prod_i \frac{g_i!}{n_i!\,(g_i - n_i)!}$$

### Step 2 — Maximise the entropy using Lagrange multipliers

Entropy is $S = k_B \ln W$. For the **most probable distribution**, we maximise $W$ subject to two constraints:

$$\sum_i n_i = N \quad \text{(particle conservation)}$$

$$\sum_i n_i E_i = E_{\text{total}} \quad \text{(energy conservation)}$$

Take $\ln W$ and apply Stirling's approximation $\ln(x!) \approx x \ln x - x$ (valid for large $g_i, n_i$):

$$\ln W \approx \sum_i \bigl[g_i \ln g_i - n_i \ln n_i - (g_i - n_i) \ln(g_i - n_i)\bigr]$$

### Step 3 — Apply variational calculus with Lagrange multipliers $\alpha$ and $\beta$

Differentiating $\ln W$ with respect to $n_i$ while enforcing constraints:

$$\frac{\partial \ln W}{\partial n_i} = \ln\!\left(\frac{g_i - n_i}{n_i}\right) = \alpha + \beta E_i$$

> **Logic:** The derivative of $\alpha \sum n_i$ gives $\alpha$, and the derivative of $\beta \sum n_i E_i$ gives $\beta E_i$. Both must equal the variation of $\ln W$.

### Step 4 — Solve for $n_i / g_i$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{g_i - n_i}{n_i} = \exp(\alpha + \beta E_i)$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{n_i}{g_i} = \frac{1}{\exp(\alpha + \beta E_i) + 1}$$

### Step 5 — Identify the physical constants

- Comparing with thermodynamics, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{k_B T}$.
- Setting $f(E_i) = n_i / g_i$ and defining $E_F = -\alpha k_B T$, we obtain:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) + 1}$$

This is the celebrated **Fermi-Dirac distribution function**.

## 3.2 Detailed Behaviour of $f(E)$ at Various Temperatures

Let us now analyse how $f(E)$ explicitly varies. We use normalised energy $\eta = (E - E_F)/(k_B T_F)$ and temperature ratio $t = T / T_F$. For copper-like metal, $T_F \approx 8.2 \times 10^4\,\text{K}$, so at $T = 300\,\text{K}$, $t \approx 0.0037$.

| Temperature condition | Energy offset $(E - E_F)$ | Value of $f(E)$ | Physical meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T = 0\,\text{K}$, $E < E_F$ | $< 0$ | $1$ | All states below $E_F$ filled |
| $T = 0\,\text{K}$, $E = E_F$ | $0$ | Step discontinuity | Fermi level at $T = 0$ |
| $T = 0\,\text{K}$, $E > E_F$ | $> 0$ | $0$ | All states above $E_F$ empty |
| $T = 0.1\,T_F$, $E = E_F$ | $0$ | $0.5$ | Exactly half-filled at $E_F$ |
| $T = 0.1\,T_F$, $E = E_F + 3 k_B T$ | $> 0$ | $\approx 0.047$ | Small population above $E_F$ |
| $T = 0.1\,T_F$, $E = E_F - 3 k_B T$ | $< 0$ | $\approx 0.953$ | Small depopulation below $E_F$ |
| $T \to \infty$ | $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ | $\exp[-(E - E_F)/k_B T]$ | Maxwell-Boltzmann limit |

## 3.3 Symmetry Property of the Fermi Function (Detailed Proof)

The Fermi function possesses a beautiful **electron-hole symmetry** about the point $(E_F, 0.5)$:

$$f(E_F + \delta) = 1 - f(E_F - \delta)$$

**Proof (Algebraic, every step shown):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(E_F + \delta) &= \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{k_B T}\right) + 1} \\[8pt]
1 - f(E_F - \delta) &= 1 - \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\delta}{k_B T}\right) + 1} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\delta}{k_B T}\right) + 1 - 1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\delta}{k_B T}\right) + 1} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\delta}{k_B T}\right)}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\delta}{k_B T}\right) + 1} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{k_B T}\right)} \\[8pt]
&= f(E_F + \delta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the result. **Geometric interpretation:** the curve is centrally symmetric (in the rotated sense) about the point where $E = E_F$ and $f = 0.5$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** For a metal with $E_F = 5.0\,\text{eV}$, calculate the probability of finding an electron in a state with energy $E = 5.05\,\text{eV}$ at $T = 300\,\text{K}$.

**Solution:**

Step (i): Compute $k_B T$ at room temperature.

$$k_B T = (8.617 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{eV/K})(300\,\text{K}) = 0.02585\,\text{eV} \approx 0.026\,\text{eV}$$

Step (ii): Compute the dimensionless exponent.

$$\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T} = \frac{5.05 - 5.00}{0.02585} = \frac{0.05}{0.02585} \approx 1.934$$

Step (iii): Evaluate the Fermi function.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(E) &= \frac{1}{e^{1.934} + 1} \\[6pt]
e^{1.934} &\approx 6.91 \\[6pt]
f(E) &\approx \frac{1}{6.91 + 1} = \frac{1}{7.91} \approx 0.126
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A state just $0.05\,\text{eV}$ above the Fermi level has only a $\sim 12.6\%$ chance of being occupied at room temperature. This numerical smallness explains why the thermal smearing, although conceptually important, is quantitatively tiny in real metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse check (KTU Examiner Pattern):** Use the symmetry relation $1 - f(E_F - \delta) = f(E_F + \delta)$. So $f(4.95\,\text{eV}) = 1 - 0.126 = 0.874$. Always verify your answer this way!

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Variation of the Fermi Function with Temperature (Mermaid Graph)

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the evolution of the Fermi curve from $T = 0\,\text{K}$ to a moderately high temperature, mapping the occupation probability at the Fermi level and the smearing width.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: T equals 0 K"]:::stepA --> B["Fermi function is a step at E sub F"]
    B --> C["f of E equals 1 for E less than E sub F"]
    B --> D["f of E equals 0 for E greater than E sub F"]
    C --> E["T rises to small finite value"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Smear width of about plus or minus 3 k sub B T around E sub F"]
    F --> G["f at E sub F equals 0.5 exactly at all T greater than 0"]
    F --> H["Antisymmetric about point E sub F, 0.5"]
    G --> I["States well below E sub F: f still about 1"]
    G --> J["States well above E sub F: f still about 0"]
    F --> K["T approaches T sub F, function broadens widely"]
    K --> L["For E minus E sub F much greater than k sub B T, Maxwell Boltzmann limit applies"]
    L --> M["End: Full temperature behaviour mapped"]

    classDef stepA fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#2c5aa0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a1d3a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart traces how a step function at $T = 0$ gradually broadens into a smooth S-shaped curve as $T$ increases, while preserving the special point $f(E_F) = 0.5$.

## 4.2 Schematic of Thermal Electron Excitation Across the Fermi Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E_band["Energy axis E in eV"]
        A1["State at E sub F minus 3 k sub B T"]:::filled
        A2["State at E sub F"]:::half
        A3["State at E sub F plus 3 k sub B T"]:::empty
    end

    A1 -- "electron absorbs thermal energy k sub B T" --> A3
    A2 -- "f of E sub F equals 0.5 always" --> A2
    A3 -- "occupying probability f of E" --> A3

    classDef filled fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    classDef half fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef empty fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Interpretation (left to right):** A previously filled state just below $E_F$ loses an electron, which is thermally excited into a previously empty state just above $E_F$. The state precisely at $E_F$ is half-filled at all $T > 0$, which is the **fingerprint of the Fermi level**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Temperature Dependence Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["Input: T and E sub F and E"]:::input --> N2["Compute dimensionless ratio, E minus E sub F over k sub B T"]:::proc
    N2 --> N3["Exponentiate: exp of the ratio"]:::proc
    N3 --> N4["Add 1 to the exponential"]:::proc
    N4 --> N5["Reciprocal: 1 over exp plus 1"]:::proc
    N5 --> N6["Output: f of E equals occupation probability"]:::output
    N2 --> N7["Decision: Is T zero?"]:::decide
    N7 -- Yes --> N8["Output: Step function at E sub F"]:::special
    N7 -- No --> N2

    classDef input fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000

```
