```mermaid
    D --> E["Region III: Transmitted wave emerges with high amplitude"]
    B -- "No, E less than V0" --> F["Region I: Incident plus reflected oscillatory wave"]
    F --> G["Region II: Wave function decays exponentially as exp of minus kappa x"]
    G --> H{"Barrier width a times kappa greater than 1?"}
    H -- "Yes, thick barrier" --> I["Region III: Tiny transmitted amplitude, T near zero"]
    H -- "No, thin barrier" --> J["Region III: Measurable transmitted wave, T finite"]
    I --> K["Engineering: Alpha decay, Fusion in stars"]
    J --> L["Engineering Applications"]
    L --> M["Scanning Tunnelling Microscope STM"]
    L --> N["Tunnel Diode Esaki"]
    L --> O["Flash Memory Floating Gate MOSFET"]
    L --> P["Josephson Junction"]
    E --> Q["Engineering: Classical optics, No tunnelling needed"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Wave Function Behaviour Across the Barrier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG1["REGION I  x less than 0  Free Particle"]
        R1A["Incident wave A_I exp i k x moves right"] 
        R1B["Reflected wave A_R exp minus i k x moves left"]
    end
    subgraph REG2["REGION II  0 to a  Inside Barrier"]
        R2A["Decaying part B exp minus kappa x"]
        R2B["Growing part C exp kappa x suppressed for thick a"]
    end
    subgraph REG3["REGION III  x greater than a  Transmitted"]
        R3A["Transmitted wave A_T exp i k x moves right"]
    end
    REG1 -- "Continuity at x=0" --> REG2
    REG2 -- "Continuity at x=a" --> REG3
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Three-Class Comparison (Energy Regimes)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Potential V of x for rectangular barrier"] --> S1["Region x less than 0: V equals 0"]
    S0 --> S2["Region 0 to a: V equals V0, barrier height"]
    S0 --> S3["Region x greater than a: V equals 0"]
    P1["Case 1: E greater than V0, classical transmission, T near 1"] --> OUT1["Wave oscillatory in all three regions"]
    P2["Case 2: E less than V0, QUANTUM TUNNELLING, T small but nonzero"] --> OUT2["Decay in Region II, survives in Region III"]
    P3["Case 3: E equals V0, marginal case, T about 0.5"] --> OUT3["Linear behaviour in Region II"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: Trace the particle's journey from left to right. Whenever the path enters a green-shaded (free) region, the wave is sinusoidal; inside the red-shaded (barrier) region, it decays. The output of Region III tells you how much of the wave successfully "leaked" through.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define quantum mechanical tunnelling. State two conditions under which tunnelling is observed.**

**Model Answer**:
Quantum mechanical tunnelling is the quantum phenomenon in which a particle with energy $E$ less than the potential barrier height $V_0$ has a finite probability of crossing the barrier and appearing on the other side, contrary to classical mechanics.

**Conditions**:
1. The particle's energy must be **less than** the barrier height: $E < V_0$.
2. The barrier width must be **comparable to or smaller than** the de Broglie wavelength of the particle (typically of the order of nanometres or less for electrons).
3. (Bonus) The particle's mass must be sufficiently small (tunnelling is more pronounced for light particles like electrons than for heavy particles).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Why does an alpha particle escape from a nucleus even though its energy is less than the nuclear potential barrier? Mention the phenomenon involved.**

**Model Answer**:
An alpha particle inside a heavy nucleus is bound by a deep potential well, while outside the nucleus it experiences the repulsive Coulomb potential of the daughter nucleus. The combined potential forms a barrier whose height is greater than the alpha particle's kinetic energy. According to classical mechanics, the alpha particle should be permanently trapped. However, due to the **quantum mechanical tunnelling** phenomenon, the wave function of the alpha particle has a finite amplitude outside the barrier, giving a small but non-zero probability of escape per unit time. This is the mechanism behind **alpha decay** (explained by Gamow, Condon, and Gurney in 1928).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) Discuss quantum mechanical tunnelling through a rectangular potential barrier. Derive the qualitative expression for the transmission coefficient. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Setup of the rectangular barrier**:
Consider a particle of mass $m$ and energy $E < V_0$ incident from the left on a rectangular barrier of height $V_0$ and width $a$ extending from $x = 0$ to $x = a$.

The potential is:

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ V_0, & 0 \le x \le a \\ 0, & x > a \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Time-independent Schrödinger equation in each region**:

For $x < 0$ (Region I):
$$\frac{d^2\psi_I}{dx^2} + k^2\psi_I = 0, \quad k = \frac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar}$$

For $0 \le x \le a$ (Region II):
$$\frac{d^2\psi_{II}}{dx^2} - \kappa^2\psi_{II} = 0, \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

For $x > a$ (Region III): same as Region I.

**Step 3 — General solutions**:

$$\psi_I(x) = A_I e^{ikx} + A_R e^{-ikx}$$
$$\psi_{II}(x) = B e^{\kappa x} + C e^{-\kappa x}$$
$$\psi_{III}(x) = A_T e^{ikx}$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions**: Continuity of $\psi$ and $\psi'$ at $x = 0$ and $x = a$ yields four equations that determine the four ratios of the coefficients.

**Step 5 — Transmission coefficient (opaque-barrier limit)**:
For $\kappa a \gg 1$, the algebra simplifies to give the standard qualitative result:

$$T = \frac{\vert A_T\vert^2}{\vert A_I\vert^2} \approx e^{-2\kappa a}$$

where
$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

This shows that tunnelling probability falls off **exponentially** with both barrier width and the square root of barrier height.

#### (b) Explain the working of a Scanning Tunnelling Microscope (STM) based on the principle of quantum tunnelling. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Principle**: When a sharp conducting tip (usually tungsten or Pt-Ir) is brought very close (~0.5–1 nm) to a conducting sample surface, the wave functions of the tip's electrons and the sample's electrons overlap across the narrow vacuum gap. A small bias voltage (typically 10 mV – 1 V) applied between tip and sample causes electrons to **tunnel** through the vacuum barrier.

**Tunnelling current**: The current depends exponentially on the gap width $d$:

$$I_T \propto V \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$$

where $\kappa = \sqrt{2m\phi}/\hbar$ and $\phi$ is the average work function (~ a few eV). Because of the exponential dependence, a change in $d$ of just **0.1 nm** changes the current by roughly a factor of 10 — providing **sub-Ångström vertical resolution**.

**Modes of operation**:

1. **Constant-height mode**: The tip is scanned at a fixed height; the tunnelling current is recorded as a function of lateral position, producing a contour map of electron density.
2. **Constant-current mode**: A feedback loop adjusts the tip height to keep $I_T$ constant, producing a topographical map of the surface. This is the most common mode.

**Achievements**: The STM, invented by **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer in 1981** (Nobel Prize 1986), has achieved **atomic-scale resolution**, enabling the imaging and even manipulation of individual atoms (e.g., the famous IBM logo written with xenon atoms in 1989).

**Resolution**: Lateral ~0.1 nm, vertical ~0.01 nm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain qualitatively the phenomenon of tunnelling. Discuss how the transmission coefficient depends on the barrier parameters. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Qualitative explanation**:
A particle of mass $m$ and energy $E$ is incident on a potential barrier of height $V_0$ and width $a$. Classically, if $E < V_0$, the particle cannot cross the barrier. Quantum mechanically, however, the particle has an associated de Broglie wave of wavelength $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2mE}$. When this wave strikes the barrier, it does not abruptly vanish; it penetrates into the barrier and decays exponentially. If the barrier is thin enough, a measurable amplitude of the wave emerges on the far side — this is **tunnelling**.

**Dependence on barrier parameters**:
The transmission coefficient is approximately:

$$T \approx e^{-2\kappa a}, \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

The qualitative trends are:
1. **Barrier width $a$**: $T$ decreases **exponentially** as $a$ increases. Halving $a$ increases $T$ by a factor $e^{\kappa a}$.
2. **Barrier height $(V_0 - E)$**: $T$ decreases as $V_0 - E$ increases. A taller barrier is "harder" to tunnel through.
3. **Particle mass $m$**: $T$ decreases as $m$ increases. Electrons tunnel much more easily than protons or heavier particles.
4. **Particle energy $E$**: $T$ increases as $E$ approaches $V_0$ from below. At $E = V_0$, the barrier effectively vanishes.

#### (b) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a tunnel diode. Mention its key feature. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Construction**:
A tunnel diode (Esaki diode) is a heavily doped p-n junction diode. The doping is so high (degenerate semiconductor) that the depletion region is extremely thin — typically **10 nm or less**. Both the p-side and n-side have their Fermi levels inside the conduction and valence bands, respectively.

**Energy band diagram and operation**:

**Forward bias = 0 (Equilibrium)**: The Fermi levels on both sides are aligned. The filled states on the n-side are at the same energy as empty states on the p-side across the thin depletion region. No net current flows.

**Small forward bias (eV ≈ peak of valence band)**: Electrons from the conduction band of the n-side tunnel through the thin barrier into empty states of the p-side valence band. **Tunnelling current is maximum** — this corresponds to the **peak current $I_P$** of the diode.

**Increased forward bias**: The relative shift in Fermi levels reduces the overlap between filled and empty states. Tunnelling current **decreases**, producing a region of **negative differential resistance (NDR)**.

**Higher forward bias**: The diode operates in the normal diffusion mode. Current increases with voltage (valley point $I_V$, then rises sharply).

**Reverse bias**: Electrons tunnel from the filled valence band of the p-side to the empty conduction band of the n-side. Reverse current rises steeply.

**Key feature**: The tunnel diode exhibits **negative differential resistance** in the region between the peak voltage $V_P$ and the valley voltage $V_V$. This unique feature makes it useful in:
- High-frequency oscillators (up to several GHz)
- Microwave mixers and detectors
- High-speed switching circuits

**Advantage**: Very fast switching speed due to tunnelling being a near-instantaneous quantum process (no minority-carrier storage delay).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $T$ for $E > V_0$ and $E < V_0$**: Many students write $T = e^{-2\kappa a}$ for *all* cases. This is **wrong** for $E > V_0$ — there, $T \to 1$ as the wave passes over the barrier. The exponential form applies **only** to $E < V_0$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the exponent**: $T = e^{-\kappa a}$ is a common mistake; the correct exponent is $-2\kappa a$, accounting for both decay and growth.
> 3. **Writing $\kappa$ without the square root**: Several students write $\kappa = 2m(V_0 - E)/\hbar$ (missing the $\sqrt{}$). This is dimensionally incorrect.
> 4. **Confusing "tunnelling" with "classical transmission"**: Tunnelling is specifically the $E < V_0$ case. Over-the-barrier propagation is classical behaviour, not tunnelling.
> 5. **In STM questions, students often forget to mention the exponential dependence of current on gap width** — this is the heart of why STM works.
> 6. **In tunnel diode questions, the negative differential resistance is the single most important feature** — always mention it explicitly for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition**: Tunnelling = particle with $E < V_0$ has finite probability of crossing the barrier.
- ✅ **Three regions** of rectangular barrier: I (free, $V=0$), II (barrier, $V=V_0$), III (free, $V=0$).
- ✅ **Wave function form**:
  - Outside barrier: $\psi \propto e^{\pm ikx}$ (oscillatory), with $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$
  - Inside barrier: $\psi \propto e^{\pm \kappa x}$ (exponential), with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$
- ✅ **Transmission coefficient (qualitative)**: $T \approx e^{-2\kappa a}$
- ✅ **Four boundary conditions**: $\psi$ and $\psi'$ continuous at $x=0$ and $x=a$.
- ✅ **Key trends**: $T$ decreases with increasing $a$, $(V_0 - E)$, and $m$.
- ✅ **Heisenberg justification**: $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \gtrsim \hbar/2$ allows temporary violation of energy conservation.
- ✅ **Applications to remember**:
  - **STM**: tunnelling current $\propto e^{-2\kappa d}$ → atomic resolution imaging
  - **Tunnel diode (Esaki)**: negative differential resistance → high-frequency oscillators
  - **Flash memory**: Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling through thin SiO₂
  - **Alpha decay**: Gamow's tunnelling explanation
  - **Stellar fusion**: protons tunnel through Coulomb barrier in the Sun
- ✅ **Numerical values** to memorize: $h$, $\hbar$, $m_e$, typical STM gap (~0.5 nm), typical oxide thickness in flash (~7–10 nm).
- ✅ **KTU-favourite topics** for short questions: (i) Define tunnelling, (ii) Alpha decay, (iii) STM principle, (iv) Tunnel diode.
- ✅ **KTU-favourite long questions**: (a) Derive $T \approx e^{-2\kappa a}$ qualitatively, (b) STM or tunnel diode working.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrinsic Semiconductor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **intrinsic semiconductor** is a pure, chemically stoichiometric, and crystallographically perfect semiconductor material in which the electrical conduction properties are governed entirely by the **thermally generated electron–hole pairs (EHPs)** arising from the host crystal lattice itself, with **no external impurities or dopants** introduced to alter the carrier statistics.

In strict KTU terminology, an intrinsic semiconductor obeys three defining conditions:

1. **Electron concentration (n)** equals **hole concentration (p)**, i.e., $n = p = n_i$, where $n_i$ is the *intrinsic carrier concentration*.
2. The **Fermi level ($E_F$)** lies exactly at the **midpoint of the forbidden energy gap**, equidistant from the conduction band minimum ($E_C$) and valence band maximum ($E_V$).
3. The **Fermi–Dirac occupation probability** at $E_F$ is exactly $f(E_F) = 0.5$.

Mathematically, the electroneutrality condition is:
$$n_0 = p_0 = n_i(T)$$

The standard examples used in KTU board questions are **pure Germanium (Ge)**, **pure Silicon (Si)**, and **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** at room temperature (**T = 300 K**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Empty Auditorium":**
> Imagine a packed cinema hall (the **valence band**, fully occupied by electrons sitting in their seats). Above the hall is an empty balcony (the **conduction band**, where electrons can move freely and conduct electricity). The stairs connecting them are blocked by a turnstile gate — the **forbidden energy gap ($E_g$)**.
>
> In an **insulator**, the gate is locked and too high to climb ($E_g > 3$ eV). In a **metal**, the gate is missing — electrons freely spill into the balcony.
>
> In a **semiconductor**, the turnstile is *just climbable* ($E_g \approx 1$ eV). When thermal energy is supplied (someone pushes from behind, equivalent to heating the crystal), a few lucky audience members in the lower hall gain enough energy to jump into the balcony. Each jumper leaves behind a **vacant seat (a HOLE)** in the lower hall. Now **two carriers exist from one act**: one electron in the balcony (free to conduct) and one hole in the lower hall (also free to conduct, as neighbors shift into the empty seat).
>
> When *no impurities* are added, every electron that jumps up leaves exactly one hole behind — hence $n = p$. That is the essence of an **intrinsic** semiconductor.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Values

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants (Bold) for KTU Calculations:**
> - **Boltzmann constant, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**
> - **Electronic charge, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
> - **Planck's constant, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
> - **Reduced Planck's constant, $\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K, $V_T = k_B T / e \approx 0.0259$ V ≈ 26 mV**

**Material Reference Data (T = 300 K):**

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $n_i$ (per m³) | $\mu_e$ (m²/V·s) | $\mu_h$ (m²/V·s) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Silicon (Si) | **1.12** | $1.5 \times 10^{16}$ | 0.135 | 0.048 |
| Germanium (Ge) | **0.67** | $2.4 \times 10^{19}$ | 0.390 | 0.190 |
| GaAs | **1.42** | $1.8 \times 10^{12}$ | 0.850 | 0.040 |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Band Theory of Intrinsic Semiconductors

The conduction behaviour of any crystalline solid is best described by **Band Theory**, a quantum-mechanical extension of atomic orbital overlap into a periodic lattice. For a B.Tech-level KTU 2024 answer, the following logical steps are mandatory:

- **Step 1 — Atomic Orbital Splitting:** When $N$ identical atoms (each with discrete allowed energy levels) are brought close together to form a crystal, Pauli's exclusion principle forces each discrete level to **split into N sub-levels**, forming a quasi-continuous **energy band**.

- **Step 2 — Valence and Conduction Bands:** The highest *fully occupied* band at 0 K is the **Valence Band (VB)**. The next higher permitted band, which is *empty at 0 K*, is the **Conduction Band (CB)**.

- **Step 3 — Forbidden Gap:** The energy region between the top of the VB ($E_V$) and the bottom of the CB ($E_C$) is the **Forbidden Energy Gap**, denoted:
$$E_g = E_C - E_V$$

- **Step 4 — Why Intrinsic Conduction is Temperature-Activated:** At 0 K, the VB is completely full and the CB is completely empty, so the crystal behaves as a **perfect insulator**. As temperature rises, lattice vibrations (phonons) supply energy $\geq E_g$ to a tiny fraction of VB electrons, **promoting** them across the gap. Each promoted electron simultaneously creates a **hole** in the VB. Both carriers are mobile and contribute to current.

- **Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition:** In an intrinsic crystal, electron–hole pair **(EHP) generation** is dynamically balanced by **recombination**. At thermal equilibrium:
$$\text{Generation Rate} (G) = \text{Recombination Rate} (R)$$
This automatically enforces $n = p = n_i$.

---

## 2.2 Effective Density of States

The number of available quantum states per unit volume in each band is condensed into two **effective density of states** (constants for a given material at a given T):

$$N_C = 2 \left( \frac{2 \pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_V = 2 \left( \frac{2 \pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$$

where $m_e^*$ and $m_h^*$ are the **density-of-states effective masses** of electrons and holes respectively. Note the **$T^{3/2}$ scaling** — this is high-yield for derivations.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every KTU-board-tested formula** on intrinsic semiconductors. Memorize the units and the conditions of validity.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Condition / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$ | Charge neutrality | Intrinsic only |
| 2 | $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ | Mass action law | Equilibrium, any doping |
| 3 | $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$ | Intrinsic concentration | Classical derivation |
| 4 | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \; \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$ | Simplified form | Shows $T^{3/2}$ prefactor |
| 5 | $E_F = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} k_B T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$ | Fermi level position | Intrinsic, exact |
| 6 | $E_F \approx \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2}$ | Approx. mid-gap | When $m_e^* \approx m_h^*$ |
| 7 | $\sigma = n_i e (\mu_e + \mu_h)$ | Conductivity | Intrinsic, T-dependent |
| 8 | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \dfrac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}$ | Varshni's equation | $E_g$ vs T dependence |
| 9 | $\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$ | Arrhenius form | $\ln \sigma$ vs $1/T$ straight line |
| 10 | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$ | Fermi–Dirac function | Prob. of occupation |
| 11 | $E_g(\text{eV}) \approx \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Photon-energy relation | Optical absorption edge |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Formula 5 is often written *without* the second (logarithmic) term by students. While the approximation in row 6 is acceptable for $m_e^* = m_h^*$, the complete expression in row 5 is what earns full marks in a 14-mark derivation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pure Si single crystals** form the substrate of every CMOS logic gate, MOSFET, and integrated circuit.
- **Pure Ge** is used in **infrared detectors** and **gamma-ray spectrometers** because of its low $E_g = 0.67$ eV (sensitive to long-wavelength photons).
- **GaAs intrinsic behaviour** underpins **laser diodes**, **solar cells** in space applications, and **high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs)** in 5G front-end modules.
- Knowledge of $n_i(T)$ is critical for **thermal runaway analysis** in power semiconductor devices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1 — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration ($n_i$)**
> This is the **most repeated derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme question papers. Follow every step.

**Starting Point — Fermi–Dirac Statistics in Each Band:**

The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is:
$$n_0 = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} N_C(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$

where $N_C(E)$ is the density of states in CB and $f(E)$ is the Fermi–Dirac function.

**Step 1 — Boltzmann Approximation.**
For an intrinsic semiconductor, $E_C - E_F \gg k_B T$ (the Fermi level is deep in the gap, far from the band edges). Therefore:
$$f(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and evaluate the integral.**
$$n_0 = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\!\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!3/2} \sqrt{E - E_C} \; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) dE$$

Let $u = (E - E_C)/k_B T$. After standard evaluation:
$$n_0 = 2 \left(\frac{2 \pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the effective density of states.**
$$N_C = 2 \left(\frac{2 \pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}$$

Therefore:
$$n_0 = N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Similar derivation for holes in the valence band.**
$$p_0 = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the intrinsic condition $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$.**
Equating (i) and (ii):
$$N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right) = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the intrinsic Fermi level.**
Take natural logarithms of both sides:
$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

Rearrange:
$$E_F (E_V \text{ terms combined}) = \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) + \frac{E_C + E_V}{2}$$

Substituting $N_C / N_V = (m_e^* / m_h^*)^{3/2}$:
$$E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4} k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Derive the expression for $n_i$.**
Multiply equations (i) and (ii):
$$n_0 \cdot p_0 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_V}{k_B T}\right) = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

Using $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$:
$$n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic for Step 7:** The factor of **2 in the denominator of the exponent** arises because $E_g$ is shared symmetrically between the electron (above $E_F$) and the hole (below $E_F$) — a direct consequence of $n = p$.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 2 — Intrinsic Fermi Level Position (Alternative Energy-Reference Method)**

This is the *second most popular* KTU derivation, often asked in **Part A (3 marks)** as a short proof.

**Step 1 — Apply $n_0 = p_0$ directly:**
$$N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right) = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log:**
$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Collect $E_F$ terms on the left:**
$$E_F \left( \frac{1}{k_B T} + \frac{1}{k_B T} \right) = \frac{E_C + E_V}{k_B T} + \ln N_V - \ln N_C$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{2 E_F}{k_B T} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{k_B T} + \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $k_B T / 2$:**
$$\boxed{\,E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4} k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)\,}$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence of Intrinsic Conductivity**

**Step 1 — Start with the conductivity equation:**
$$\sigma = n_i e (\mu_e + \mu_h)$$

**Step 2 — Mobility temperature dependence.** For lattice (phonon) scattering, which dominates above ~150 K:
$$\mu_e, \mu_h \propto T^{-3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the $n_i$ expression:**
$$\sigma(T) = e (\mu_e + \mu_h) \sqrt{N_C N_V} \; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine all $T$-dependences.**
$N_C \propto T^{3/2}$ and $N_V \propto T^{3/2}$, so $\sqrt{N_C N_V} \propto T^{3}$.
Mobility $\mu \propto T^{-3/2}$.

The product $\mu \sqrt{N_C N_V} \propto T^{3/2}$.

**Step 5 — Final expression:**
$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \, T^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where $\sigma_0$ is a material-specific pre-exponential constant.

**Step 6 — Linearized (Arrhenius) form for experimental plot:**
$$\ln \sigma = \ln \sigma_0 + \frac{3}{2} \ln T - \frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}$$

A plot of $\ln \sigma$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line** in the intrinsic region, with **slope = $-E_g / 2 k_B$**, allowing experimental extraction of $E_g$.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 4 — Law of Mass Action ($n \cdot p = n_i^2$) from Thermal Equilibrium Kinetics**

**Step 1 — Define generation and recombination rates.** At thermal equilibrium, the rate of EHP generation per unit volume is $G_{th}$ (a function of T only), and the recombination rate per unit volume is $R = B n p$, where $B$ is the recombination coefficient.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:**
$$G_{th} = B n_0 p_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_0 p_0 = \frac{G_{th}}{B} = n_i^2$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the law of mass action states:**
$$\boxed{\,n_0 \cdot p_0 = n_i^2(T)\,}$$

This product is **temperature-dependent but doping-independent** — a powerful KTU result used in pn-junction and bipolar-transistor analysis.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram of an Intrinsic Semiconductor

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the **band architecture** of an intrinsic semiconductor. It maps the relative positions of $E_C$, $E_F$, and $E_V$, the location of generated electrons and holes, and the direction of thermal excitation across the forbidden gap.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CB["CONDUCTION BAND (E_C) - Partially Filled Above 0 K"]
        ELEC["Free Electron (e⁻)\nMobile negative carrier\noccupies CB states"]
    end
    subgraph GAP["FORBIDDEN ENERGY GAP (E_g = E_C − E_V)"]
        EF["FERMI LEVEL (E_F)\nLocated at mid-gap\nE_F = (E_C + E_V)/2\nf(E_F) = 0.5"]
        ARR["Thermal Excitation\nEnergy ≥ E_g\nfrom phonon absorption"]
    end
    subgraph VB["VALENCE BAND (E_V) - Holes Appear Above 0 K"]
        HOLE["Hole (h⁺)\nMobile positive carrier\nvacancy in VB states"]
    end
    ARROW1["Promotion of electron\nby thermal energy k_B T"] -.-> ELEC
    HOLE -.->|"Leaves behind vacancy"| ARROW1
    CB --- GAP
    GAP --- VB
    EF -.->|"E_g/2 above E_V"| ELEC
    EF -.->|"E_g/2 below E_C"| HOLE
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The lower band is the **Valence Band** (initially full of bound electrons). The upper band is the **Conduction Band** (initially empty). The horizontal gap between them represents the **forbidden energy $E_g$**. The dashed line $E_F$ is the **Fermi level**, pinned to mid-gap. The dashed arrow represents a thermally excited electron that has jumped from VB to CB, leaving behind a hole.

---

## 4.2 Generation–Recombination Dynamics Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lattice Atom\nBound Electron in VB"] -->|"Phonon absorption\nEnergy ≥ E_g"| B["Free Electron in CB\nMobile e⁻"]
    A -->|"Vacancy left behind"| C["Hole in VB\nMobile h⁺"]
    B -->|"Direct recombination\nRadiative or non-radiative\nR = B·n·p"| A
    C -->|"Direct recombination\nR = B·n·p"| A
    B -->|"Drift under E-field\nContributes to current"| OUT1["Electron Current\nJ_n = n·e·v_n"]
    C -->|"Drift under E-field\nContributes to current"| OUT2["Hole Current\nJ_p = p·e·v_p"]
    GEN["Thermal Generation\nG = G_th(T)"] --> A
    REC["Recombination\nR = B·n·p"] --> A
```

> **Interpretation:** At thermal equilibrium, the **generation rate $G_{th}$** (which is purely a function of temperature) is exactly balanced by the **recombination rate $R = Bnp$**. This is the kinetic origin of the **Law of Mass Action** and the *intrinsic* condition $n = p$.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Why an Intrinsic Semiconductor Behaves the Way It Does

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PURE["Pure Crystalline Lattice\nNo dopants, no defects\nStoichiometric Si or Ge"] --> FORM["Energy Band Formation\nOverlap of N atomic orbitals\nproduces valence + conduction bands"]
    FORM --> T0["At T = 0 K\nVB full, CB empty\nActs as perfect insulator"]
    FORM --> TG["At T > 0 K\nThermal energy k_B T\nEHPs generated"]
    TG --> NE["n = p = n_i\nCharge neutrality enforced"]
    TG --> FERMI["E_F = mid-gap\nf(E_F) = 0.5\nApproximately"]
    NE --> COND["Conductivity\nσ = n_i e (μ_e + μ_h)\nT-dependent, increases exponentially"]
    FERMI --> MASS["Law of Mass Action\nn · p = n_i^2(T)\nHolds for any doping"]
    COND --> APPL["Engineering Applications\nCMOS substrate, IR detectors,\nsolar cells, temperature sensors"]
    MASS --> APPL
```

> **Reading the Block Diagram:** This is the **functional flow** that examiners love to see in **14-mark answers**. Trace the path: pure crystal → band formation → temperature activation → carrier generation → neutrality/Fermi conditions → conductivity → engineering applications.
