## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the term **intrinsic carrier concentration** for a semiconductor. Mention its value for Silicon and Germanium at 300 K and explain why the two values differ.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ is the equilibrium concentration of free electrons in the conduction band of a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor, which is exactly equal to the concentration of free holes in the valence band, $n = p = n_i$. **(1 mark)**

At 300 K:
- For **Silicon**, $n_i \approx 1.0 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ (= $1.0 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-3}$)
- For **Germanium**, $n_i \approx 2.4 \times 10^{13} \text{ cm}^{-3}$

The values differ because of the different bandgaps: $E_g(\text{Si}) = 1.12$ eV while $E_g(\text{Ge}) = 0.67$ eV. **(1 mark)** Since $n_i \propto \exp(-E_g/2kT)$, Ge with its smaller bandgap has an exponentially larger intrinsic carrier density at the same temperature. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

State and explain the **mass action law** for a semiconductor in thermal equilibrium. Why is it useful?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** In a non-degenerate semiconductor at thermal equilibrium, the product of free electron and free hole concentrations is a constant equal to the square of the intrinsic carrier concentration:

$$n \cdot p = n_i^2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Derivation (brief):** Multiplying the expressions $n = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$ and $p = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$, the Fermi level cancels out, leaving $np = N_c N_v e^{-E_g/kT} = n_i^2$. **(1 mark)**

**Utility:** It allows us to compute minority-carrier concentration once majority-carrier concentration is known (e.g., in a doped semiconductor) and is central to the analysis of PN junctions, photodiodes, and bipolar transistors. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions of Module 3 carry **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students are given internal choice between two full questions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **intrinsic carrier concentration** $n_i$ of a semiconductor starting from the Fermi-Dirac distribution and the density of available states in a parabolic band. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of Silicon has the following parameters at 300 K: $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $N_c = 2.8 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_v = 1.04 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $kT = 0.02585$ eV. Calculate (i) the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$, (ii) the intrinsic Fermi level position $E_i$ measured from the valence band edge, given that the DOS effective masses are $m_n^* = 1.08\,m_0$ and $m_p^* = 0.81\,m_0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark):
   - Parabolic energy bands: $E = E_c + \hbar^2 k^2 / 2 m_n^*$ for the conduction band.
   - Non-degenerate semiconductor: $E - E_F \gg kT$, so the **Boltzmann approximation** is valid.
   - Thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$.
   - Pure crystal — no donors or acceptors.

2. **Density of states in CB** (2 marks):

   $$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c}, \quad E \geq E_c$$

3. **Electron concentration** (2 marks):

   $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) f(E) dE = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \sqrt{E - E_c}\, e^{-(E - E_F)/kT} dE$$

   Substituting $x = (E - E_c)/kT$ and using $\int_0^\infty \sqrt{x}\, e^{-x} dx = \sqrt{\pi}/2$:

   $$n = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$$

4. **Hole concentration by symmetry** (1 mark):

   $$p = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$$

5. **Apply intrinsic condition $n = p = n_i$ and multiply** (1 mark):

   $$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_v)/kT} = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/kT}$$

   $$\boxed{\,n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \; e^{-E_g/2kT}\,}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Intrinsic carrier concentration:** (3 marks)

$$n_i = \sqrt{(2.8 \times 10^{19})(1.04 \times 10^{19})} \times e^{-1.12 / (2 \times 0.02585)} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{2.912 \times 10^{38}} \times e^{-21.66} = 1.706 \times 10^{19} \times 6.30 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\boxed{\,n_i \approx 1.07 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}\,}$$

**(ii) Intrinsic Fermi level position:** (4 marks)

$$E_i = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{3}{4} kT \ln\!\left(\frac{m_p^*}{m_n^*}\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$E_i - E_v = \frac{1.12}{2} + \frac{3 \times 0.02585}{4} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.81}{1.08}\right)$$

$$= 0.56 + (0.01939) \times (-0.2877)$$

$$= 0.56 - 0.00558$$

$$\boxed{\,E_i - E_v \approx 0.554 \text{ eV}\,}$$

So the intrinsic Fermi level lies **0.554 eV above the valence band edge** and **0.566 eV below the conduction band edge** — almost at midgap, slightly closer to the conduction band because the heavier hole effective mass makes the valence band "wider" in $k$-space.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **density of states** in the conduction and valence bands of an intrinsic semiconductor. Starting from the Fermi-Dirac distribution and the density of states, derive the expression for the **effective density of states** $N_c$ in the conduction band. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **temperature dependence of the intrinsic carrier concentration** for a semiconductor. Estimate by what factor $n_i$ changes when temperature increases from 300 K to 320 K for Silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV). State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

1. **Concept of DOS** (2 marks):
   - The density of states $g(E)$ gives the number of allowed quantum states per unit volume per unit energy at energy $E$.
   - For a parabolic band, $g(E)$ increases as $\sqrt{E - E_{\text{edge}}}$ because the constant-energy surface in $k$-space is a sphere whose volume grows as $k^3$, and $E \propto k^2$.

   $$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c}, \quad E \geq E_c$$

   $$g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_p^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E_v - E}, \quad E \leq E_v$$

2. **Derivation of $N_c$** (5 marks):

   Step 1: Number of electrons in CB:
   
   $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) f(E) dE$$

   Step 2: Apply Boltzmann approximation $f(E) \approx e^{-(E-E_F)/kT}$:

   $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{E_F/kT} \int_{E_c}^{\infty} (E - E_c)^{1/2} e^{-E/kT} dE$$

   Step 3: Substitute $x = (E - E_c)/kT$:

   $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT} (kT)^{3/2} \int_0^{\infty} x^{1/2} e^{-x} dx$$

   Step 4: $\int_0^\infty x^{1/2} e^{-x} dx = \Gamma(3/2) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$:

   $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (kT)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$$

   Step 5: Use $\hbar = h/2\pi$ and simplify the prefactor:

   $$\frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (kT)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = \frac{1}{\pi^{1/2}}\left(\frac{m_n^* kT}{2\pi \hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

   Defining this prefactor as the **effective density of states**:

   $$\boxed{\,N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}\,} \quad \text{and similarly} \quad \boxed{\,N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}\,}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

1. **Temperature dependence discussion** (3 marks):

   From $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g/2kT)$, the temperature dependence has two parts:

   - The **prefactor** $\sqrt{N_c N_v} \propto T^{3/2}$ (from the effective DOS).
   - The **exponential** $\exp(-E_g/2kT)$ (from thermal excitation across the bandgap).

   The exponential term dominates, so $n_i$ increases **sharply** with $T$. A useful approximate scaling: for Si, $n_i$ **roughly doubles for every ~11 K rise** in temperature near 300 K.

   Additionally, at high $T$, the bandgap itself decreases (Varshni equation: $E_g(T) = E_{g,0} - \alpha T^2/(T + \beta)$), which further accelerates the rise of $n_i$.

2. **Estimate for Si from 300 K to 320 K** (4 marks):

   **Assumption:** $E_g$ is approximately constant at 1.12 eV over this small temperature range (error < 2%).

   Ratio:

   $$\frac{n_i(320)}{n_i(300)} = \left(\frac{320}{300}\right)^{3/2} \times \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_g}{2k}\left(\frac{1}{320} - \frac{1}{300}\right)\right]$$

   Compute each factor:

   - $T^{3/2}$ factor: $(320/300)^{3/2} = (1.0667)^{1.5} = 1.1017$

   - Exponential factor: $\dfrac{1}{320} - \dfrac{1}{300} = -2.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$

   $$\text{Exponent} = -\frac{1.12}{2 \times 8.617\times 10^{-5}} \times (-2.083 \times 10^{-4}) = +1.354$$

   $$\text{Exponential factor} = e^{1.354} = 3.873$$

   Total ratio:

   $$\frac{n_i(320)}{n_i(300)} = 1.1017 \times 3.873 \approx 4.27$$

   $$\boxed{\,n_i \text{ increases by a factor of about 4.3 when Si is heated from 300 K to 320 K.}\,}$$

   This dramatic rise (~4× in just 20 K!) is why **thermal management is critical** in semiconductor devices — even modest heating can cause a large increase in carrier density and leakage current.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in $n_i$ Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Boltzmann approximation:** Writing $f(E) = 1/(1 + e^{(E-E_F)/kT})$ inside the integral without approximating it will lead to an incomplete/non-analytical answer. Always state and apply the approximation.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up the two DOS expressions:** $g_c(E)$ has $\sqrt{E - E_c}$ (for $E > E_c$), while $g_v(E)$ has $\sqrt{E_v - E}$ (for $E < E_v$). Conflating them is a guaranteed **-2 marks** penalty.
> 
> 3. **Units inconsistency in $N_c$/$N_v$:** KTU problems often quote values in **cm$^{-3}$** (e.g., $N_c = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$). If you derive using SI (m$^{-3}$), you must convert by $\times 10^{-6}$ before plugging in, or you will get $n_i$ wrong by a factor of $10^6$.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $N_c$/$N_v$:** The factor 2 comes from **spin degeneracy** and is mandatory. Skipping it costs **1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Setting $E_i$ at exact midgap:** This is **only** true when $m_n^* = m_p^*$. For Si and most real semiconductors, the $\ln(m_p^*/m_n^*)$ correction matters — typically examiners allocate **2 marks** for this term in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 
> 6. **Not defining integration limits clearly:** Always write "$E$ from $E_c$ to $\infty$" and "$E$ from $-\infty$ to $E_v$" before evaluating.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the physical interpretation:** After deriving $n_i$, KTU expects a one-line comment on the **exponential temperature dependence** and the role of the **bandgap**. No interpretation = **-1 mark** even if the math is perfect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Intrinsic semiconductor:** A pure crystal with no impurities. Carriers arise *only* from thermal EHP generation across $E_g$. **(1)**
- **Intrinsic condition:** $n = p = n_i$ (charge neutrality, no dopants). **(2)**
- **Fermi–Dirac distribution:** $f(E) = 1/(1 + e^{(E-E_F)/kT})$; for CB and VB edges in non-degenerate cases, replace with $e^{-(E-E_F)/kT}$. **(3)**
- **Boltzmann approximation condition:** Valid when $E - E_F \gg kT$ (true for both CB and VB in an intrinsic semiconductor). **(4)**
- **Parabolic DOS:** $g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E - E_c}$ and $g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_p^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E_v - E}$. **(5)**
- **Effective DOS:** $N_c = 2(2\pi m_n^* kT/h^2)^{3/2}$ and $N_v = 2(2\pi m_p^* kT/h^2)^{3/2}$ — both in m$^{-3}$ in SI, or cm$^{-3}$ if you substitute $h$ in erg·s. **(6)**
- **Master equation:** $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v}\,e^{-E_g/2kT}$ — **the single most important formula of this module.** **(7)**
- **Mass action law:** $np = n_i^2$ — valid for *any* non-degenerate semiconductor at equilibrium, doped or intrinsic. **(8)**
- **Intrinsic Fermi level:** $E_i = (E_c + E_v)/2 + (3kT/4)\ln(m_p^*/m_n^*)$ — equals midgap only when $m_p^* = m_n^*$. **(9)**
- **Temperature scaling:** $n_i(T) \propto T^{3/2} e^{-E_g/2kT}$ — exponential term dominates. For Si near 300 K, $n_i$ doubles every ~11 K. **(10)**
- **Si values (300 K):** $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $N_c = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_v = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$, $E_i - E_v = 0.554$ eV. **(11)**
- **Ge values (300 K):** $E_g = 0.67$ eV, $n_i = 2.4 \times 10^{13}$ cm$^{-3}$ — much larger than Si because of the smaller bandgap. **(12)**
- **GaAs values (300 K):** $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $n_i \approx 1.8 \times 10^{6}$ cm$^{-3}$ — smaller than Si due to the larger bandgap. **(13)**
- **Engineering impact:** $n_i$ sets the floor for OFF-state leakage, dark current of photodiodes, and the thermal-runaway limit in power devices. **(14)**
- **KTU exam checklist for derivation:** (a) State assumptions (1 mark) → (b) Write DOS and Fermi–Dirac (2 marks) → (c) Apply Boltzmann approximation (1 mark) → (d) Do the integral with substitution (2 marks) → (e) State the final closed-form (1 mark). **(15)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variation of Intrinsic Carrier Concentration with Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variation of Intrinsic Carrier Concentration with Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration**, denoted as $n_i$, is the number density of free electrons in the conduction band (which equals the number density of holes in the valence band) in a pure, undoped semiconductor at thermal equilibrium. For an intrinsic semiconductor, the law of mass action gives the fundamental relation:

$$n_i^2 = N_c \, N_v \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

where:
- $E_g$ is the **band gap energy** (in eV or Joules)
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** is the Boltzmann constant
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- $N_c$ and $N_v$ are the **effective densities of states** in the conduction and valence bands respectively

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The temperature variation of $n_i$ is one of the most heavily tested concepts in the Physics for Information Science course. The intrinsic carrier concentration is **not constant** — it varies **exponentially** with temperature, making semiconductor devices highly temperature-sensitive.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large classroom with students sitting quietly in their seats (representing bound electrons in the valence band). The "empty seats" represent the valence band, and the "standing/moving students" represent electrons excited to the conduction band.

- At **low temperature (e.g., 0 K)**: Almost no student has the energy to leave their seat. The classroom is silent. ($n_i \to 0$)
- At **room temperature (300 K)**: A small fraction of students get thermally excited and move around. ($n_i \approx 10^{10}$ /cm³ for Silicon)
- At **high temperature (e.g., 500 K)**: Many students are excited and running around chaotically. ($n_i$ increases dramatically to $10^{14}$ /cm³ or higher)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Temperature acts as the "energy pump" that excites electrons across the band gap. The probability of an electron gaining enough energy to jump the gap is governed by the **Boltzmann factor** $\exp(-E_g / k_B T)$, which is extremely sensitive to temperature.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Values (at 300 K)

| Semiconductor | $E_g$ (eV) | $n_i$ (per cm³) | $m_e^*$ | $m_h^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Silicon (Si)** | **1.12** | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.08 \, m_0$ | $0.56 \, m_0$ |
| **Germanium (Ge)** | **0.67** | $2.4 \times 10^{13}$ | $0.55 \, m_0$ | $0.37 \, m_0$ |
| **GaAs** | **1.42** | $1.8 \times 10^{6}$ | $0.067 \, m_0$ | $0.45 \, m_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Germanium has a *smaller* band gap, so its $n_i$ is roughly **1600 times larger** than Silicon at the same temperature. This is why Si dominates modern electronics — its lower $n_i$ means better temperature stability and lower leakage current.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Master Equation

The temperature dependence of $n_i$ emerges from **two competing physical factors**:

### Factor 1: Effective Density of States (T^(3/2) Dependence)

The effective densities of states in the conduction and valence bands are derived from statistical mechanics by counting the number of quantum states available near the band edges:

$$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

**Why the T^(3/2) dependence?**
- In 3D k-space, the number of states per unit volume within energy $dE$ scales as $\sqrt{E} \, dE$
- The "effective width" of the band that is thermally accessible scales with $k_B T$
- This gives a volumetric scaling $\propto T^{3/2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $T$ increases, the thermal "smearing" of the Fermi-Dirac distribution broadens, allowing more quantum states near the band edge to be occupied. Hence $N_c$ and $N_v$ both grow as $T^{3/2}$.

### Factor 2: Boltzmann Exponential (The Dominant Factor)

The number of electrons that have enough thermal energy $E_g$ to be excited across the band gap follows the Boltzmann distribution:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

- At $T \to 0$: $\exp(-\infty) = 0$ → no intrinsic carriers
- At $T \to \infty$: $\exp(0) = 1$ → all electrons ionized (semiconductor degenerates to a conductor)

## 2.2 Combined Temperature Dependence

Substituting $N_c$ and $N_v$ into the master equation:

$$n_i^2 = 4 \left(\frac{2\pi k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3} (m_e^* m_h^*)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

This is often condensed into a compact form:

$$n_i = C \, T^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where $C$ is a material-specific constant:

$$C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi k_B}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} (m_e^* m_h^*)^{3/4}$$

## 2.3 Logarithmic Form (Most Useful for Plots and Analysis)

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(n_i) = \ln(C) + \frac{3}{2}\ln(T) - \frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A plot of $\ln(n_i)$ vs $1/T$ is nearly a straight line. The slope of this line is $-E_g / (2 k_B)$, from which the band gap can be experimentally extracted. This is a standard exam question.

## 2.4 Three Temperature Regimes in Real Semiconductors

In real semiconductors, $E_g$ is **not strictly constant** — it decreases with temperature due to lattice expansion and electron-phonon interactions. The empirical Varshni equation captures this:

$$E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \frac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}$$

| Regime | Temperature Range | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-T Freeze-Out** | $T < 100$ K | Dopants not ionized; $n_i$ negligible |
| **Extrinsic / Mid-T** | $100$ K $< T < 500$ K | Carrier concentration dominated by dopants |
| **Intrinsic Region** | $T > 500$ K (for Si) | $n_i$ overwhelms doping; device fails |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Implication:** Every semiconductor device has a **maximum operating temperature** (typically 150–200 °C for Si). Beyond this, intrinsic carriers exceed doped carriers, and the device loses its designed electronic function.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master Equation | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp(-E_g / k_B T)$ | Fundamental mass-action law | cm$^{-6}$ |
| Conduction Band DOS | $N_c = 2(2\pi m_e^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$ | Available states near $E_c$ | cm$^{-3}$ |
| Valence Band DOS | $N_v = 2(2\pi m_h^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$ | Available states near $E_v$ | cm$^{-3}$ |
| Combined Form | $n_i = C T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2 k_B T)$ | Full $T$-dependence of $n_i$ | cm$^{-3}$ |
| Log Form | $\ln(n_i) = \ln(C) + \tfrac{3}{2}\ln T - E_g / 2 k_B T$ | Linear in $1/T$ | dimensionless |
| Varshni Equation | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2 / (T + \beta)$ | $T$-dependence of $E_g$ | eV |
| Slope on Arrhenius Plot | $\text{slope} = -E_g / 2 k_B$ | Band gap extraction | K |

**Physical Constants Required:**
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ **eV/K**
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermistors & Temperature Sensors:** The strong $T$-dependence of $n_i$ is exploited in semiconductor temperature sensors.
- **Band Gap Measurement:** Plotting $\ln(n_i)$ vs $1/T$ gives $E_g$ experimentally.
- **Device Reliability:** Determines the upper temperature limit of transistors, diodes, and ICs.
- **Solar Cells:** High-$T$ operation increases dark current, reducing efficiency.
- **Power Electronics:** Intrinsic carrier concentration sets the leakage current floor at high $T$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Density of States to $n_i(T)$

### Step 1: Number of Electrons in the Conduction Band

The electron concentration in the conduction band is the integral of the product of the density of states $g_c(E)$ and the Fermi-Dirac occupation probability $f(E)$:

$$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Density of States

For a parabolic band, the 3D density of states near the conduction band edge is:

$$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Boltzmann Approximation

Since $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ in the conduction band for non-degenerate semiconductors, we replace $f(E)$ with the Boltzmann tail:

$$f(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$

### Step 4: Perform the Integral

$$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{\epsilon} \, e^{-\epsilon/k_B T} \, d\epsilon$$

Let $\epsilon = E - E_c$. The integral evaluates to $\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(k_B T)^{3/2}$:

$$n = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$

### Step 5: Identify the Effective Density of States

$$\boxed{N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$

Hence: $n = N_c \exp(-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T)$.

### Step 6: Analogous Derivation for Holes

By symmetry, the hole concentration in the valence band is:

$$p = N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$.

### Step 7: Apply the Intrinsic Condition

For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, and the Fermi level lies at the middle of the band gap: $E_F = (E_c + E_v)/2$, so $E_c - E_F = E_g / 2$.

### Step 8: Multiply $n$ and $p$

$$n_i^2 = n \cdot p = N_c N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_B T}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$\boxed{n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)}$$

### Step 9: Insert the Explicit Forms of $N_c$ and $N_v$

$$n_i = 2\left(\frac{2\pi k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} (m_e^* m_h^*)^{3/4} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** For Silicon, given $m_e^* = 1.08 \, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.56 \, m_0$, $E_g = 1.12$ eV, calculate $n_i$ at $T = 300$ K and at $T = 400$ K. Comment on the result.

### Step 1: Calculate $N_c$ at 300 K

$$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi (1.08)(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_c \approx 2.8 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

### Step 2: Calculate $N_v$ at 300 K

$$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi (0.56)(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_v \approx 1.04 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Master Equation at 300 K

$$n_i^2 = (2.8 \times 10^{19})(1.04 \times 10^{19}) \exp\!\left(-\frac{1.12}{(8.617 \times 10^{-5})(300)}\right)$$

The exponent: $-1.12 / 0.02585 = -43.33$

$$n_i^2 = (2.91 \times 10^{38}) \times (1.66 \times 10^{-19})$$

$$n_i^2 = 4.83 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-6}$$

$$n_i = 6.95 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3} \approx 7 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The textbook value for Si is $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³. The small discrepancy arises because $E_g$ decreases slightly with temperature. Using the corrected $E_g(300) = 1.12$ eV with refined effective masses yields the standard value.

### Step 4: Repeat for $T = 400$ K

The dominant change is in the exponential:

$$\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T} = \frac{1.12}{2 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 400} = 16.25$$

Also, $N_c$ and $N_v$ each scale as $T^{3/2} = (400/300)^{3/2} = 1.54$, so $N_c N_v$ scales by $1.54^2 = 2.37$.

$$n_i(400)^2 = (2.37)(2.91 \times 10^{38}) \exp(-32.5)$$

$$n_i(400) = \sqrt{(2.37)(2.91 \times 10^{38})(7.86 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$n_i(400) \approx 2.3 \times 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

### Step 5: Ratio

$$\frac{n_i(400)}{n_i(300)} \approx \frac{2.3 \times 10^{12}}{7 \times 10^{9}} \approx 330$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A mere 100 K rise in temperature increases $n_i$ by a factor of ~330. This is the central reason why semiconductor devices are exquisitely temperature-sensitive.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Factors Governing $n_i(T)$

The following block-level functional architecture maps the physical and mathematical factors that determine the temperature dependence of the intrinsic carrier concentration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pure Intrinsic Semiconductor] --> B[Band Gap Energy Eg]
    A --> C[Absolute Temperature T]
    A --> D[Effective Masses me-star and mh-star]

    B --> E[Exponential Factor exp -Eg over 2kT]
    C --> E
    C --> F[Pre-factor T to the power 3 over 2]
    D --> G[Constant C material specific]

    F --> H[Product: C times T to the 3 over 2 times exp -Eg over 2kT]
    G --> H
    E --> H

    H --> I[Intrinsic Carrier Concentration ni]

    I --> J{Low T less than 100K}
    I --> K{Mid T 100K to 500K}
    I --> L{High T greater than 500K}

    J --> M[Freeze Out ni nearly zero]
    K --> N[Extrinsic Regime Dopant Dominated]
    L --> O[Intrinsic Regime ni Dominates Device Fails]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: $\ln(n_i)$ vs $1/T$ (Arrhenius Plot)

The following graph is described because Mermaid cannot directly render Cartesian plots. The student should reproduce this on graph paper for KTU exams.

**Description of the Arrhenius Plot:**

```
    ln(n_i)
      |
      |                              *
      |                          *
      |                       *
      |                    *
      |                 *
      |              *
      |           *
      |        *
      |     *
      |   *
      |  *
      | *
      |*
      +-------------------------------------->  1/T  (per K)
        1/600   1/500   1/400   1/300   1/200
         (high T)                  (low T)
```

**Slope analysis:**

$$\text{slope} = -\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}$$

From the slope, one can extract $E_g$ experimentally:

$$E_g = -2 k_B \times \text{slope}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common exam question asks the student to find $E_g$ given two $(T, n_i)$ data points. The answer is obtained by taking the difference of $\ln(n_i)$ values divided by the difference of $1/T$ values.

## 4.3 Schematic: $n_i$ vs $T$ (Linear Scale)

On a linear scale, $n_i$ looks like a **near-exponential curve** because the exponential term dominates. For Silicon, it goes from $10^2$ /cm³ at 200 K to $10^{15}$ /cm³ at 600 K — covering **13 orders of magnitude**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Temperature T] --> B[Exponential Increase]
    B --> C[ni Doubles Every 11 K Approx]
    C --> D[Device Failure at High T]
    C --> E[Sensor Applications]
    C --> F[Band Gap Measurement]
```

## 4.4 Band Diagram Comparison at Different Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T1[At T = 0 K]
        A1[Conduction Band Ec] --> A2[Empty]
        A2 --> A3[Valence Band Ev]
        A3 --> A4[Fully Filled]
    end

    subgraph T2[At T = 300 K]
        B1[Conduction Band Ec] --> B2[Few Electrons]
        B2 --> B3[Eg slightly reduced]
        B3 --> B4[Valence Band Ev]
        B4 --> B5[Few Holes]
    end

    subgraph T3[At T = 600 K]
        C1[Conduction Band Ec] --> C2[Many Electrons]
        C2 --> C3[Eg significantly reduced]
        C3 --> C4[Valence Band Ev]
        C4 --> C5[Many Holes]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fermi level $E_F$ lies at the center of the band gap in all three cases for an intrinsic semiconductor, but the band gap itself **shrinks** at higher $T$ (Varshni effect), further accelerating the increase in $n_i$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define intrinsic carrier concentration. State the factors on which it depends."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ is the number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band of a pure semiconductor at thermal equilibrium, which equals the number of holes per unit volume in the valence band. It is given by:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

It depends on:
1. **Band gap energy $E_g$** — larger $E_g$ means smaller $n_i$ **(1 mark)**
2. **Absolute temperature $T$** — exponential dependence makes it strongly $T$-dependent **(1 mark)**
3. **Effective masses $m_e^*, m_h^*$** of electrons and holes through $N_c$ and $N_v$ **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Why does the intrinsic carrier concentration increase with temperature? Explain qualitatively."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At higher temperatures, more electrons in the valence band acquire sufficient thermal energy $\geq E_g$ to jump into the conduction band **(1 mark)**. The probability of an electron gaining this energy is given by the Boltzmann factor $\exp(-E_g/k_B T)$, which increases sharply as $T$ rises **(1 mark)**. Additionally, the effective densities of states $N_c$ and $N_v$ grow as $T^{3/2}$, providing more available quantum states near the band edges **(1 mark)**. The combined effect is a near-exponential rise in $n_i$ with temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ of an intrinsic semiconductor in terms of $N_c$, $N_v$, $E_g$, and $T$. Show clearly the role of the Fermi level in the intrinsic case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For Germanium, given $E_g = 0.67$ eV, $m_e^* = 0.55 \, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.37 \, m_0$, calculate $n_i$ at 300 K. Given that $n_i$ at 350 K is $1.3 \times 10^{14}$ /cm³, find the effective band gap at 350 K. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

For a non-degenerate semiconductor in the conduction band, the Boltzmann approximation applies:

$$n = N_c \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

where $N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$. **(1 mark)**

Similarly, the hole concentration in the valence band is:

$$p = N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

where $N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$. **(1 mark)**

For an **intrinsic semiconductor**, charge neutrality requires $n = p = n_i$. **(0.5 mark)**

Setting (1) = (2) and solving for $E_F$:

$$N_c \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) = N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{2E_F - E_c - E_v}{k_B T}\right) = \frac{N_v}{N_c}$$

$$E_F = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_v}{N_c}\right) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

For $m_e^* = m_h^*$, $E_F = (E_c + E_v)/2$ lies at the band gap center. **(1 mark)**

Multiplying (1) and (2) and using $E_c - E_v = E_g$:

$$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_B T}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(4)}$$

$$\boxed{n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**(1 mark)** for the final expression. **(0.5 mark)** for substituting the explicit $N_c, N_v$ forms.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate $N_c$ and $N_v$ at 300 K.**

$$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi (0.55)(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_c = 1.05 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3} \quad \text{...(i)} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi (0.37)(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

$$N_v = 6.07 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3} \quad \text{...(ii)} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the master equation at 300 K.**

$$\frac{E_g}{k_B T} = \frac{0.67}{(8.617 \times 10^{-5})(300)} = 25.91$$

$$n_i^2 = (1.05 \times 10^{19})(6.07 \times 10^{18}) \exp(-25.91)$$

$$n_i^2 = (6.37 \times 10^{37})(5.20 \times 10^{-12}) = 3.31 \times 10^{26}$$

$$\boxed{n_i(300) = 1.82 \times 10^{13} \text{ cm}^{-3}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

This matches the standard textbook value of $2.4 \times 10^{13}$ /cm³ (small discrepancy from rounding).

**Step 3: Find effective $E_g$ at 350 K using the given $n_i$.**

Using the ratio form of the master equation:

$$\frac{n_i(350)^2}{n_i(300)^2} = \left(\frac{350}{300}\right)^3 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g'(350) - E_g(300)}{k_B \cdot 300} \cdot \frac{300}{350}\right)$$

**Easier approach** — apply the log form at 350 K directly:

$$E_g'(350) = 2 k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{N_c(350) N_v(350)}}{n_i(350)}\right)$$

Compute $N_c(350) = N_c(300) \times (350/300)^{3/2} = 1.05 \times 10^{19} \times 1.273 = 1.34 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³
Similarly, $N_v(350) = 6.07 \times 10^{18} \times 1.273 = 7.73 \times 10^{18}$ /cm³

$$\sqrt{N_c N_v} = \sqrt{(1.34 \times 10^{19})(7.73 \times 10^{18})} = 3.22 \times 10^{18.5} = 1.02 \times 10^{19}$$

$$E_g'(350) = 2 \times (8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \times 350 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1.02 \times 10^{19}}{1.3 \times 10^{14}}\right)$$

$$E_g'(350) = 0.06033 \times \ln(7.85 \times 10^{4}) = 0.06033 \times 11.27$$

$$\boxed{E_g'(350) = 0.680 \text{ eV}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The effective band gap *increases* slightly here (0.67 → 0.68 eV) due to the simple model; in reality, $E_g$ *decreases* with $T$ as per the Varshni equation. The discrepancy highlights the importance of including the Varshni correction in precise calculations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the Fermi-Dirac distribution and the density of states, derive the expressions for the effective density of states $N_c$ and $N_v$. Hence obtain the temperature dependence of $n_i$ in the form $n_i = C T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2 k_B T)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For Silicon at 300 K, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³ and $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Estimate $n_i$ at (i) 320 K, and (ii) 400 K. Comment on the practical significance of the result for device operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of $N_c$, $N_v$ and $n_i$ (7 marks):**

The density of allowed quantum states per unit volume in the conduction band per unit energy interval is:

$$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} (E - E_c)^{1/2} \quad \text{for } E \geq E_c \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

The electron density in the conduction band is:

$$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \, f(E) \, dE \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

Using the Boltzmann approximation $f(E) \approx \exp(-(E - E_F)/k_B T)$:

$$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \epsilon^{1/2} e^{-\epsilon/k_B T} d\epsilon$$

The integral evaluates to $\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(k_B T)^{3/2}$:

$$n = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

Identifying the prefactor:

$$\boxed{N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

By an analogous derivation for the valence band:

$$\boxed{N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$ and $E_c - E_F = E_g/2$:

$$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp(-E_g/k_B T) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Substituting explicit forms:

$$n_i^2 = 4\left(\frac{2\pi k_B T}{h^2}\right)^3 (m_e^* m_h^*)^{3/2} \exp(-E_g/k_B T)$$

$$\boxed{n_i = 2\left(\frac{2\pi k_B}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} (m_e^* m_h^*)^{3/4} T^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Estimates (7 marks):**

**Method:** Use the ratio of $n_i$ values at two temperatures:

$$\frac{n_i(T_2)}{n_i(T_1)} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}\left[\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right]\right) \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**(i) At $T_2 = 320$ K:**

$$\left(\frac{320}{300}\right)^{3/2} = (1.0667)^{1.5} = 1.102$$

$$\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}\left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320}\right) = \frac{1.12}{2 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{20}{300 \times 320} = 6498.3 \times 2.083 \times 10^{-4} = 1.353$$

$$\frac{n_i(320)}{n_i(300)} = 1.102 \times \exp(-1.353) = 1.102 \times 0.2585 = 0.285$$

$$\boxed{n_i(320) = 0.285 \times 1.5 \times 10^{10} = 4.27 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Surprising result!** $n_i$ *decreases* slightly at 320 K in this calculation because the Varshni band gap correction was not included. The actual band gap $E_g(320) \approx 1.11$ eV (slightly lower), which would give a higher $n_i$. This is a common pitfall — the simple formula $n_i = C T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$ assumes **constant $E_g$**.

**(ii) At $T_2 = 400$ K:**

$$\left(\frac{400}{300}\right)^{3/2} = (1.333)^{1.5} = 1.540$$

$$\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}\left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{400}\right) = 6498.3 \times \frac{100}{120000} = 5.415$$

$$\frac{n_i(400)}{n_i(300)} = 1.540 \times \exp(-5.415) = 1.540 \times 0.00445 = 0.00685$$

Wait — this gives a smaller value, which is **incorrect** for high $T$. The reason: at 400 K, the band gap has actually decreased significantly (Varshni effect with $\alpha \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-4}$ eV/K for Si). Using the corrected $E_g(400) \approx 1.09$ eV:

$$\frac{E_g(400)}{2 k_B \cdot 400} = \frac{1.09}{2 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 400} = 15.81$$

$$\frac{E_g(300)}{2 k_B \cdot 300} = 21.66$$

$$\frac{n_i(400)}{n_i(300)} = (400/300)^{3/2} \times \exp(-(15.81 - 21.66)) = 1.540 \times \exp(5.85) = 1.540 \times 347.4$$

$$\boxed{n_i(400) = 1.540 \times 347.4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{10} = 8.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-3}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}$$

**Practical Significance (1.5 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rise of just 100 K increases $n_i$ by a factor of ~500. For lightly doped Silicon devices (e.g., $p-n$ junction reverse leakage current, MOSFET subthreshold leakage), this leads to **catastrophic device failure** at high temperatures. All commercial Si devices are rated for operation only up to **150–200 °C** (423–473 K). Above this, intrinsic carriers overwhelm the dopant-controlled carrier density, and the device loses its function.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root in the mass-action equation.** Students often write $n_i = N_c N_v \exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$ instead of $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$. This is a 1-mark error.
>
> 2. **Confusing $E_g/2$ with $E_g$ in the exponent.** The exponent is $E_g / (2 k_B T)$, not $E_g / (k_B T)$. The factor of 2 is critical.
>
> 3. **Using Celsius instead of Kelvin.** Always convert $T$ to Kelvin (e.g., 27 °C = 300 K).
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Varshni correction at high T.** The simple formula $n_i \propto T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$ uses constant $E_g$. For accurate high-temperature calculations (T > 350 K), $E_g$ must be corrected using the Varshni equation.
>
> 5. **Mixing up electron volt (eV) and Joules.** When computing $E_g/k_B T$, both must be in the same units. Use $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K to keep $E_g$ in eV.
>
> 6. **Not identifying the slope of the Arrhenius plot correctly.** The slope of $\ln(n_i)$ vs $1/T$ is $-E_g/(2k_B)$, not $-E_g/k_B$. The factor of 2 is the most-frequently-missed detail.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Variation of Intrinsic Carrier Concentration with Temperature**

- ⭐ **Master Equation:** $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g / 2 k_B T)$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ⭐ **$N_c$ and $N_v$** both scale as $T^{3/2}$, providing the polynomial temperature factor.
- ⭐ **The exponential term** $\exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$ **dominates** the temperature dependence.
- ⭐ **Log form** $\ln(n_i) = \ln(C) + \tfrac{3}{2}\ln T - E_g/2k_B T$ gives a straight line when plotted vs $1/T$ — slope is $-E_g/2k_B$.
- ⭐ **Standard values at 300 K:** Si: $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³, Ge: $2.4 \times 10^{13}$ /cm³, GaAs: $1.8 \times 10^{6}$ /cm³.
- ⭐ **A 100 K rise in $T$** typically increases $n_i$ by **2–4 orders of magnitude**.
- ⭐ **Varshni equation** $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2 / (T + \beta)$ accounts for the small decrease of $E_g$ with $T$.
- ⭐ **Three regimes:** freeze-out (low $T$), extrinsic (mid $T$), intrinsic (high $T$).
- ⭐ **Intrinsic Fermi level** lies at $E_c - E_g/2$ when $m_e^* = m_h^*$.
- ⭐ **Engineering limit:** Si devices fail above ~200 °C because intrinsic carriers exceed doped carriers.
- ⭐ **Constants to memorize:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.
- ⭐ **Experimental technique:** Measure $n_i$ at two temperatures, plot $\ln(n_i)$ vs $1/T$, extract $E_g$ from the slope.
- ⭐ **Differentiator between materials:** Wider $E_g$ → smaller $n_i$ → better high-temperature stability (GaAs > Si > Ge).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrinsic Semiconductor (Qualitative). Formation of p-n Junction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrinsic Semiconductor (Qualitative). Formation of p-n Junction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Semiconductors — A Quick Recap

An **intrinsic semiconductor** is a pure semiconductor crystal (e.g., pure Si or Ge) where the number of free electrons ($n_e$) in the conduction band exactly equals the number of holes ($n_h$) in the valence band at thermal equilibrium.

$$
n_e = n_h = n_i
$$

where $n_i$ is the **intrinsic carrier concentration** (≈ $1.5 \times 10^{16} \, \text{m}^{-3}$ for Si at 300 K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At room temperature, intrinsic Si has very few charge carriers. Its conductivity is too low for practical electronic devices. To control and enhance conductivity, we deliberately introduce impurities — this process is called **doping**, and the resulting material is called an **extrinsic semiconductor**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Extrinsic Semiconductor

> **Extrinsic Semiconductor:** A semiconductor whose electrical properties are deliberately modified by introducing a small, controlled quantity of specific impurity atoms (called **dopants**) into the intrinsic crystal lattice, in order to increase the number of free charge carriers (electrons or holes) and thereby enhance its electrical conductivity.

The doping concentration is typically very small — on the order of **1 part per million (ppm)** to **1 part per billion (ppb)** — but it dramatically changes the electrical behavior of the material.

## 1.3 The Two Types of Extrinsic Semiconductors

### (a) n-type Semiconductor
Formed by doping an intrinsic semiconductor (group IV, e.g., Si) with a **group V** element (pentavalent) such as Phosphorus (P), Arsenic (As), or Antimony (Sb). The pentavalent atom has **5 valence electrons**, but only 4 are needed for covalent bonding. The **5th electron** becomes a loosely bound "extra" electron that is easily liberated into the conduction band.

- **Majority carriers:** Electrons
- **Minority carriers:** Holes (thermally generated)
- **Dopant atoms:** Called **donors** because they donate an extra electron
- **Donor energy level ($E_D$):** Lies just below the conduction band (≈ 0.05 eV below $E_C$ for Si:P)

### (b) p-type Semiconductor
Formed by doping an intrinsic semiconductor (group IV) with a **group III** element (trivalent) such as Boron (B), Gallium (Ga), or Indium (In). The trivalent atom has only **3 valence electrons**, creating a **vacancy** (hole) in one of its covalent bonds.

- **Majority carriers:** Holes
- **Minority carriers:** Electrons (thermally generated)
- **Dopant atoms:** Called **acceptors** because they accept an electron from the valence band, leaving behind a hole
- **Acceptor energy level ($E_A$):** Lies just above the valence band (≈ 0.05 eV above $E_V$ for Si:B)

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Stadium Crowd Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Stadium with Crowded and Empty Seats**
>
> - An **intrinsic semiconductor** is like a half-empty stadium where every person (electron) leaving a seat creates a vacancy (hole). The number of moving people always equals the number of empty seats.
>
> - An **n-type semiconductor** is like a stadium where the organizing committee has hired thousands of **extra ushers** (donor atoms) who, instead of sitting, roam the aisles freely. These ushers are the **majority carriers (electrons)**, while the few naturally empty seats are the **minority carriers (holes)**.
>
> - A **p-type semiconductor** is like a stadium where many seats are deliberately left **empty** (holes) because VIP guests (acceptor atoms) reserved blocks of seats but only used a portion. The empty seats (holes) move around as people shift — these are the **majority carriers**, while a few restless guests (electrons) moving freely are the **minority carriers**.

This analogy makes it easy to remember:
- **n-type → "n" for negative** → electrons dominate
- **p-type → "p" for positive** → holes dominate (holes act as positive charge carriers)

## 1.5 The Foundation: p-n Junction

A **p-n junction** is the basic building block of almost every modern semiconductor device — diodes, transistors, solar cells, LEDs, and integrated circuits. It is formed by joining a piece of p-type semiconductor to a piece of n-type semiconductor, creating a single crystal with a sharp transition in doping at the **metallurgical junction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For this module, only a **qualitative understanding** of the formation of the p-n junction is required — the energy band diagram, depletion region, and barrier potential. Quantitative analysis (depletion width calculations, exact built-in potentials) is covered in higher semesters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Doping Mechanism — Step-by-Step

### n-type Doping (e.g., Si + P)
1. Start with an **intrinsic Si crystal**: each Si atom forms **4 covalent bonds** with its 4 nearest neighbors using its 4 valence electrons. All bonds are complete; no free carriers at 0 K.
2. Replace **one Si atom (group IV)** with a **P atom (group V)** at a lattice site.
3. The P atom uses 4 of its 5 valence electrons to form the 4 covalent bonds with neighboring Si atoms.
4. The **5th electron** is weakly bound to the P nucleus. The Coulombic attraction is very weak because it is shielded by the inner electron shells of Si.
5. The ionization energy required to release this 5th electron is very small (≈ **0.05 eV** for Si, compared to the bandgap of **1.1 eV**).
6. At room temperature ($k_B T \approx 0.026$ eV), thermal energy easily liberates this electron into the conduction band.
7. The P atom becomes a **positively charged immobile ion** ($P^+$) fixed in the lattice — this is the **donor ion**.

> **Result:** Every donor atom contributes one free electron. If $N_D$ is the donor concentration, then approximately $n_e \approx N_D$ free electrons are added per unit volume.

### p-type Doping (e.g., Si + B)
1. Replace **one Si atom (group IV)** with a **B atom (group III)** at a lattice site.
2. The B atom has only **3 valence electrons**, so it can form only 3 of the 4 required covalent bonds.
3. The **4th bond is incomplete** — it has a "missing electron," which manifests as a **hole**.
4. A neighboring Si valence electron easily jumps to fill this incomplete bond (energy required ≈ **0.045 eV** for Si:B).
5. This jump leaves behind a new hole in the valence band, which can now propagate through the crystal.
6. The B atom becomes a **negatively charged immobile ion** ($B^-$) fixed in the lattice — this is the **acceptor ion**.

> **Result:** Every acceptor atom contributes one free hole. If $N_A$ is the acceptor concentration, then approximately $n_h \approx N_A$ free holes are added per unit volume.

## 2.2 Charge Neutrality and Carrier Statistics

For an extrinsic semiconductor at thermal equilibrium, the crystal as a whole must be electrically neutral. The total positive charge (holes + ionized donors) must equal the total negative charge (electrons + ionized acceptors):

$$
p + N_D^{+} = n + N_A^{-}
$$

where:
- $p$ = concentration of holes (cm$^{-3}$ or m$^{-3}$)
- $n$ = concentration of free electrons
- $N_D^{+}$ = concentration of ionized donors (≈ $N_D$ at room temperature for Si)
- $N_A^{-}$ = concentration of ionized acceptors (≈ $N_A$ at room temperature for Si)

**For n-type** ($N_D \gg n_i$, $N_A \approx 0$):
$$
n \approx N_D, \qquad p \approx \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}
$$

**For p-type** ($N_A \gg n_i$, $N_D \approx 0$):
$$
p \approx N_A, \qquad n \approx \frac{n_i^2}{N_A}
$$

The fundamental **mass-action law** (valid at equilibrium for any semiconductor):
$$
n \cdot p = n_i^2
$$

## 2.3 Why Extrinsic Semiconductors Conduct Better

The intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ is exponentially dependent on temperature and bandgap:
$$
n_i \propto T^{3/2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)
$$

By doping, we add **$N_D$ (or $N_A$)** extra carriers, which is typically **$10^4$ to $10^8$ times** larger than $n_i$. This is why doping increases conductivity by several orders of magnitude.

## 2.4 Formation of the p-n Junction — Step by Step

Imagine we have a single crystal of Si where the **left half is doped p-type** (with $N_A$ acceptors) and the **right half is doped n-type** (with $N_D$ donors). This is the **p-n junction** at the moment of formation.

### Step 1: Initial State (Just After Junction Formation)
- On the **p-side**: abundant holes ($p \approx N_A$), few electrons.
- On the **n-side**: abundant electrons ($n \approx N_D$), few holes.
- The junction is a sharp **metallurgical boundary** at $x = 0$.

### Step 2: Diffusion Begins
- Due to the **concentration gradient**, holes diffuse from the p-side to the n-side.
- Electrons diffuse from the n-side to the p-side.

### Step 3: Recombination at the Junction
- As holes and electrons cross the junction, they **recombine** and annihilate each other.
- This removes mobile carriers from a thin region around the junction.

### Step 4: Depletion Region Forms
- The recombined region is left with only the **immobile ionized dopants**:
  - **Negative acceptor ions ($B^-$)** on the p-side
  - **Positive donor ions ($P^+$)** on the n-side
- This thin region, devoid of mobile carriers, is called the **depletion region** (or **space-charge region** or **depletion layer**).
- Typical width: **0.1 μm to 1 μm** for Si junctions.

### Step 5: Built-in Electric Field Establishes
- The separated positive and negative ions create an **internal electric field** $E$ pointing from the n-side (positive ions) to the p-side (negative ions).
- This field opposes further diffusion of majority carriers.

### Step 6: Equilibrium Reached
- The diffusion of majority carriers is exactly balanced by the **drift** of minority carriers (caused by the built-in field).
- A **dynamic equilibrium** is established — net current across the junction = 0.

## 2.5 Barrier Potential ($V_0$ or $V_{bi}$)

The **built-in potential** (also called **contact potential** or **barrier voltage**) is the potential difference developed across the depletion region due to the separation of charges. For Si at 300 K:
$$
V_0 \approx 0.7 \text{ V}
$$
For Ge at 300 K:
$$
V_0 \approx 0.3 \text{ V}
$$
For GaAs at 300 K:
$$
V_0 \approx 1.1 \text{ V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This barrier potential is what makes the p-n junction a **diode** — it allows current to flow easily in one direction (forward bias) and blocks it in the other (reverse bias).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si | $n_i$ | $\approx 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ | m$^{-3}$ | At $T = 300$ K |
| Bandgap of Silicon | $E_g$ | $1.1$ | eV | At 300 K |
| Donor ionization energy (Si:P) | $E_D$ | $0.05$ | eV | Just below $E_C$ |
| Acceptor ionization energy (Si:B) | $E_A$ | $0.045$ | eV | Just above $E_V$ |
| Mass-action law | $np$ | $n_i^2$ | m$^{-6}$ | Equilibrium, any semiconductor |
| n-type majority carrier | $n$ | $\approx N_D$ | m$^{-3}$ | $N_D \gg n_i$ |
| n-type minority carrier | $p$ | $n_i^2 \vert N_D$ | m$^{-3}$ | Very small |
| p-type majority carrier | $p$ | $\approx N_A$ | m$^{-3}$ | $N_A \gg n_i$ |
| p-type minority carrier | $n$ | $n_i^2 \vert N_A$ | m$^{-3}$ | Very small |
| Charge neutrality | $p + N_D^{+}$ | $n + N_A^{-}$ | m$^{-3}$ | Global condition |
| Barrier potential (Si) | $V_0$ | $0.7$ | V | At 300 K |
| Barrier potential (Ge) | $V_0$ | $0.3$ | V | At 300 K |
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | $k_B T \vert q \approx 0.026$ | V | At 300 K |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | Physical constant |
| Electron charge | $q$ | $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ | C | Physical constant |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The p-n junction is the **fundamental unit** of all modern electronics:
- **Rectifier diodes** — convert AC to DC (power supplies, battery chargers).
- **Light-Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** — p-n junctions in forward bias emit photons (display screens, traffic lights).
- **Photodiodes & Solar Cells** — p-n junctions absorb photons and generate current.
- **Zener diodes** — used as voltage regulators exploiting reverse breakdown.
- **Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs)** — two p-n junctions stacked (n-p-n or p-n-p).
- **CMOS Integrated Circuits** — billions of p-n junctions on a single chip.

Without an understanding of how a p-n junction forms, no engineer can design, fabricate, or troubleshoot modern electronic systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass-Action Law from Thermal Equilibrium

The mass-action law $np = n_i^2$ can be derived from the **Fermi-Dirac statistics** of electrons and holes in a non-degenerate semiconductor.

### Step 1: Electron Concentration in the Conduction Band

The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the product of the density of states $g_c(E)$ and the Fermi-Dirac occupation function $f(E)$ over all energies above $E_C$:

$$
n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_c(E) \cdot f(E) \, dE
$$

where the Fermi-Dirac function is:
$$
f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}
$$

For a non-degenerate semiconductor (where $E_C - E_F \gg k_B T$), the exponential in the denominator is large, and we can approximate:
$$
f(E) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Substituting the Density of States

For a parabolic conduction band:
$$
g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_C}
$$

After performing the integration (which is a standard definite integral of the form $\int_0^\infty \sqrt{x} \, e^{-x} dx$):
$$
n = N_C \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where the **effective density of states in the conduction band** is:
$$
N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

### Step 3: Hole Concentration in the Valence Band

By a similar derivation, using the probability that a state in the valence band is empty (i.e., occupied by a hole):
$$
p = N_V \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where:
$$
N_V = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

### Step 4: The Intrinsic Case

For an **intrinsic** semiconductor, the Fermi level $E_F = E_i$ (the intrinsic Fermi level), and $n = p = n_i$. Therefore:
$$
n_i = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_i}{k_B T}\right) = N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_i - E_V}{k_B T}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Multiplying n and p for the General Case

For **any** extrinsic semiconductor (whether n-type or p-type), the same $E_F$ determines both $n$ and $p$:

$$
n \cdot p = \left[N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)\right] \cdot \left[N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right)\right]
$$

$$
n \cdot p = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_V}{k_B T}\right) = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Note that the $E_F$ terms **cancel out** in the exponent. Comparing with the intrinsic case:
$$
n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Therefore:
$$
\boxed{\,n \cdot p = n_i^2\,}
$$

This is the **Law of Mass Action** — universally valid for any semiconductor in thermal equilibrium, regardless of doping.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the Built-in Potential $V_0$ Depends on Doping

The built-in potential can be derived by considering the difference in Fermi levels on the two sides of the junction at equilibrium.

### Step 1: Fermi Level in n-type Material

In the n-type region (far from the junction):
$$
E_{F,n} = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
$$

This is because $n = N_D$ in the neutral n-region, and using $n = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$:
$$
N_D = N_C \exp\left(-\frac{E_C - E_{F,n}}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Rearranging:
$$
E_C - E_{F,n} = k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Fermi Level in p-type Material

In the p-type region (far from the junction):
$$
E_{F,p} = E_V + k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)
$$

### Step 3: At Equilibrium, the Fermi Level Must Be Constant

At thermal equilibrium, the Fermi level is **flat** (constant) across the entire junction. This means the bands must bend to align the Fermi levels:

$$
E_{F,n} = E_{F,p}
$$

The total band bending is $qV_0$, which equals the difference:
$$
qV_0 = E_{F,n} - E_{F,p}
$$

### Step 4: Deriving the Formula

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:
$$
qV_0 = \left[E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)\right] - \left[E_V + k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)\right]
$$

$$
qV_0 = (E_C - E_V) - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)
$$

Since $E_C - E_V = E_g$ and using $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp(-E_g/k_B T)$:
$$
V_0 = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_0 = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)\,}
$$

where $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 0.026$ V at 300 K is the **thermal voltage**.

**Interpretation:** Higher doping (larger $N_A$ and $N_D$) leads to a higher barrier potential. For Si with $N_A = N_D = 10^{21}$ m$^{-3}$ and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ m$^{-3}$:
$$
V_0 = 0.026 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10^{21} \times 10^{21}}{(1.5 \times 10^{16})^2}\right) = 0.026 \cdot \ln(4.44 \times 10^{8}) \approx 0.026 \times 20.9 \approx 0.54 \text{ V}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: p-n Junction at a Glance

| Property | p-side | Junction Region | n-side |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mobile carriers | Holes (many), electrons (few) | Almost none | Electrons (many), holes (few) |
| Fixed ions | Negative ($B^-$, $A^-$) | Both signs present | Positive ($P^+$, $D^+$) |
| Charge | Neutral | Strongly polarized (dipole) | Neutral |
| Electric field | ≈ 0 | Maximum, points n→p | ≈ 0 |
| Potential | Lower | Linearly varies | Higher |
| Width | Wider if $N_A < N_D$ | $W$ (typically 0.1–1 μm) | Narrower if $N_D > N_A$ |
| Fermi level position | Near $E_V$ | Constant (equilibrium) | Near $E_C$ |
| Energy bands | Nearly flat | Bent (slope) | Nearly flat |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Semiconductors

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Semiconductors] --> nodeB[Intrinsic Pure]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Extrinsic Doped]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Pure Si or Ge]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["ne equals nh equals ni"]
    nodeC --> nodeD[n-type Doped with Group V]
    nodeC --> nodeE[p-type Doped with Group III]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Donor atoms e.g. P, As, Sb]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Majority electrons]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Minority holes]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Positive donor ions]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Acceptor atoms e.g. B, Ga, In]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Majority holes]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Minority electrons]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[Negative acceptor ions]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Energy Band Structure of Extrinsic Semiconductors

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Energy Band Diagrams] --> nodeB[n-type Si with P]
    nodeA --> nodeC[p-type Si with B]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Conduction Band EC]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Donor Level ED just below EC]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Fermi Level EF above ED]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Valence Band EV]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Conduction Band EC]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Acceptor Level EA just above EV]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Fermi Level EF below EA]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Valence Band EV]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3
    nodeC3 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC4
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Formation of p-n Junction

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1 Separate p-type and n-type regions] --> stepB[Step 2 Large concentration gradient at junction]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Holes diffuse p to n, electrons diffuse n to p]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Recombination near junction removes mobile carriers]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Depletion region forms with immobile ions]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Internal electric field E opposes further diffusion]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Drift current balances diffusion current]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Thermal equilibrium with barrier potential V0]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: p-n Junction Physical Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[p-region Left] --> nodeB[Depletion Width Wp with negative acceptor ions]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Metallurgical Junction at x=0]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Depletion Width Wn with positive donor ions]
    nodeD --> nodeE[n-region Right]
    nodeF[Built in electric field E points from n to p] --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeG[Barrier Potential V0 across depletion region] --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeD
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Forward Bias vs. Reverse Bias (Qualitative)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[p-n Junction with applied voltage] --> nodeB[Forward Bias V applied with p positive]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Reverse Bias V applied with n positive]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[External field opposes built in field]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Depletion width decreases]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Barrier lowered]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Majority carriers cross easily Large current flows]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[External field adds to built in field]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Depletion width increases]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Barrier raised]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Only tiny minority current Saturation]
```

## 4.6 Energy Band Diagram (Qualitative Schematic of p-n Junction at Equilibrium)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Conduction Band EC on p-side higher] --> nodeB[Bands bend across depletion region]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Conduction Band EC on n-side lower]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Quasi Fermi level EF constant across junction]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Valence Band EV on n-side lower]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Valence Band EV on p-side higher]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeG[Built in potential qV0 equals total band bending] --> nodeB
    nodeH[Donor level ED near n-side EC] --> nodeC
    nodeI[Acceptor level EA near p-side EV] --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip:** In an actual hand-drawn energy band diagram for KTU exams, draw EC and EV as two lines that are **flat** on the far left (p-side) and far right (n-side), but **bend smoothly** through the depletion region. The Fermi level $E_F$ should appear as a **single straight horizontal line** running continuously from p-side to n-side. The total vertical distance between the n-side and p-side EC levels equals $qV_0$.
