<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Intrinsic Semiconductor | Extrinsic Semiconductor |
|---|---|---|
| Purity | Pure, no impurities | Doped with specific impurities |
| Carrier concentration | $n = p = n_i$ | $n \neq p$, depends on dopant |
| Conductivity | Low, depends only on temperature | High, controlled by doping |
| Examples | Pure Si, Pure Ge | Si doped with P (n-type), Si doped with B (p-type) |

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Tabular comparison (1) + Examples (1).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Explain the formation of a depletion region in a p-n junction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a p-n junction is formed, there exists a large concentration gradient of charge carriers across the metallurgical junction. Holes diffuse from the p-side to the n-side, and electrons diffuse from the n-side to the p-side. As these carriers cross the junction, they **recombine** and disappear, leaving behind a thin region near the junction that is depleted of mobile carriers. This region contains only the **immobile ionized dopants** — negative acceptor ions on the p-side and positive donor ions on the n-side. This charge-separated region is called the **depletion region** (or space-charge region). The separated charges create a built-in electric field that opposes further diffusion, eventually establishing **thermal equilibrium**.

**Mark distribution:** Diffusion + Recombination (1) + Immobile ions remain (1) + Field & equilibrium (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is doping? Explain the formation of n-type and p-type semiconductors with neat energy band diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the formation of a p-n junction and explain the concept of depletion region and barrier potential. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Doping (1 mark):**
Doping is the process of intentionally introducing a small, controlled amount of specific impurity atoms (dopants) into an intrinsic semiconductor crystal to modify its electrical properties — specifically, to increase the number of free charge carriers and thereby enhance conductivity.

**n-type semiconductor (3 marks):**
- Formed by doping a group IV semiconductor (Si) with a **group V (pentavalent)** element such as Phosphorus (P).
- The 5th valence electron of P is loosely bound and easily liberated into the conduction band at room temperature.
- P becomes a positively charged **donor ion ($P^+$)**.
- **Majority carriers = electrons**, minority carriers = holes.
- Donor energy level $E_D$ lies just below the conduction band edge $E_C$ (≈ 0.05 eV for Si:P).
- The Fermi level $E_F$ shifts **upward**, closer to $E_C$.

**p-type semiconductor (3 marks):**
- Formed by doping Si with a **group III (trivalent)** element such as Boron (B).
- B has only 3 valence electrons, leaving one covalent bond incomplete → a **hole** is created in the valence band.
- B becomes a negatively charged **acceptor ion ($B^-$)**.
- **Majority carriers = holes**, minority carriers = electrons.
- Acceptor energy level $E_A$ lies just above the valence band edge $E_V$ (≈ 0.045 eV for Si:B).
- The Fermi level $E_F$ shifts **downward**, closer to $E_V$.

**Energy Band Diagram (see Section 4.2 above for Mermaid schematic):**

For n-type: EC is at top, then a thin gap, then the donor level ED just below EC, the Fermi level EF slightly below ED, and EV at the bottom.

For p-type: EC at top, the Fermi level EF slightly above the acceptor level EA, then EA just above EV.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Junction formation (2 marks):**
When a p-type region and an n-type region are brought into contact within a single crystal, a sharp metallurgical junction is formed at $x = 0$. On the p-side, holes are abundant; on the n-side, electrons are abundant.

**Step 2 — Diffusion and recombination (2 marks):**
Due to concentration gradients, holes diffuse from p to n, and electrons diffuse from n to p. They recombine near the junction and annihilate each other.

**Step 3 — Depletion region (2 marks):**
A thin region on both sides of the junction is left with only immobile ionized dopants: negative acceptor ions ($B^-$) on the p-side and positive donor ions ($P^+$) on the n-side. This region, devoid of mobile carriers, is called the **depletion region** (typical width 0.1–1 μm for Si).

**Step 4 — Barrier potential (1 mark):**
The space charge creates a built-in electric field $E$ pointing from n to p, and a corresponding **barrier potential $V_0$** (≈ 0.7 V for Si) across the depletion region. This barrier prevents further net diffusion of majority carriers, establishing thermal equilibrium.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.4):** Draw p-region on the left with negative ion symbols ($-$), depletion region in the middle, n-region on the right with positive ion symbols ($+$). Indicate the built-in field arrow from n to p, and label $V_0$ across the depletion region.

---

### Question B (Choice B) — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the terms: (i) donor, (ii) acceptor, (iii) majority carrier, (iv) minority carrier. With a suitable example, explain why the conductivity of an extrinsic semiconductor is much higher than that of an intrinsic one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the energy band diagram of a p-n junction at equilibrium and explain the role of the Fermi level in determining the barrier height. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definitions (4 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Donor:** An impurity atom (group V) that donates a free electron to the conduction band of the host semiconductor. Example: Phosphorus in Silicon.
2. **Acceptor:** An impurity atom (group III) that accepts an electron from the valence band, creating a hole. Example: Boron in Silicon.
3. **Majority carrier:** The type of charge carrier that is present in larger numbers in a given semiconductor. Example: Electrons in n-type, holes in p-type.
4. **Minority carrier:** The type of charge carrier that is present in smaller numbers. Example: Holes in n-type, electrons in p-type.

**Conductivity comparison (3 marks):**
For intrinsic Si at 300 K, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ m$^{-3}$, and conductivity is:
$$
\sigma_i = n_i q (\mu_e + \mu_h) \approx 4.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/m}
$$
For n-type Si doped with $N_D = 10^{21}$ m$^{-3}$ phosphorus:
$$
\sigma_n \approx N_D q \mu_e \approx 10^{21} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.14 \approx 22.4 \text{ S/m}
$$
The conductivity is increased by a factor of about **$5 \times 10^{4}$**. This is because each donor atom contributes one free electron, and the donor concentration $N_D$ is typically $10^4$ to $10^6$ times larger than the intrinsic concentration $n_i$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Energy band diagram at equilibrium (4 marks):**
- Draw the conduction band edge $E_C$ and valence band edge $E_V$ for both p-side (left) and n-side (right).
- On the p-side, the Fermi level $E_F$ lies close to $E_V$ (just above the acceptor level $E_A$).
- On the n-side, $E_F$ lies close to $E_C$ (just below the donor level $E_D$).
- At equilibrium, the Fermi level is **flat** (a single horizontal line across the entire diagram), since the system is in thermal equilibrium.
- The bands **bend** in the depletion region to align the Fermi levels. The total vertical band bending equals $qV_0$.

**Role of Fermi level in barrier height (3 marks):**
- The barrier potential $qV_0$ is the energy required for an electron to move from the n-side to the p-side (or for a hole to move from p-side to n-side).
- From the band diagram: $qV_0 = E_{F,n} - E_{F,p}$ (the difference in Fermi level positions on the two sides of the junction).
- Larger doping concentrations push $E_F$ further from the band edges (deeper into the band for n-type, deeper into the band for p-type), thereby **increasing** the band-bending $qV_0$ and the barrier height.
- The quantitative relation is: $V_0 = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.6):** Show flat bands on both far ends, a smooth S-shaped band bending in the middle, a horizontal $E_F$ line throughout, and label $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_D$, $E_A$, and $qV_0$ clearly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing majority and minority carriers** — Remember: in n-type, electrons are majority; in p-type, holes are majority. (Loss: 1 mark per error)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention ionized dopants remain immobile** — Many students describe the depletion region as "having no charges," which is WRONG. The depletion region has **immobile ions**, not zero charge. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Drawing the Fermi level as bent in the depletion region** — At equilibrium, the Fermi level is FLAT (constant). Bending is in $E_C$ and $E_V$, not in $E_F$. This is a critical error. (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 4. **Stating the barrier potential as 0.7 V for ALL semiconductors** — This value is specific to **Silicon**. For Ge it is ≈ 0.3 V, for GaAs ≈ 1.1 V. (Loss: 1 mark if asked for justification)
>
> 5. **Skipping the energy band diagram in p-n junction questions** — KTU examiners expect a properly labeled energy band diagram. Drawing it without labeling $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, and $V_0$ fetches only partial credit. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
>
> 6. **Confusing "doping" with "alloying"** — Doping is at ppm/ppb levels; alloying is at much higher concentrations. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 7. **Forgetting the mass-action law $np = n_i^2$** — This is a frequently tested relation. Failing to apply it in numerical problems leads to incorrect minority carrier concentrations. (Loss: 2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Topic**

- [ ] **Intrinsic semiconductor:** Pure Si/Ge; $n = p = n_i$; conductivity depends only on temperature.
- [ ] **Extrinsic semiconductor:** Doped; conductivity is dominated by dopant-controlled majority carriers.
- [ ] **n-type:** Group V dopant (P, As, Sb) in Si; **majority = electrons**, minority = holes; $n \approx N_D$.
- [ ] **p-type:** Group III dopant (B, Ga, In) in Si; **majority = holes**, minority = electrons; $p \approx N_A$.
- [ ] **Donor level $E_D$:** Lies just below $E_C$ (≈ 0.05 eV for Si:P).
- [ ] **Acceptor level $E_A$:** Lies just above $E_V$ (≈ 0.045 eV for Si:B).
- [ ] **Fermi level shift:** n-type → $E_F$ moves up toward $E_C$; p-type → $E_F$ moves down toward $E_V$.
- [ ] **Mass-action law:** $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ (valid at equilibrium, any doping).
- [ ] **Charge neutrality:** $p + N_D^{+} = n + N_A^{-}$.
- [ ] **p-n junction formation:** Diffusion of majority carriers → recombination → immobile ions left behind → depletion region → built-in $E$-field → equilibrium.
- [ ] **Depletion region:** Thin layer (0.1–1 μm) with only immobile ions, no mobile carriers.
- [ ] **Barrier potential $V_0$:** ≈ 0.7 V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge, 1.1 V for GaAs (at 300 K).
- [ ] **Fermi level at equilibrium:** Flat (constant) across the entire p-n junction.
- [ ] **Energy bands:** Bend smoothly in the depletion region; total bending = $qV_0$.
- [ ] **Built-in field:** Points from n-side to p-side (from + ions to − ions).
- [ ] **Physical constants to memorize:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V_T \approx 0.026$ V at 300 K.
- [ ] **Formula for $V_0$:** $V_0 = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — higher doping gives higher barrier.
- [ ] **Engineering relevance:** p-n junction = basis of diodes, transistors, LEDs, solar cells, ICs.

---

*End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Extrinsic Semiconductor (Qualitative). Formation of p-n Junction*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermi Level in Semiconductors - Intrinsic and Extrinsic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermi Level in Semiconductors - Intrinsic and Extrinsic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fermi Level — Formal Definition

The **Fermi Level** ($E_F$) is defined as the energy level inside a material at which the probability of finding an electron is exactly **50 %** at thermodynamic equilibrium. Mathematically, it is the energy reference in the **Fermi–Dirac distribution function** that governs the statistical occupancy of available quantum states by electrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fermi–Dirac Distribution Function**
>
> $$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_BT}}$$
>
> where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the **Boltzmann constant** and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin. When $E = E_F$, $f(E) = 0.5$, confirming the definition.

In semiconductor physics, $E_F$ is **not a fixed energy level** like a discrete atomic orbital — it is a **statistical reference** that can lie anywhere within the forbidden energy gap depending on the carrier concentrations, doping type, and temperature.

## 1.2 Why is the Fermi Level Important in Semiconductors?

The position of $E_F$ relative to the **conduction band edge** ($E_C$) and the **valence band edge** ($E_V$) directly determines:

1. The concentration of free **electrons** in the conduction band.
2. The concentration of free **holes** in the valence band.
3. The **type** of majority carrier and hence the semiconductor classification (n-type or p-type).
4. The behaviour of **p–n junctions**, transistors, and most solid-state devices.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Level Reservoir

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
>
> Imagine a multi-storey water tank. Each storey represents a different energy level. The **Fermi level is like the "water surface level"**. If the water surface is high, the upper storeys (conduction band) are filled with water (electrons) — that is **n-type** behaviour. If the water surface is low, the lower storeys (valence band) are full but the upper ones are nearly empty — that is **p-type** behaviour. If the water level sits exactly in the middle, very few electrons escape to the top — that is the **intrinsic** case.
>
> Doping is equivalent to either pumping water in (donors → n-type) or pumping water out (acceptors → p-type), shifting the water surface (Fermi level) up or down.

## 1.4 Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Semiconductors — Quick Distinction

| Parameter | Intrinsic | Extrinsic |
|---|---|---|
| Purity | Pure semiconductor (no impurities) | Doped with donor / acceptor impurities |
| Carrier concentration | $n = p = n_i$ | $n \neq p$ |
| Fermi level position | Near mid-gap | Close to $E_C$ (n-type) or $E_V$ (p-type) |
| Conductivity | Low, strongly temperature dependent | High, controlled by doping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must clearly derive and interpret the position of $E_F$ in **(i) intrinsic**, **(ii) n-type**, and **(iii) p-type** semiconductors and explain its variation with temperature and doping concentration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recap — Carrier Concentration in Bands

The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the density of states $g_C(E)$ with the Fermi function:

$$n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_C(E)\, f(E)\, dE$$

Assuming $(E_C - E_F) \gg k_BT$ (non-degenerate case, valid for typical KTU-level problems), the standard result is:

$$n = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}$$

Similarly, for holes in the valence band:

$$p = N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$$

where $N_C$ and $N_V$ are the **effective densities of states** in the conduction and valence bands respectively, and $E_g = E_C - E_V$ is the **band-gap energy**.

## 2.2 Fermi Level in an Intrinsic Semiconductor

For a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor, charge neutrality demands:

$$n = p = n_i$$

Equating the two exponential expressions:

$$N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_{Fi})/k_BT} = N_V\, e^{-(E_{Fi} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

Solving for $E_{Fi}$ gives the **intrinsic Fermi level**:

$$\boxed{E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4}\, k_B T\, \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the special case $N_C = N_V$ (e.g. at 0 K, or in a hypothetical symmetric band), the logarithmic term vanishes and $E_{Fi}$ lies exactly at the **mid-gap**. In real materials, the small second term shifts $E_{Fi}$ slightly above or below mid-gap depending on whether $N_V > N_C$ or $N_C > N_V$.

## 2.3 Fermi Level in an n-Type Semiconductor

In an n-type material, donor atoms contribute electrons. Under the assumption of **complete ionisation** and $N_D \gg n_i$:

$$n \approx N_D$$

Substituting $n = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT} = N_D$:

$$\boxed{E_F = E_C - k_B T\, \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fermi level lies **below** the conduction band edge by an amount $k_BT \ln(N_C/N_D)$. Heavier doping (larger $N_D$) pushes $E_F$ **closer to $E_C$**. In the **degenerate limit** $N_D \to N_C$, the Fermi level can even enter the conduction band.

## 2.4 Fermi Level in a p-Type Semiconductor

By analogous reasoning, with acceptors of concentration $N_A$ (assuming complete ionisation, $N_A \gg n_i$):

$$p \approx N_A = N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$$

$$\boxed{E_F = E_V + k_B T\, \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Fermi level lies **above** the valence band edge by $k_BT \ln(N_V/N_A)$. Heavier doping pushes $E_F$ **closer to $E_V$**, and in the degenerate limit it can even enter the valence band.

## 2.5 Mass-Action Law

Multiplying the expressions for $n$ and $p$ eliminates $E_F$:

$$n \cdot p = N_C N_V\, e^{-E_g/k_BT} = n_i^{\,2}$$

This is the **Law of Mass Action**, an extremely important KTU-result.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**
> This relation shows that increasing the majority carrier concentration (by doping) automatically decreases the minority carrier concentration. This is the working principle behind controlling leakage current, photodetector sensitivity, and transistor gain in devices such as MOSFETs, BJTs, and solar cells.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fermi function | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_BT}}$ | All semiconductors |
| 2 | Electron concentration | $n = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}$ | Non-degenerate, $E_C - E_F \gg k_BT$ |
| 3 | Hole concentration | $p = N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$ | Non-degenerate, $E_F - E_V \gg k_BT$ |
| 4 | Intrinsic Fermi level | $E_{Fi} = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4}k_BT\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$ | Intrinsic, $n = p = n_i$ |
| 5 | n-type Fermi level | $E_F = E_C - k_BT\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$ | $N_D \gg n_i$, complete ionisation |
| 6 | p-type Fermi level | $E_F = E_V + k_BT\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_V}{N_A}\right)$ | $N_A \gg n_i$, complete ionisation |
| 7 | Mass-action law | $n \cdot p = n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V e^{-E_g/k_BT}$ | Thermal equilibrium |
| 8 | Effective density of states | $N_C = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_e^{\ast} k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 9 | Effective density of states | $N_V = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_h^{\ast} k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 10 | Intrinsic carrier conc. | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\, e^{-E_g/2k_BT}$ | Intrinsic |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **MOSFET Design:** Threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ depends on the work function, which in turn depends on $E_F$ relative to the vacuum level.
2. **Photovoltaic Cells:** Open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ is proportional to the splitting of quasi-Fermi levels under illumination.
3. **Thermoelectrics:** The Seebeck coefficient is governed by the energy difference $E_C - E_F$.
4. **LED / Laser Diodes:** The emitted photon energy is tuned by engineering the band gap and Fermi level positions in the heterojunction active region.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Intrinsic Fermi Level $E_{Fi}$

**Statement:** For an intrinsic semiconductor, the Fermi level lies (almost) at the centre of the forbidden gap.

**Starting point — Charge neutrality condition:**

$$n = p$$

**Step 1 — Write the carrier concentrations using the Boltzmann-approximated Fermi function.**

$$n = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_{Fi})/k_BT}$$

$$p = N_V\, e^{-(E_{Fi} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 2 — Equate $n$ and $p$.**

$$N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_{Fi})/k_BT} = N_V\, e^{-(E_{Fi} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides.**

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{k_BT} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{k_BT}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange terms to isolate $E_{Fi}$.**

$$\frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{k_BT} - \frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{k_BT} = \ln N_V - \ln N_C$$

$$\frac{2E_{Fi} - (E_C + E_V)}{k_BT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $k_BT$ and solve.**

$$2E_{Fi} = E_C + E_V + k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\, k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks (e.g. Streetman & Banerjee) use a slightly different prefactor of $\tfrac{3}{4}$ if a temperature-dependent effective mass is incorporated. The $\tfrac{1}{2}$ form above assumes constant effective mass, which is the KTU-board-accepted form.

**Step 6 — Limiting case.** If $N_C = N_V$, then $\ln(N_V/N_C) = 0$ and

$$E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} \quad \text{(mid-gap)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fermi Level in an n-Type Semiconductor

**Assumption:** Complete ionisation of donors, $N_D \gg n_i$, hence electron concentration is set by donors.

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality (majority-carrier dominated):**

$$n \approx N_D$$

**Step 2 — Express $n$ via the Boltzmann form:**

$$N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT} = N_D$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the exponential factor:**

$$e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT} = \frac{N_D}{N_C}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_BT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E_F$:**

$$E_F = E_C - k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.** The shift from the conduction band edge is

$$\Delta E = E_C - E_F = k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) > 0$$

confirming $E_F$ lies **below** $E_C$. As $N_D$ increases, $\Delta E$ decreases, pushing $E_F$ upward toward $E_C$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fermi Level in a p-Type Semiconductor

**Assumption:** $N_A \gg n_i$, complete ionisation, hole-dominated.

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality:**

$$p \approx N_A$$

**Step 2 — Boltzmann form for holes:**

$$N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT} = N_A$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the exponential:**

$$e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT} = \frac{N_A}{N_V}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural log:**

$$-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_BT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{N_V}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\,E_F = E_V + k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.** The shift above $E_V$ is

$$E_F - E_V = k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right) > 0$$

As $N_A$ increases, this shift shrinks, pushing $E_F$ **down** toward $E_V$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mass-Action Law

**Step 1 — Multiply the general expressions for $n$ and $p$:**

$$n \cdot p = \left[N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}\right] \cdot \left[N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Combine the exponentials — the $E_F$ terms cancel:**

$$n \cdot p = N_C N_V\, e^{-(E_C - E_V)/k_BT} = N_C N_V\, e^{-E_g/k_BT}$$

**Step 3 — In an intrinsic semiconductor $n = p = n_i$:**

$$n_i \cdot n_i = n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V\, e^{-E_g/k_BT}$$

**Step 4 — Generalisation (independent of doping):**

$$\boxed{\,n \cdot p = n_i^{\,2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result holds for **any** semiconductor at thermal equilibrium regardless of whether it is intrinsic or extrinsic. It is the foundation for analysing minority-carrier diffusion lengths, recombination currents, and open-circuit voltages in solar cells.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration in Terms of $E_g$

From the mass-action result $n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V e^{-E_g/k_BT}$:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\; e^{-E_g/2k_BT}$$

This makes explicit why semiconductors with **larger band gaps** (Si $\sim 1.1$ eV, GaAs $\sim 1.42$ eV, diamond $\sim 5.5$ eV) have exponentially smaller intrinsic carrier densities at a given temperature.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part):**
> For silicon at 300 K, $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$. Calculate (a) the intrinsic Fermi level position relative to mid-gap, and (b) the Fermi level in an n-type sample with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$.

**Solution (a):**

The shift from mid-gap is

$$\Delta E_{Fi} = \frac{1}{2} k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

At $T = 300$ K, $k_BT = (8.617 \times 10^{-5})(300) = 0.02585$ eV.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.04 \times 10^{19}}{2.8 \times 10^{19}}\right) = \ln(0.3714) = -0.9905$$

$$\Delta E_{Fi} = \frac{1}{2}(0.02585)(-0.9905) = -0.01280 \text{ eV}$$

So $E_{Fi}$ lies about **12.8 meV below mid-gap** — slightly closer to the valence band.

**Solution (b):**

$$E_C - E_F = k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.02585 \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right)$$

$$= 0.02585 \ln(2800) = 0.02585 \times 7.937 = 0.2052 \text{ eV}$$

So the Fermi level lies **0.205 eV below $E_C$** (i.e. comfortably inside the band gap, since $E_g = 1.12$ eV).

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — How $E_F$ Moves

| Change in System | Effect on $E_F$ |
|---|---|
| Add donors (increase $N_D$) in n-type | $E_F$ moves **up**, closer to $E_C$ |
| Add acceptors (increase $N_A$) in p-type | $E_F$ moves **down**, closer to $E_V$ |
| Increase temperature (low doping) | $E_F$ moves toward **mid-gap** $E_{Fi}$ |
| Increase temperature (high doping) | $E_F$ first moves toward $E_{Fi}$, then intrinsic behaviour dominates |
| Increase band gap $E_g$ | Carrier density $\downarrow$ exponentially, $E_F$ anchored more strongly to band edge |
| Compensation (both donors and acceptors present) | $E_F$ set by $N_D - N_A$ net doping |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fermi Level Position Map

This Mermaid block diagram maps out the logic governing where the Fermi level resides in different semiconductor types, replacing a physical energy-band sketch with a structured functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Semiconductor Material] --> B{Is material doped?}
    B -- No --> C[Intrinsic Semiconductor]
    C --> C1[Charge neutrality: n = p = n_i]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Boltzmann forms for n and p]
    C2 --> C3[Equate and solve for E_Fi]
    C3 --> C4[E_Fi equals mid-gap plus half kT ln N_V over N_C]

    B -- Yes --> D{Donor or Acceptor doping?}
    D -- Donor, N_D dominant --> E[n-type Semiconductor]
    E --> E1[Charge neutrality: n approx N_D]
    E1 --> E2[Use n = N_C exp of minus E_C minus E_F over kT]
    E2 --> E3[Solve for E_F]
    E3 --> E4[E_F = E_C minus kT ln N_C over N_D]
    E4 --> E5[E_F lies BELOW E_C inside the band gap]

    D -- Acceptor, N_A dominant --> F[p-type Semiconductor]
    F --> F1[Charge neutrality: p approx N_A]
    F1 --> F2[Use p = N_V exp of minus E_F minus E_V over kT]
    F2 --> F3[Solve for E_F]
    F3 --> F4[E_F = E_V plus kT ln N_V over N_A]
    F4 --> F5[E_F lies ABOVE E_V inside the band gap]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Temperature Dependence of $E_F$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Very low T, freeze-out] --> T1[Donors or acceptors not yet ionised]
    T1 --> T2[E_F pinned near donor or acceptor level]
    T2 --> T3[As T increases, E_F moves toward band edge of majority carriers]
    T3 --> T4[Intermediate T, extrinsic region]
    T4 --> T5[E_F at E_C minus kT ln N_C over N_D for n-type]
    T5 --> T6[Higher T, intrinsic region]
    T6 --> T7[E_F approaches E_Fi near mid-gap]
    T7 --> T8[Highest T, fully intrinsic, n = p = n_i]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Interaction Between Carrier Densities and $E_F$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EFERM[E_F position] --> NCTRL[Electron density n]
    EFERM --> PCTRL[Hole density p]
    NCTRL --> MASSACT[Mass action law: n times p = n_i squared]
    PCTRL --> MASSACT
    MASSACT --> DEVMIN[Minority carrier control in devices]
    DEVMIN --> APPL1[Photovoltaic open-circuit voltage]
    DEVMIN --> APPL2[BJT base current]
    DEVMIN --> APPL3[MOSFET subthreshold leakage]
    DOPECTRL[Doping type and concentration] --> EFERM
    TEMPCTRL[Absolute temperature T] --> EFERM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams above:**
> - The first chart shows the **logical derivation tree** that leads to the three $E_F$ formulas (intrinsic, n-type, p-type).
> - The second chart shows **how $E_F$ migrates** as temperature rises from cryogenic to very high values.
> - The third chart shows the **engineering relevance** of $E_F$: it controls both carrier densities, which feed into device-level behaviour via the mass-action law.

For students who benefit from a quick textual picture of the band structure:

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Fermi level. Where does it lie in an intrinsic semiconductor and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fermi level** ($E_F$) is the energy at which the probability of electron occupation is exactly 0.5 according to the Fermi–Dirac distribution function $f(E) = 1/(1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_BT})$. In an **intrinsic semiconductor**, charge neutrality demands $n = p = n_i$. Equating the Boltzmann expressions for $n$ and $p$ and solving gives

$$E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{1}{2}k_BT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

For $N_C \approx N_V$, the logarithmic term is small and $E_{Fi}$ lies **very close to the mid-gap**, because electrons and holes are generated in equal numbers by thermal excitation across the band gap.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain how the position of the Fermi level changes when an n-type semiconductor is heavily doped.**

**Model Answer:**

For an n-type semiconductor with donor concentration $N_D$ (assuming complete ionisation),

$$E_F = E_C - k_BT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

As $N_D$ **increases**, the ratio $N_C/N_D$ decreases, and so does the term $k_BT\ln(N_C/N_D)$. This means $E_F$ moves **upward** toward the conduction band edge. In the **degenerate (heavily doped)** limit, $N_D$ approaches or exceeds $N_C$, and the Fermi level can even move **inside the conduction band** — leading to band-gap narrowing and metallic-like (degenerate) conduction. This regime is critical in heavily doped emitter regions of BJTs and the source/drain regions of modern nanoscale MOSFETs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

### Question B1. (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**a)** Derive an expression for the position of the Fermi level in an **intrinsic semiconductor**. Discuss how it varies with temperature. (7 marks)

**b)** Derive an expression for the Fermi level in an **n-type semiconductor** and explain the effect of doping concentration and temperature on its position. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Intrinsic condition:** $n = p = n_i$ (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Write the Boltzmann-approximated carrier concentrations:**

$$n_i = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_{Fi})/k_BT}$$

$$n_i = N_V\, e^{-(E_{Fi} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

(1 mark for correct expressions)

**Step 3 — Equate and take the natural logarithm:**

$$N_C e^{-(E_C - E_{Fi})/k_BT} = N_V e^{-(E_{Fi} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{k_BT} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{k_BT}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_{Fi}$:**

$$2E_{Fi} = E_C + E_V + k_BT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{1}{2}k_BT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)\,}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Temperature variation discussion (1 mark):**
- At $T = 0$ K, the logarithmic term vanishes only if $N_C = N_V$; otherwise a small offset remains.
- The ratio $N_V/N_C$ is temperature dependent (through effective masses and $T^{3/2}$ dependence), so $E_{Fi}$ shifts slightly with temperature.
- For $N_C = N_V$, $E_{Fi}$ sits exactly at mid-gap for all temperatures.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Charge neutrality for n-type (complete ionisation):** $n \approx N_D$ (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Boltzmann form:** $N_C e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT} = N_D$ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$E_F = E_C - k_BT\ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Effect of doping (2 marks):**
- $E_F - E_C = -k_BT\ln(N_C/N_D)$.
- Higher $N_D$ → smaller difference → $E_F$ moves **closer to $E_C$**.
- In the degenerate limit $N_D \gtrsim N_C$, $E_F$ moves **into the conduction band**.

**Step 5 — Effect of temperature (1 mark):**
- At fixed $N_D$, raising $T$ increases the multiplicative factor $k_BT$ in the logarithmic term, pulling $E_F$ **down** from $E_C$ back toward mid-gap.
- At very high $T$, $n_i$ becomes comparable to $N_D$ and the material reverts to **intrinsic behaviour**, with $E_F \to E_{Fi}$.

---

### Question B1. (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Derive the **mass-action law** $np = n_i^{\,2}$ for a semiconductor in thermal equilibrium and explain its significance. (7 marks)

**b)** For a p-type silicon sample at 300 K with $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, and $E_g = 1.12$ eV, calculate the position of the Fermi level relative to the valence band edge and estimate the minority carrier (electron) concentration. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write $n$ and $p$ in Boltzmann form (2 marks):**

$$n = N_C\, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}, \quad p = N_V\, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply (1 mark):**

$$np = N_C N_V\, e^{-(E_C + E_V - 2E_F)/k_BT}$$

Wait — correctly grouped:

$$np = N_C N_V\, e^{-(E_C - E_V)/k_BT} = N_C N_V\, e^{-E_g/k_BT}$$

because the $E_F$ terms cancel. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — For intrinsic material, $n = p = n_i$ (1 mark):**

$$n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V\, e^{-E_g/k_BT}$$

**Step 4 — Combine and state the result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,np = n_i^{\,2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Significance (1 mark):**
- Independent of doping — the product of majority and minority carrier densities is a constant at fixed $T$.
- Heavy doping decreases minority carriers, enabling precise control of leakage, diffusion length, and recombination in devices.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the p-type Fermi level expression (2 marks):**

$$E_F - E_V = k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Insert values (1 mark):**

$$k_BT = (8.617 \times 10^{-5})(300) = 0.02585 \text{ eV}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.04 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right) = \ln(1040) = 6.947$$

**Step 3 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$E_F - E_V = (0.02585)(6.947) = 0.1796 \text{ eV}$$

So $E_F$ lies **0.18 eV above $E_V$** (about 16 % up into the 1.12 eV band gap).

**Step 4 — Minority carrier concentration (1 mark):**

First compute $n_i$:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\; e^{-E_g/2k_BT} = \sqrt{(2.8 \times 10^{19})(1.04 \times 10^{19})}\; e^{-1.12/(2 \times 0.02585)}$$

$$= \sqrt{2.912 \times 10^{38}}\; e^{-21.66}$$

$$= 1.706 \times 10^{19} \times 4.05 \times 10^{-10} = 6.91 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Apply mass-action law (1 mark):**

$$n = \frac{n_i^{\,2}}{p} = \frac{n_i^{\,2}}{N_A} = \frac{(6.91 \times 10^{9})^2}{10^{16}} = \frac{4.77 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}$$

$$\boxed{\,n \approx 4.77 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-3}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):**
The minority electron density is about **12 orders of magnitude smaller** than the majority hole density, demonstrating the power of doping to suppress minority carriers — a cornerstone of device physics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in logarithmic arguments:** Students frequently write $E_F = E_C - k_BT\ln(N_D/N_C)$ instead of $E_C - k_BT\ln(N_C/N_D)$. The argument of the log must be the **ratio that makes $\Delta E$ positive** when $E_F$ lies below $E_C$. Always cross-check: $E_F$ should lie **below** $E_C$ for n-type, **above** $E_V$ for p-type.
>
> 2. **Confusing intrinsic and extrinsic expressions:** Students sometimes apply the intrinsic formula to a doped sample or vice versa. Always re-derive the expression under the correct charge-neutrality condition ($n = p$ vs. $n = N_D$ or $p = N_A$).
>
> 3. **Forgetting units of $k_BT$:** Use $k_BT = 0.02585$ eV at 300 K. Mixing J and eV in the same equation leads to a full-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Skipping the temperature discussion:** A 7-mark question in KTU typically awards 1–2 marks for a clear physical discussion of how $E_F$ moves with $T$ or doping. Do **not** derive the formula in isolation without explaining its meaning.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the mass-action law:** Many problems on $E_F$ implicitly require $np = n_i^{\,2}$ to find the minority carrier density. Forgetting this connection loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fermi Level in Semiconductors**

- The **Fermi level** $E_F$ is the energy at which electron occupation probability is 50 %.
- The **Fermi–Dirac function** is $f(E) = 1/(1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_BT})$.
- In the **non-degenerate limit**, carrier densities follow Boltzmann statistics:
  - $n = N_C e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}$
  - $p = N_V e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$
- **Intrinsic Fermi level:**
  $E_{Fi} = (E_C + E_V)/2 + (1/2)k_BT\ln(N_V/N_C)$ — at mid-gap if $N_C = N_V$.
- **n-type Fermi level:**
  $E_F = E_C - k_BT\ln(N_C/N_D)$ — lies **below** $E_C$.
- **p-type Fermi level:**
  $E_F = E_V + k_BT\ln(N_V/N_A)$ — lies **above** $E_V$.
- **Mass-action law:** $np = n_i^{\,2}$ — holds at thermal equilibrium for any semiconductor.
- **Intrinsic carrier density:** $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\, e^{-E_g/2k_BT}$.
- **Temperature behaviour:**
  - At low $T$: freeze-out — $E_F$ pinned near donor / acceptor level.
  - At intermediate $T$: extrinsic — $E_F$ near band edge of majority carriers.
  - At high $T$: intrinsic — $E_F \to E_{Fi}$ at mid-gap.
- **Doping trend:**
  - Higher $N_D$ in n-type → $E_F$ rises toward $E_C$.
  - Higher $N_A$ in p-type → $E_F$ falls toward $E_V$.
- **Degenerate limit:** $E_F$ enters the conduction / valence band; Boltzmann approximation breaks down.
- **Standard $k_BT$ at 300 K:** **0.02585 eV** (or $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J).
- **Effective density of states:** $N_C, N_V \propto T^{3/2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Band Diagram of p-n Junction - Qualitative Description of Charge Flow Across a p-n Junction - Forward and Reverse Biased p-n Junctions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Band Diagram of p-n Junction - Qualitative Description of Charge Flow Across a p-n Junction - Forward and Reverse Biased p-n Junctions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The p-n Junction — Formal Definition

A **p-n junction** is the fundamental building block of modern semiconductor electronics. It is formed when a **p-type semiconductor** (excess holes as majority carriers, doped with acceptor impurities like Boron) is brought into intimate atomic contact with an **n-type semiconductor** (excess electrons as majority carriers, doped with donor impurities like Phosphorus). At the atomic interface, a region known as the **depletion region** (or space-charge region) is formed, across which a built-in electric field exists, and through which controlled charge transport can be achieved by applying an external bias.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A p-n junction is the cornerstone device from which diodes, BJTs, LEDs, solar cells, and integrated circuits (ICs) are derived. Mastering the energy band diagram is **mandatory** for KTU board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Dam" Model

Think of the p-n junction like a **hydroelectric dam** between two reservoirs:

- The **p-side** is a high lake filled with "water" (holes) at a high energy level.
- The **n-side** is a low lake filled with "water" (electrons) at a low energy level.
- The **depletion region** acts as the **dam wall**, which has a height (the built-in potential $V_{bi}$).
- When you **lower the dam** by applying **forward bias**, water (current) rushes downhill — a large current flows.
- When you **raise the dam higher** by applying **reverse bias**, no water can flow — only a tiny trickle (leakage current) exists.

This dam metaphor perfectly captures the **rectifying (one-way valve) nature** of a p-n junction diode.

## 1.3 The Energy Band Diagram — A Visual Snapshot

In an isolated p-type and n-type semiconductor, the **Fermi level ($E_F$)** sits close to the valence band ($E_V$) on the p-side and close to the conduction band ($E_C$) on the n-side. When they are joined to form a junction, electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side **diffuse** across the junction and recombine. This diffusion leaves behind ionized donors (positive) on the n-side and ionized acceptors (negative) on the p-side, creating a **space-charge (depletion) region** with a built-in electric field $E_{built}$.

The result? The energy bands **bend** near the junction so that a single, uniform Fermi level $E_F$ is established across the entire structure at **thermal equilibrium**. The amount of bending is exactly equal to the built-in potential energy $qV_{bi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** At thermal equilibrium (no external bias), the Fermi level is **flat (constant) throughout the entire device**. This is the single most important visual rule for drawing energy band diagrams.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

The following standard values are used universally in KTU numerical problems:

- Electronic charge: **$q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$**
- Boltzmann constant: **$k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$**
- Thermal voltage at room temperature (300 K): **$V_T = kT/q \approx 0.0259 \text{ V} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$**
- Permittivity of Silicon: **$\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$**
- Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K: **$n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$**
- Bandgap of Silicon at 300 K: **$E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Formation of the Energy Band Diagram at Equilibrium

Let us walk through the *Why* and *How* of the energy band formation:

### Step 1 — The Isolated Semiconductors
- In a **p-type** material: $E_F$ lies just above $E_V$ (because acceptors create empty states near the valence band).
- In an **n-type** material: $E_F$ lies just below $E_C$ (because donors add electrons near the conduction band).

### Step 2 — Joining the Two Materials
- A large **concentration gradient** exists for electrons (high on n-side) and holes (high on p-side).
- **Electrons diffuse** from n → p, and **holes diffuse** from p → n.

### Step 3 — Formation of the Depletion Region
- As electrons leave the n-side, they leave behind **immobile positive donor ions ($N_D^+$)**.
- As holes leave the p-side, they leave behind **immobile negative acceptor ions ($N_A^-$)**.
- These uncovered ions form the **depletion region (W)**, which is **depleted of mobile carriers**.

### Step 4 — Built-in Electric Field and Equilibrium
- The exposed ions create a **built-in electric field** $E_{built}$ pointing from n → p.
- This field opposes further diffusion. A dynamic equilibrium is reached when the **drift current** exactly balances the **diffusion current**.
- At equilibrium, $E_F$ becomes **flat** across the junction — the bands must bend by an amount $qV_{bi}$ to achieve this flatness.

### Step 5 — Visualization of Band Bending
- The conduction band $E_C$ and valence band $E_V$ **bend upward** on the p-side relative to the n-side.
- The total band bending equals $qV_{bi}$, where $V_{bi}$ is the **built-in (contact) potential**.

## 2.2 Why Does Band Bending Happen? — A Deeper Look

The local electrostatic potential $\phi(x)$ in the depletion region satisfies **Poisson's equation**:

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

The band edges are related to the potential by:
$$E_C(x) = -q\phi(x) + \text{const}, \quad E_V(x) = E_C(x) - E_g$$

A non-zero $\phi(x)$ inside the depletion region **directly produces** the band bending observed in the diagram. Outside the depletion region, $\phi(x)$ is constant, so the bands are flat.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Built-in Potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Contact potential at equilibrium | Volts (V) |
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | $\approx 26$ mV at 300 K | Volts (V) |
| Depletion Width (abrupt junction) | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$ | Total width of space-charge region | cm or µm |
| Depletion Width on p-side | $x_p = \dfrac{N_D}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W$ | Extent into p-side | cm or µm |
| Depletion Width on n-side | $x_n = \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W$ | Extent into n-side | cm or µm |
| Maximum Electric Field | $E_{max} = \dfrac{2 V_{bi}}{W}$ | Peak field at metallurgical junction | V/cm |
| Diode Current (Shockley) | $I = I_0 \!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right] = I_0 \!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$ | Forward/reverse current relation | Amperes |
| Reverse Saturation Current | $I_0 = qA \!\left(\dfrac{D_p n_i^2}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n n_i^2}{L_n N_A}\right)$ | Minority carrier diffusion current | Amperes |
| Dynamic Resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{V_T}{I_D}$ | Small-signal AC resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Junction Capacitance | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | Depletion (transition) capacitance | Farads (F) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When using absolute value bars in formulas, ALWAYS write them as `\vert V_R \vert` or `\mid V_R \mid` in your answer sheets to avoid formatting issues. In handwritten exams, use the standard absolute value notation $|V_R|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The p-n junction is the workhorse of virtually every modern electronic system:

- **Rectifiers** in power supplies (AC → DC conversion) — exploit the one-way current flow.
- **Solar Cells (Photovoltaics)** — operate in the **photovoltaic mode**, generating a voltage under illumination.
- **LEDs (Light-Emitting Diodes)** — forward bias recombines electrons and holes, releasing photons.
- **Zener Diodes** — heavily doped junctions operating in **reverse breakdown** as voltage regulators.
- **Transistors (BJTs, MOSFETs)** — use two back-to-back p-n junctions to amplify signals.
- **Integrated Circuits (ICs)** — billions of p-n junctions on a single chip form CPUs, memory, and logic gates.

The energy band diagram is the **universal language** engineers use to analyze, design, and predict the behavior of all these devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$

The built-in potential is the **electrostatic potential difference** between the n-side and p-side bulk regions at thermal equilibrium. We derive it from the requirement of a single, flat Fermi level.

### Step 1 — Write the electron concentration in terms of $E_F$

For a non-degenerate semiconductor, the electron concentration in the conduction band is:
$$n = N_C \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)$$

The hole concentration in the valence band is:
$$p = N_V \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_F - E_V}{kT}\right)$$

where $N_C$ and $N_V$ are the effective densities of states in the conduction and valence bands.

### Step 2 — Apply charge neutrality in the bulk regions

In the **n-type bulk** (far from junction), charge neutrality gives $n \approx N_D$:
$$N_D = N_C \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_C - E_F^{(n)}}{kT}\right)$$

Solving for $E_F^{(n)}$ relative to $E_C$:
$$E_C - E_F^{(n)} = kT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_C}{N_D}\right)$$

In the **p-type bulk**, $p \approx N_A$:
$$N_A = N_V \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_F^{(p)} - E_V}{kT}\right)$$

$$E_F^{(p)} - E_V = kT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_V}{N_A}\right)$$

### Step 3 — The total band bending at equilibrium

The total built-in potential energy $qV_{bi}$ equals the difference between $E_F^{(n)}$ and $E_F^{(p)}$ before contact:
$$qV_{bi} = (E_C - E_F^{(n)}) + (E_g - E_C + E_V) + (E_F^{(p)} - E_V)$$

After substituting the expressions and using $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp(-E_g / kT)$:

$$qV_{bi} = kT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_C N_V}{N_D N_A}\right) + E_g = kT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_C N_V \exp(-E_g/kT)}{N_D N_A}\right) \cdot e^{E_g/kT}$$

After simplification (this is the well-known KTU textbook derivation):

$$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

This is the **single most important formula** for p-n junction problems. The result is **physically intuitive**: higher doping → larger $V_{bi}$; larger bandgap (smaller $n_i$) → larger $V_{bi}$.

### Step 4 — Final form and evaluation

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

For a Si junction with $N_A = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ at 300 K:
$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{18} \cdot 10^{16}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln(4.44 \times 10^{14}) \approx 0.83 \text{ V}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Depletion Width $W$

Poisson's equation in the depletion region (assuming complete ionization and abrupt junction):

For $-x_p \le x \le 0$ (p-side):
$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_s}$$

For $0 \le x \le x_n$ (n-side):
$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

### Step 1 — Integrate with boundary conditions

Boundary conditions:
- $\phi(-x_p) = 0$ (reference potential on p-side bulk)
- $\phi(x_n) = V_{bi}$ (built-in potential on n-side)
- Electric field $E = -d\phi/dx$ is continuous at $x = 0$.

Integrating the first equation and using $E(-x_p) = 0$:
$$E(x) = -\frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_s}(x + x_p), \quad -x_p \le x \le 0$$

Integrating the second equation and using $E(x_n) = 0$:
$$E(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x_n - x), \quad 0 \le x \le x_n$$

### Step 2 — Apply charge neutrality

Total negative charge on p-side = Total positive charge on n-side:
$$N_A x_p = N_D x_n$$

Total depletion width:
$$W = x_p + x_n$$

From charge neutrality:
$$x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D} W, \quad x_n = \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} W$$

### Step 3 — Integrate the field to get the potential

$$V_{bi} = -\int_{-x_p}^{x_n} E(x) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} E_{max} W$$

where $E_{max} = \dfrac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \dfrac{q N_A x_p}{\varepsilon_s}$.

Combining:
$$V_{bi} = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W^2$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $W$

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}}$$

This is the **standard textbook result** used in all KTU derivations.

## 3.3 The Shockley Diode Equation — Derivation

Under forward bias, minority carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region **increase** above their equilibrium values. The excess minority carriers **diffuse** into the quasi-neutral regions, where they recombine. Integrating the diffusion equation (with boundary conditions) gives the total current:

### Step 1 — Boundary conditions for minority carriers

At the edge of the depletion region (x = $x_n$) in the n-side:
$$p_n(x_n) = p_{n0} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right)$$

The excess minority carrier concentration:
$$\Delta p_n(x_n) = p_{n0} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

Similarly at $x = -x_p$ in the p-side:
$$\Delta n_p(-x_p) = n_{p0} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

### Step 2 — Solve diffusion equations

The steady-state minority carrier diffusion in the n-region is governed by:
$$D_p \frac{d^2 \Delta p_n}{dx^2} - \frac{\Delta p_n}{\tau_p} = 0$$

Solution (assuming $\Delta p_n \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$):
$$\Delta p_n(x) = \Delta p_n(x_n) \exp\!\left(-\frac{x - x_n}{L_p}\right)$$

where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the diffusion length.

### Step 3 — Compute diffusion current at the junction edge

$$I_p(x_n) = -q A D_p \left.\frac{d(\Delta p_n)}{dx}\right|_{x = x_n} = \frac{q A D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

A similar term comes from the electron diffusion on the p-side. Combining:

$$I = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

where:
$$I_0 = qA\left(\frac{D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} + \frac{D_n}{L_n} n_{p0}\right) = qA\left(\frac{D_p n_i^2}{L_p N_D} + \frac{D_n n_i^2}{L_n N_A}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** For $V \gg V_T$ (i.e., $V > 0.1$ V), the $-1$ term is negligible, and $I \approx I_0 \exp(V/V_T)$. This means the diode current **increases exponentially** with forward voltage — a doubling of $V_T$ in voltage causes current to increase by a factor of $e \approx 2.718$.

## 3.4 Forward and Reverse Bias — Quantitative Summary

| **Condition** | **Applied Voltage** | **Barrier Potential** | **Depletion Width** | **Current** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium** | $V = 0$ | $V_{bi}$ (full barrier) | $W_0$ (natural) | $I = 0$ |
| **Forward Bias** | $V > 0$ (p-side positive) | $V_{bi} - V$ (reduced) | $W < W_0$ (narrows) | Large (exponential) |
| **Reverse Bias** | $V < 0$ (n-side positive) | $V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$ (enhanced) | $W > W_0$ (widens) | Tiny ($I \approx -I_0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Depletion Width Under Bias:** The width formula becomes $W(V) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$, where $V$ is **positive for forward** and **negative for reverse** bias. This unified form is what KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram of p-n Junction at Thermal Equilibrium

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional architecture** of band alignment at equilibrium, including the key features examiners expect to see in a labeled sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P["P-SIDE BULK"]
        EvP["EV_p (Valence Band - p side)"]
        EcP["EC_p (Conduction Band - p side)"]
        EfP["EF_p (Fermi Level - p side, near EV)"]
    end

    subgraph DR["DEPLETION REGION (Space-Charge)"]
        Efield["Built-in Electric Field E_built (n to p)"]
        NegativeIons["Negative Acceptor Ions (N_A^-)"]
        PositiveIons["Positive Donor Ions (N_D^+)"]
    end

    subgraph N["N-SIDE BULK"]
        EvN["EV_n (Valence Band - n side, lower)"]
        EcN["EC_n (Conduction Band - n side, higher)"]
        EfN["EF_n (Fermi Level - n side, near EC)"]
    end

    EvP --- EcP
    EvN --- EcN
    EfP -.->|"Aligns Flat at Equilibrium"| EfN
    EcP -->|"Bends Downward by qV_bi"| EcN
    EvP -->|"Bends Downward by qV_bi"| EvN
    NegativeIons --- PositiveIons
    Efield -->|"Opposes Diffusion"| DR
```

## 4.2 Forward Bias — Functional Flow Diagram

Under forward bias, the applied voltage **reduces** the built-in potential barrier, allowing massive carrier injection and current flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_FB["P-SIDE (Positive Terminal)"]
        HolesP["Majority Holes (+)"]
    end

    subgraph DR_FB["DEPLETION REGION (Narrowed)"]
        ReducedBarrier["Effective Barrier = V_bi - V_F (REDUCED)"]
        Recomb["Electron-Hole Recombination (Light Emission in LED)"]
    end

    subgraph N_FB["N-SIDE (Negative Terminal)"]
        ElectronsN["Majority Electrons (-)"]
    end

    VSource["DC Voltage Source V_F"] -->|Positive| P_FB
    VSource -->|Negative| N_FB
    HolesP -->|"Pushed toward junction"| ReducedBarrier
    ElectronsN -->|"Pushed toward junction"| ReducedBarrier
    ReducedBarrier --> Recomb
    Recomb -->|"Large Forward Current I_F (mA to A)"| ExternalCircuit["External Circuit"]
```

## 4.3 Reverse Bias — Functional Flow Diagram

Under reverse bias, the applied voltage **increases** the barrier, suppressing carrier diffusion and producing only a tiny leakage current.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_RB["P-SIDE (Negative Terminal)"]
        HolesP_R["Majority Holes (+), PULLED AWAY from junction"]
    end

    subgraph DR_RB["DEPLETION REGION (Widened)"]
        IncreasedBarrier["Effective Barrier = V_bi + |V_R| (INCREASED)"]
        NoFlow["No diffusion across junction"]
    end

    subgraph N_RB["N-SIDE (Positive Terminal)"]
        ElectronsN_R["Majority Electrons (-), PULLED AWAY from junction"]
    end

    VSource_R["DC Voltage Source V_R (Reverse)"] -->|Negative| P_RB
    VSource_R -->|Positive| N_RB
    HolesP_R -.->|"Drift away"| IncreasedBarrier
    ElectronsN_R -.->|"Drift away"| IncreasedBarrier
    NoFlow -->|"Tiny Reverse Saturation Current I_0 (nA to µA)"| ExternalCircuit_R["External Circuit"]
```

## 4.4 Carrier Flow and I-V Characteristic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ELEC["ELECTRICAL INPUTS"]
        VApp["Applied Voltage V (Volts)"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL REGIONS"]
        PBlock["P-Region: p ≈ N_A"]
        DRBlock["Depletion Region: Built-in E-field"]
        NBlock["N-Region: n ≈ N_D"]
    end

    subgraph CURR["CURRENT COMPONENTS"]
        I_Diff["Diffusion Current (Majority Carriers)"]
        I_Drift["Drift Current (Minority Carriers)"]
        I_Total["Net Current I = I_Diff - I_Drift"]
    end

    VApp -->|"Forward: V > 0"| PBlock
    VApp -->|"Reverse: V < 0"| NBlock
    PBlock --> I_Diff
    NBlock --> I_Diff
    DRBlock --> I_Drift
    I_Diff --> I_Total
    I_Drift --> I_Total
    I_Total -->|"Exponential in Forward, Constant in Reverse"| I_V_Plot["I-V Characteristic Curve"]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description of the I-V Characteristic (For KTU Sketches)

When examiners ask you to draw the I-V characteristic, the key reference points are:

- **Forward Region (V > 0):** Current rises **exponentially**. At $V \approx 0.7$ V (Si) or $V \approx 0.3$ V (Ge), the current becomes significant (turn-on voltage).
- **Reverse Region (V < 0):** Current is **constant** and equal to $-I_0$ (a few nA for Si).
- **Breakdown Region (V < $V_{BR}$):** At a large reverse voltage, the curve drops sharply downward — the junction breaks down (Zener or avalanche effect).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "depletion region" in a p-n junction. Explain how the built-in potential barrier is formed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **depletion region** (also called the space-charge region) is the narrow zone surrounding the metallurgical junction of a p-n diode that is **depleted of mobile charge carriers** (free electrons and holes). It contains only **immobile ionized dopant atoms** — positive donor ions ($N_D^+$) on the n-side and negative acceptor ions ($N_A^-$) on the p-side. **(1 Mark)**

When the p-n junction is formed, the concentration gradient causes **electrons to diffuse from n → p** and **holes to diffuse from p → n**. As these carriers cross the junction, they recombine, leaving behind the uncovered ionized dopants. The separated charges create a **built-in electric field** pointing from the n-side to the p-side, which opposes further diffusion. **(1 Mark)**

At equilibrium, the **drift current** (due to minority carriers) exactly balances the **diffusion current** (due to majority carriers), establishing a **constant Fermi level** across the device. The associated electrostatic potential difference is the **built-in potential $V_{bi}$**, given by $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between forward bias and reverse bias in a p-n junction diode. State the effect on the depletion region in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Forward Bias** | **Reverse Bias** |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity of p-side | Connected to **positive** terminal of battery | Connected to **negative** terminal of battery |
| Polarity of n-side | Connected to **negative** terminal | Connected to **positive** terminal |
| Barrier Potential | **Reduced** to $V_{bi} - V_F$ | **Increased** to $V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$ |
| Depletion Region | **Narrows** ($W < W_0$) | **Widens** ($W > W_0$) |
| Current | **Large** (exponential with V) | **Negligible** (≈ $-I_0$, a few nA) |
| Diode Behavior | Conducts (ON state) | Blocks (OFF state) |

In **forward bias**, the applied voltage opposes the built-in field, lowering the barrier, allowing majority carriers to cross the junction — leading to a large forward current. **(1 Mark)**

In **reverse bias**, the applied voltage aids the built-in field, widening the depletion region, and only a tiny drift current due to minority carriers flows. **(1 Mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to convert doping concentrations to cm⁻³ (NOT m⁻³). KTU answers **must** use cm⁻³.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Using $T = 273$ K (0°C) instead of **T = 300 K** (room temperature) unless explicitly stated. At 300 K, $V_T = 0.0259$ V.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Drawing the energy band diagram with the **Fermi level not flat** at equilibrium. This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Confusing the direction of current (conventional vs. electron flow). **Conventional current flows p → n inside the diode during forward bias.**
> - **Pitfall 5:** Not labeling $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $qV_{bi}$, $W$, $x_n$, $x_p$ in the band diagram. Each label = 0.5 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Sketch the energy band diagram of a p-n junction at thermal equilibrium and label the important features. Explain the physical origin of the band bending. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the operation of a p-n junction under (i) forward bias and (ii) reverse bias. Discuss the current-voltage characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Energy Band Diagram (Sketch Required — 3 Marks):**

A proper KTU diagram must show:
1. Conduction band $E_C$ bending **downward** on the p-side relative to the n-side.
2. Valence band $E_V$ bending in parallel with $E_C$ (parallel bending — bandgap is constant).
3. Fermi level $E_F$ drawn as a **single horizontal flat line** across the entire device.
4. Labels: $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $qV_{bi}$ (the band bending energy), $W$ (depletion width), $x_p$ and $x_n$.

**Physical Origin of Band Bending (4 Marks):**

At the instant of junction formation, the large concentration gradient drives:
- **Electrons** from n-side (high concentration) to p-side (low concentration).
- **Holes** from p-side (high concentration) to n-side (low concentration).

As electrons leave the n-side, they leave behind **uncovered positive donor ions ($N_D^+$)**, and as holes leave the p-side, they leave behind **uncovered negative acceptor ions ($N_A^-$)**. These fixed ions form the **space-charge (depletion) region**, which generates a **built-in electric field** $E_{built}$ pointing from n → p.

This field sets up an electrostatic potential $\phi(x)$ inside the depletion region. Since the conduction and valence band edges depend on the local potential as $E_C(x) = -q\phi(x) + \text{const}$, a non-zero $\phi(x)$ causes the bands to **bend**.

The total amount of bending is $qV_{bi}$, where $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$. At thermal equilibrium, the Fermi level becomes a constant across the junction (no net current flow), which geometrically requires the bands to bend by exactly this amount.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Forward Bias (3.5 Marks):**

When the **positive terminal** of an external battery is connected to the **p-side** and the **negative terminal** to the **n-side**, the diode is **forward biased**. The applied voltage $V_F$ **opposes** the built-in field.

**Effect on Energy Bands:**
- The effective barrier is reduced from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V_F$.
- The depletion width decreases: $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_F)}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$.
- The Fermi level on the p-side shifts **upward** by $qV_F$ relative to the n-side, splitting the previously flat $E_F$ into two quasi-Fermi levels $E_{F,p}$ and $E_{F,n}$.

**Effect on Current:**
- For $V_F > V_{bi}$, large numbers of majority carriers are injected across the junction.
- The current follows the **Shockley equation**: $I = I_0 \left[\exp(V_F / V_T) - 1\right] \approx I_0 \exp(V_F / V_T)$ for $V_F \gg V_T$.
- The current is **exponentially large** and the diode behaves like a **closed switch (ON state)**.

**Reverse Bias (3.5 Marks):**

When the polarity is reversed (positive to n-side, negative to p-side), the diode is **reverse biased**. The applied voltage $V_R$ **aids** the built-in field.

**Effect on Energy Bands:**
- The effective barrier increases to $V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$.
- The depletion width widens: $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert)}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$.
- The Fermi level on the n-side shifts **upward** by $q \vert V_R \vert$ relative to the p-side.

**Effect on Current:**
- The diffusion of majority carriers is **suppressed**.
- Only a tiny **drift current** due to minority carriers flows: $I \approx -I_0$ (a few nA for Si, a few µA for Ge).
- The diode behaves like an **open switch (OFF state)** until the breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$ is reached.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the built-in potential $V_{bi}$ of a p-n junction. Calculate $V_{bi}$ for a Si p-n junction with $N_A = 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ at 300 K. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the total depletion width $W$ of an abrupt p-n junction. A Si p-n junction has $N_A = 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, and $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V. Calculate the depletion width and the maximum electric field. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

At thermal equilibrium, the Fermi level $E_F$ must be **flat** across the junction (constant). This implies that the energy difference between $E_F$ and the band edges varies across the device.

In the **n-type bulk** (charge neutrality: $n \approx N_D$):
$$N_D = N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)$$
$$E_C - E_F = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

In the **p-type bulk** (charge neutrality: $p \approx N_A$):
$$N_A = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{kT}\right)$$
$$E_F - E_V = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

The total band bending at equilibrium is the **sum of the two Fermi-level offsets**:
$$qV_{bi} = (E_C - E_F)_n + (E_F - E_V)_p + E_g - E_g$$

Using $n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp(-E_g / kT)$ and rearranging:
$$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**

At $T = 300$ K, $V_T = kT/q = 0.0259$ V.

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{17} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{32}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{11})$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times 26.82 = 0.695 \text{ V}$$

**Answer: $V_{bi} \approx 0.695$ V** ✓

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Derivation of $W$ (4 Marks):**

Poisson's equation in the depletion region (with complete ionization):
$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q}{\varepsilon_s}\!\left[p - n + N_D^+ - N_A^-\right]$$

In the **depletion approximation** (n = p = 0 inside the depletion region), and using the boundary conditions $E(-x_p) = 0$ and $E(x_n) = 0$, integration gives a **triangular electric field profile** with peak $E_{max}$ at $x = 0$.

Charge neutrality: $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$, so $W = x_p + x_n = x_n (1 + N_D/N_A)$.

The potential difference across the depletion region equals the area under the $E$-$x$ triangle:
$$V_{bi} = \frac{1}{2} E_{max} W$$

Since $E_{max} = q N_D x_n / \varepsilon_s = q N_A x_p / \varepsilon_s$, we obtain:
$$V_{bi} = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D} \cdot W^2$$

Solving for $W$:
$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V, $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm $= 1.035 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm.

**Step 1:** Compute the doping factor.
$$\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D} = \frac{10^{18} + 10^{16}}{10^{18} \times 10^{16}} = \frac{1.01 \times 10^{18}}{10^{34}} = 1.01 \times 10^{-16} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the $W$ formula.
$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.75}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.5525 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{9.703 \times 10^{6} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-16}} = \sqrt{9.80 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$W = 3.13 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.313 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $E_{max}$.
$$E_{max} = \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.75}{3.13 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.79 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/cm}$$

**Answer: $W \approx 0.313$ µm, $E_{max} \approx 4.79 \times 10^4$ V/cm** ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This is your **high-density rapid-revision checklist** for last-minute KTU exam prep:

- **p-n Junction Formation:** Formed by joining p-type and n-type semiconductors. Carrier diffusion creates a depletion region with a built-in electric field.

- **Energy Band Diagram at Equilibrium:** $E_C$ and $E_V$ bend by an amount $qV_{bi}$, with the Fermi level $E_F$ remaining **flat and constant** across the entire device.

- **Built-in Potential Formula:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — increases with doping and with bandgap.

- **Depletion Width Formula:** $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s V_{bi} / q) \cdot (N_A + N_D) / (N_A N_D)}$ — extends more into the **lightly doped side**.

- **Charge Neutrality in Depletion:** $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$ — fundamental constraint used in all derivations.

- **Forward Bias (V > 0):** Barrier reduced to $V_{bi} - V_F$; depletion width **narrows**; large **exponential** forward current flows.

- **Reverse Bias (V < 0):** Barrier increased to $V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$; depletion width **widens**; only tiny reverse saturation current $I_0$ flows.

- **Shockley Diode Equation:** $I = I_0 \left[\exp(V / V_T) - 1\right]$ — universal I-V relation; at 300 K, $V_T \approx 26$ mV.

- **Sign Convention:** Conventional current flows from **p to n inside the diode** during forward bias; **electron flow is opposite** to conventional current.

- **Real-World Applications:** Rectifiers, solar cells, LEDs, Zener diodes, transistors, ICs — the p-n junction is the **fundamental unit of all modern semiconductor devices**.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never draw a band diagram at equilibrium with a **sloped Fermi level**. A sloped $E_F$ implies **non-equilibrium** (current flow), which is only the case under bias.

- **Units to Memorize:** $N_A, N_D, n_i$ in **cm⁻³**; $W$ in **cm or µm**; $V_T$ in **V** (≈ 0.026 V); $\varepsilon_s$ in **F/cm**; $E_{max}$ in **V/cm**.

- **Forgetting to Label:** Every KTU energy band diagram MUST have $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $qV_{bi}$, $W$, and (for biased diagrams) $V_F$ or $V_R$ explicitly marked. Missing labels = lost marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diode Equation (Derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diode Equation (Derivation) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diode Equation (Shockley Diode Equation):** The fundamental current–voltage relationship that quantitatively describes the electrical behavior of a p–n junction diode under forward and reverse bias conditions. Mathematically, the diode equation is expressed as: $$I = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V}{\eta V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$ where $I$ is the diode current, $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current, $V$ is the applied voltage across the junction, $\eta$ is the **ideality factor** (typically between **1** and **2**), and $V_T$ is the **thermal voltage**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Dam and Reservoir" Analogy:** Imagine a p–n junction as a water dam separating two reservoirs of different water levels (representing the n-side and p-side with their respective majority carriers — electrons and holes). The height of the dam represents the **built-in potential barrier** ($V_{bi}$). - In **equilibrium** (no external voltage), the dam holds steady — diffusion current of electrons trying to cross from n to p is exactly cancelled by drift current due to the built-in field. - When you **forward bias** the junction, you lower the dam wall externally. Massive numbers of carriers (electrons from n-side, holes from p-side) rush across — this is the **large forward current**. - When you **reverse bias** it, you raise the dam wall higher. Only a tiny trickle of minority carriers (thermally generated) flows — this is the **reverse saturation current** $I_0$. - The exponential term $e^{V/\eta V_T}$ captures how drastically the carrier populations change at the depletion edges as you adjust the dam (voltage). > [!TIP] > **Why exponential?** Carrier concentration at the depletion edge follows the **Boltzmann distribution**. Voltage modifies the barrier height linearly, and Boltzmann's law converts that linear change into an *exponential* change in carrier density. This is the deep physical reason behind the diode's iconic exponential I–V curve. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Exam) | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (at 300 K) | |--------|----------|---------------------------| | $q$ | Electronic charge | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$ | | $k$ | Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$ | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | **300 K** (room temperature) | | $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage | $\mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ (≈ **26 mV**) | | $n_i$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration (Si) | $\mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Step-by-Step Logic The derivation of the diode equation rests on **four foundational pillars** of semiconductor physics: 1. **The Depletion Approximation:** All space charge and built-in field are confined to a narrow depletion region; outside this region, the semiconductor is charge-neutral and field-free. 2. **Boltzmann's Relation for Quasi-Equilibrium:** Under low-level injection, the carrier concentrations at the depletion edges are modified by the applied bias via the Boltzmann factor $e^{V/V_T}$. 3. **Continuity Equation for Minority Carriers:** Governs how injected excess minority carriers recombine as they diffuse away from the junction. 4. **Diffusion Current Mechanism:** In a p–n diode, the current is carried *entirely by minority carrier diffusion* in the quasi-neutral regions — the drift current in those regions is zero (no field). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The diode equation is **not derived from Ohm's law** — it is the result of solving the **diffusion equation** for minority carriers with bias-dependent boundary conditions. Marks are frequently lost when students write "I = V/R" anywhere in a diode derivation. ## 2.2 Boundary Conditions and Assumptions - **Low-level injection:** Excess minority carrier concentration ≪ equilibrium majority carrier concentration. This justifies $\eta = 1$ (ideal diode). - **No generation–recombination in depletion region:** All recombination occurs in the neutral regions via minority carrier diffusion. - **Quasi-neutral regions (p-side and n-side) are field-free**, so transport is purely diffusive. - **Steady-state:** $\partial / \partial t = 0$. ## 2.3 The Minority Carrier Picture (the heart of the derivation) When forward bias $V$ is applied, the minority carrier concentrations at the **edges of the depletion region** become: $$n_p(x = -x_p) = n_{p0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_T}\right)$$ $$p_n(x = +x_n) = p_{n0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_T}\right)$$ Here, $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ (electrons in p-region at equilibrium) and $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ (holes in n-region at equilibrium). The **excess** minority carrier concentrations are $\Delta n_p = n_p - n_{p0}$ and $\Delta p_n = p_n - p_{n0}$. Far from the junction, they decay back to zero via recombination. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Print or memorize this table** — it covers every equation you may need in a KTU derivation or numerical on the diode equation. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---------|----------------| | 1 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | | 2 | $n_{p0} = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_A}, \quad p_{n0} = \dfrac{n_i^2}{N_D}$ | Equilibrium minority carrier densities | | 3 | $n_p(-x_p) = n_{p0} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_T}\right)$ | Electron density at p-side depletion edge (forward bias) | | 4 | $p_n(x_n) = p_{n0} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_T}\right)$ | Hole density at n-side depletion edge (forward bias) | | 5 | $D_n \dfrac{d^2 \Delta n_p}{dx^2} - \dfrac{\Delta n_p}{\tau_n} = 0$ | Steady-state minority electron diffusion equation in p-region | | 6 | $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}, \quad L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ | Minority carrier diffusion lengths | | 7 | $\Delta n_p(x) = \Delta n_p(0) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{L_n}\right)$ | Excess electron profile in p-side | | 8 | $I_{n,\text{diff}} = q A D_n \dfrac{d \Delta n_p}{dx} \bigg\vert_{x=0}$ | Electron diffusion current at junction edge | | 9 | $I_0 = q A \left( \dfrac{D_n \, n_{p0}}{L_n} + \dfrac{D_p \, p_{n0}}{L_p} \right)$ | Reverse saturation current | | 10 | $I = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V}{\eta V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$ | **Shockley Diode Equation (Final Result)** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** The formula $I = I_0 [e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1]$ is for **forward bias** convention. Some texts define current direction oppositely.
